#### service, devotional service, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KTHudZWO2Ac?t=1812
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Desiring to serve Krishna in some big way, it is not that it is not devotional service."
**Summary:** In response to a question, the speaker clarifies that desiring to serve Krishna in a big way is still devotional service, but it's not the highest order, which involves serving Krishna according to His desire.

A devotee is denying some you said that if a devotee is denying some services, discriminating the services, and in the future he may become a pure devotee, you mentioned. So how does the devotee who denies, discriminates the services, becomes a pure devotee? I didn't get the question. What was that? You said in the class, if a devotee is denying the menial services, want to do some big services, so in the future if he becomes a pure devotee also, he will become Devata in the material world. So how does a devotee who denies the, discriminates the services, and he can become a pure devotee? Well, the point is that, desiring to serve Krishna, in any way, is also devotional service only. It's not that it is not devotional service. If somebody desires, I should serve Krishna in some big way, it is not out of the ambit of devotional service. Because of one's past conditioning, one may have that ambition to do something big. So you, you dovetail that desire in doing something big for Krishna. Nothing wrong in that. But then, one has to go beyond even that. One has to, one has to go to a stage, where one will be simply interested in, serving Krishna's desires, whether as a success or a failure. So, such a devotee, when he becomes completely liberated, he will go to the supreme abode. He will go to the Goloka Vrindavan. So that is the point. So, desiring to serve Krishna in some big way, it is not that it is not devotional service. So that is also devotional service only, but it is not devotional service of the highest order. The main point is, when, when, higher than that is, to desire to serve Krishna according to his desire. That is more, that is even higher than desiring to serve Krishna in some big way. Is that okay? Any other?

---
#### self-realization, satisfaction, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KTHudZWO2Ac?t=1941
**Verse References:** BG 3.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By serving Krishna, everything else is performed."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of 'taking pleasure in the self' (Atmarathi, Atmatruthah, and Atmanyeva Santoshra) as understanding oneself as a spirit soul, engaging in spiritual pursuits, and finding complete satisfaction in serving Krishna.

Yes, please. Taking pleasure in the self, what it actually means. When we say taking pleasure only in the self, what it means is that, one, like Prabhupada is mentioning here, in the purport to that 17th verse, I will read that. Actually, those three things, that Prabhupada mentions, Krishna mentions, Atmarathi, Atmatruthah, and Atmanyeva Santoshra. These three are connected. So, Atmarathi means, a person, when a person engages in, when a, when a person, Atmarathi means one who takes pleasure only in the self. A person who is fully Krishna conscious, and is fully satisfied by his acts in Krishna consciousness, no longer has any duty to perform. Due to his being Krishna conscious, all impiety within is instantly cleansed, and effect of many many thousands of Vijnaya performances. So actually Atmarathi means, a person who understands that, he is not the body, he is a spirit soul. So he is interested only in spiritual subject matter. He is not interested in satisfying his senses. Right? So he is not interested in searching after the sense objects. He is more interested in understanding, he is more interested in understanding about himself, in spiritual subject matter. So such a person is said to be, taking pleasure only in the self. And, when one, engages in, in devotional service to Krishna, with that understanding, that he wants to, with that, keeping away the desire for sense gratification, then what happens is, by such clearing of consciousness, one becomes fully confident of his eternal position, in relationship with the Supreme. His duty thus becomes self-illuminated. So, Atma-tripta, Srila Prabhupada is translating, Atma-tripta as, one who is illumined in the self. So illumined in the self meaning, when one is engaged in understanding, when one is engaged in spiritual pursuit, and one engages in service to Krishna, one understands that, Oh! I am a servant of Krishna. This is my true position. So, such a position is called, self-illuminated, means he understands that he is a servant of Krishna, he is a part and parcel of Krishna, and he has to serve Krishna. And such a person, Atma-santushtaha, he is satisfied with the self only. Meaning, he is fully satiated, meaning he is satisfied, so by, first is he, he takes, he does activities only, related to spiritual subject matter. He is not interested in sense gratification. That is Atmarathi. And as an Atmarathi, when one understands that I am, I have to serve Krishna, I am a part and parcel of Krishna, then he becomes self-illuminated. Atma-tripta. And when in, when, when on that platform of understanding, I am part and parcel of Krishna, one is completely satisfied by serving Krishna. Oh! I am part and parcel of Krishna, I do service for Krishna, I am fully satisfied. He doesn't, he has come to a final conclusion. He is no more searching. There is no more search. What am I supposed to do? What are my duties? Nothing. This is the final thing. By serving Krishna, everything else is performed. So that is the level of satisfaction that a person feels. So then, that person is called an Atma-santushta. So that is a person who is satiated only in, only with the Self. So one is taking pleasure in the Self, one is illumined in the Self, and finally, one is satisfied with the Self. So this is, these are the three things. It's a progressive state.

### AAQ: Can Only Full-Time Devotees Become fully Krishna Conscious? | HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### Krishna consciousness, devotional service, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jsHhWWbyW4E?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "No matter what condition we are in, we can become Krishna conscious."
**Summary:** The speaker asserts that Krishna consciousness is possible for everyone, regardless of their material circumstances, using Bhaktivinoda Thakur as an example.

This is the question: In Kali Yuga, is only full-time devotee is possible for Krishna consciousness till death, where we can remember Krishna at the time of death? That's not true. The point is that if we look at the example of great Acharyas like Bhaktivinoda Thakur, he was engaged in government service, but still he was Krishna conscious. Of course, he is a pure devotee, he is a great Acharya, we cannot compare with our own condition. But Bhaktivinoda Thakur by his example, he established that no matter what condition we are in, we can become Krishna conscious. We can remain in pure devotional service irrespective of what material engagement we have.

---
#### surrender, devotional service, activity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jsHhWWbyW4E?t=55
**Verse References:** BG 9.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whatever we do, we have to surrender to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna instructs in the Bhagavad Gita that all actions should be surrendered to Him, and that one's material situation does not necessitate giving up devotional service.

So Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita, yad karośi, yad aśnāsi, yad juhośi, dadāsi, yad tapasya sikaunte, yad kurusva madharpanam, whatever we do, we have to surrender to Krishna. And that's why we have to give up our devotional service. Sthane sthitaha. Sthane sthitaha, wherever whatever material situation we are in, we need not give it up. If we are able to give it up and dedicate ourselves fully to our spiritual master's mission, that's the best thing, definitely. But if due to some commitments, some considerations, some opposition, we are unable to dedicate ourselves, still in whatever condition we have, it's not possible to give up. So we are not able to give up our devotional service, we are not able to give up our spiritual service, we are in, we can render devotion and service to Krishna, we can remain Krishna conscious and at the time of death by the mercy of the Guru, by the mercy of a pure devotee, spiritual master which is Srila Prabhupada, by his mercy we will be able to remember Krishna and go back to Godhead. So remembering Krishna at the time of death and going back to Godhead is not dependent upon my status. My status as a full time devotee, congregation devotee, folk devotee, Grihastha, Brahmachari, Sanyasi, these are all material considerations, material situations. According to our body, according to the circumstances we may take up a certain status or position in life because everyone has to have some, the material world is such that you have to have some status. Either as a man or as a woman. Either as a married person or as a bachelor or as a full time devotee or working outside. You have to do something.

---
#### activity, karma, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jsHhWWbyW4E?t=179
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we start attributing the result to ourselves, then you are on the material platform."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that activity is inherent to the soul, and the key is to avoid attributing the results of our actions to ourselves, which keeps us on the material platform.

Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita that the nature of the living entity is to be active. And therefore Krishna says, you cannot remain inactive because if you are inactive you cannot even maintain your body. So activity is the characteristic of the soul, soul cannot remain inactive. Now in what capacity he acts in the material world, as a Brahmachari, as a Grihastha, as a folk boy, as a congregation devotee, as a full time missionary, whatever activities you do in whatever condition, you have to situate yourself somewhere and carry out some activity. You cannot remain inactive. The point is, what do we do with the result of those activities? If I am a full time missionary, then I cannot remain inactive. If I am a full time missionary, but I am thinking, oh this activity I am doing because of this so much this one result is coming is all because I have worked hard for it. I worked hard, therefore so much donation came to the temple. I worked hard, so many people became devotees of Krishna. When we start attributing the result to ourselves, then you are on the material platform. So, to whom do we attribute the result? No matter in what condition we are in, no matter what status we are in, no matter what is our field of activity, that actually determines whether we receive the mercy of Guru and Krishna and we are able to remember Krishna at the time of death and we are able to become free from the cycle of birth and death and go back to Krishna Loka or not. So it's not a matter of being a full time devotee or not, it's a matter of receiving the mercy of Guru and Krishna.

---
#### mercy, consciousness, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jsHhWWbyW4E?t=291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimately it is the consciousness of that individual which determines whether it goes back to Godhead or not."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes that while dedicating oneself fully to Krishna's mission is advantageous, ultimately it is one's consciousness that determines their spiritual destination.

Guru is Srila Prabhupada and Krishna himself. And if we have the opportunity and the chance to dedicate ourselves to Krishna, our life, to Prabhupada's mission, to our Guru Maharaj's mission, then there is a very high degree of chance that every activity that we do, we will be able to surrender the result to Krishna. So the situation becomes more favorable when we are a full time devotee, but ultimately it is the consciousness of that individual which determines whether it goes back to Godhead or not. So this is the fundamental point which we have to always keep in mind. Otherwise, being a full time missionary, yes, is more definitely far more conducive to be able to practice Krishna consciousness and to be able to come to the point of remembering Krishna all the time, which will help us to remember Krishna even at the time of death and take us back to Godhead. Okay.

---

### AAQ: Does Modern Science Believe in Reincarnation  |  His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### science, reincarnation, belief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XmSILnukHO8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "It's not that if you are a believer in science, you cannot be a believer in God. It's not like that. Because both are different branches of knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of whether modern science accepts reincarnation, stating that opinions vary among scientists and that belief in science does not preclude belief in God.

I believe in reincarnation. Does modern science accept it or not? Modern science, well, it ranges from 0 to 100. There are people in science who don't believe in reincarnation. There are people who believe somewhere in between. There are people who believe totally in reincarnation. So we have all kinds of people. It's not that if you are a believer in science, you cannot be a believer in God. It's not like that. Because both are different branches of knowledge. It's like asking, does someone who believes in physics really believe in mathematics? Or someone who believes in mathematics really believe in biology? Both are different branches of knowledge. So you cannot compare both of them. But yes, there are people who believe in God. There are people who believe in science who don't believe in reincarnation, don't believe in the existence of soul, etc. And there are many scientists, Nobel laureates, those who have achieved a lot in the field of chemistry, science, physics, etc. There are many people like that who believe that there must be a higher being behind this creation. There must be a higher intelligence behind this creation.

---
#### material energy, spiritual energy, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XmSILnukHO8?t=112
**Verse References:** BG 7.4, BG 7.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And all the living entities are trying to control the material energy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the Vaishnava understanding of material and spiritual energy based on the Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing the living entity's attempt to control material energy, and contrasting this with the scientific view.

So there are all kinds of people. So we go by, see, the point is that science deals with matter, material energy. According to the understanding of the Bhagavad Gita, apareyam itas tv anyam prakritim viddhi me param, Krishna says, first of all, there is, first of all, Krishna says, there is one energy of his, which is called the differentiated energy or the interior energy. And this interior energy of Krishna, it consists of earth, water, fire, air, space, mind, intelligence and falsity. These eight elements constitute the interior energy of Krishna. Apareyam itas tv anyam prakritim viddhi me param jiva-bhutam maha-baho yayedam dharyate jagat. And then Krishna says, the entire material energy that we see comprises different combinations of these eight elements. Whether you look at a human body, animal body, tree body, earth, planets, different planets, all of them comprise these fundamental eight elements. In varying proportions, varying forms, etc. But Krishna says, beyond this, there is a higher energy, superior energy of mine, which is called the living entity, jiva-bhutam yayedam dharyate jagat. And all the living entities are trying to control the material energy. You see how Krishna has beautifully put it. If you analyze this material, entire material manifestation also, you have matter, which is what scientists are all studying. Then there is the scientist who studies that matter, who wants to control all that matter by studying it. There are two distinct things, isn't it? One is matter itself. Then there is the person who is trying to harness material energy, control it, use it according to his purposes.

---

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vaishnava view of a separate living force with the scientific view that consciousness arises from material interactions, using the example of AI development to illustrate the point.

So Krishna brings this distinction out. And he says, both of them are different energies. Even the living entity is also energy of Krishna. Many scientists, of course, they do not accept that living force is a separate energy. They say that no, living force is also part of the material energy. Some point of time, when the chemicals mix together, consciousness is produced. That's all. And so that's why they are saying very quickly, we will produce sentient AI. And once we are there, we have produced life. Okay. Sentient AI already to some extent, they have already done. If you look at, if you go to Google, there is an option, Google Lens. You take a picture of something and put it into Google. It will search and bring out all the information related to it. Just the other day, somebody was asking us, if you see next to our compound wall, there are a lot of trees. There are those big, big fruits coming from the tree hanging down. So somebody asked, there must be some use for this fruit. So much of it is hanging down. Why are you not using it? So I immediately pulled out my mobile, took a picture and searched and found out that the fruit is poisonous. So you cannot use it. So I said, see, it is here. So you see, vision, form, identifying form. Our sight is what? Our power of sight is to identify forms and to be able to discriminate and determine. Okay. This form belongs to that of a speaker. This is the form of a fan. This is the form of a TV. We see the form and identify what the object is, what its functions are. Right. Similarly, AI is doing that. You capture a photograph. It will give you all information, what it is all about. So power of sight is already there. Right. And power of sound is already there. Now, latest thing is you hum a tune. Some song is running in your mind. You are remembering the tune, but you have forgotten the song. You hum a tune into your mobile and search, it will search out that song and tell you. I don't know whether you have used that feature. So even sound, it is able to, and also we are able to see that Siri and in Google also, there is one lady. You speak to her and tell, call this person. It will immediately, she will immediately call. You ask for directions, she will show from where to where, how to go. So sound also is recognizing. And you can dictate. Now it has become part of all softwares. Word, MS word you open, olden days we had to sit and type. Now we are all from that dinosaur age. So we are, you know, automatically tuned to open a word file and start typing. But now you don't have to do that. Open a word file and start dictating. Whichever language you want. You dictate in Tamil, it will type in Tamil. You dictate in Hindi, it will type in Hindi. You dictate in English, it will type in English. So sound also it has got recognized. So AI, now what is pending in AI?

So you see people get carried away by this kind of imitations after all ultimately what is the experience it is imitating mimicking human intelligence that's all know so we are using our human intelligence and mimicking imitating original intelligence so AI is created you did not come up with that that is no off so we all gotta communicate Napoleon about it that is looping in the air modifications is created by us it is created by an intelligent human being so to create an imitation you need a living person so how can you say that the original has come into existence on its own that it has come about just by a combination of chemicals so ai also you you know allow chemicals to combine and form ai why it is not happening it will not happen so you see anything that we have to create requires intelligence creating some meaningful object cannot happen without intelligence some by chance some chemicals came together and created a beautiful object not possible so meaningful objects can be created only when you have a person behind it and that person cannot be created by a combination of chemicals so the person is beyond the ordinary or intelligent physical form called material energy. #### material energy, spiritual energy, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XmSILnukHO8?t=667
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There is a distinct difference between a living person and dead matter."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the distinction between material and spiritual energy, emphasizing that spiritual energy is superior due to its controlling tendency and consciousness, which material energy lacks.

One is material energy, another is spiritual energy. Spiritual energy is superior because it is trying to control and manipulate the inferior material energy. Why is one called the inferior and the other called superior? Because the superior energy has got the controlling tendency and manipulating tendency, whereas the other energy does not have. The spiritual energy, the superior energy has consciousness, therefore it is superior. Material energy cannot possess consciousness at any point of time, therefore it is inferior. So this is how we have to see distinctly, you know, any person with common sense can make out this distinction. There is a distinct difference between a living person and dead matter. And that is why very simple example, as long as we are alive, we look at each other and call each other by names. We go to a hospital, they will ask for the medical record and they will say so and so person is getting treated here and all that. And suppose a person goes to the hospital and he dies in the hospital. What happens? The doctor will come and tell, please take the dead body and go. They will not tell the name of the person because no more, that person is no more, that person is gone. Only body has been left behind. Very simple, no, what is the difference between a living person and a dead body? It is the person who is there within the body. So it's very common sense. It doesn't require much, very great intelligence to understand this.

#### illusion, materialism, science
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XmSILnukHO8?t=770
**Verse References:** BG 7.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Their knowledge has been snatched away by the glamour, the glitter of material energy."
**Summary:** The speaker criticizes scientists who, despite their intelligence, fail to recognize the simple truth of the soul's existence due to being deluded by the allure of material energy and scientific progress.

But unfortunately, scientists, although they are apparently very intelligent, they are not able to see even this simple truth. That is why Krishna refers to them in the Bhagavad Gita as mayayapahrta-jnana. Their knowledge has been snatched away by the glamour, the glitter of material energy. They will invent one AI and they will say, we are almost there. We are going to create sentient air. We have, we have figured out how to create light. And they go on like this, like this. They will, you know, they will die. Their sons will come. They will also die. Their grandchildren will come. They will also die. Each of them will keep telling the people of their generation. We are almost there. We have figured it out. Don't worry. And life will go on. Death will go on. Business of science will also go on like this. So we should not get carried away by the glitter of the material energy. That's the whole thing. If you are carried away by the glitter of material energy, how much science and technology has had to do with it, how much science has advanced, what all discoveries have happened. So when you look at all those things, yes, we will be able to figure out what are the chemicals bringing about consciousness and life. This is how maya covers the intelligence of the living entity. This is called the avarana atmika shakti of maya. Material energy, although it is inferior, it has the ability to blind us with her glitter, promising something in the future. In the future, we are going to find out. In the future, we are going to be able to create, recreate life. So these kinds of false assurances are given to us by maya which covers our intelligence and we are unable to see the simple stark reality.

---
#### reincarnation, belief, intelligence

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Amongst the scientists, those who are mayayapahrta-jnana, their intelligence has been stolen away by maya, will not be able to see how reincarnation is happening."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating that belief in reincarnation depends on whether one's intelligence is covered by illusion, and notes the diversity of beliefs among scientists, some of whom acknowledge a higher intelligence.

This is what the Vedic scriptures are pointing out. So it doesn't matter. Amongst the scientists, those who are mayayapahrta-jnana, their intelligence has been stolen away by maya, will not be able to see how reincarnation is happening. Whereas those who are not completely bewildered by that illusory energy will be able to see the truth. That's why among scientists, you will see some of them are definitely, they believe in God. There are many others who don't believe in God. So many scientists will say, religion is all not required. We should remove religion. We should remove religion. We should remove religion. We should remove religion. We should remove religion. We should remove religion. And there in his row, one scientist will be releasing one satellite and before that he'll be going and doing one Ganesha puja. So you have both varieties. Because ultimately human beings, human beings, some people whose intelligence is not so much covered will be able to see that beyond our efforts and beyond our intelligence, there is a higher intelligence and we are dependent on that. So that's how things are.

### AAQ: How do we not Overthink, Over Analyze | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### faith, knowledge, modern skepticism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kmO-HeLRBHw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "To acquire spiritual knowledge, you need faith."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the question of how to avoid overthinking and overanalyzing, emphasizing the importance of faith in acquiring knowledge, particularly spiritual knowledge, contrasting it with the modern skeptical view.

Interesting question. How do we not overthink or overanalyze? How to avoid overthinking and overanalyzing? Well, if we look at the Vedic scriptures, if we look at the Bhagavad Gita, faith is given a very important role to play in the acquisition of knowledge. Now, of course, in the modern day, faith is treated as a very contemptuous thing. All religions are all fakes. Leave it aside. Science is real knowledge. That's the way it is presented.

---
#### faith, science, spiritual knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kmO-HeLRBHw?t=56
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Faith is common for any acquisition of knowledge, whether biology, botany, chemistry, physics, mathematics, any branch of knowledge if you want to acquire, faith is common."
**Summary:** The speaker argues that faith is essential not only for spiritual knowledge but also for scientific knowledge, using the example of accepting scientific facts from textbooks without personal verification.

It is true that to acquire spiritual knowledge, you need faith. Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita that ashraddha dana, those who are unfaithful, those who do not have faith, they will have neither peace in this life nor in the next one. So to be able to acquire spiritual knowledge, faith is very important. But then to acquire science also faith is required. All of us, we learn science. How? By going to the school and college, sitting in a classroom and studying the textbook. Whatever the textbook is teaching, we accept it as truth. We say to water, consciousness, there are two molecules of water. All of us have done it. How many of you have actually taken out water and analyzed how many atoms of hydrogen, how many atoms of oxygen are there? How many of you have done it? None of us have done it. True, no? But if anybody asks any one of us, what is the composition of water? What is the chemical composition of water? H2O. Immediately answer will come. Where did you get this H2O from? What is hydrogen in the first place? What is oxygen? Oxygen, can you separate it out and show it to me? If you can you show it to me, where is oxygen? Show me. Can you show me? Not possible. Can you show me hydrogen? Not possible. Can you show me God? Not possible. So can you show me God? I cannot show you God because you cannot see. So God does not exist. You cannot show me hydrogen, how should I accept that hydrogen exists? You are telling periodic table you are showing and all those things. Very fine. All right. That table anybody can make. Even a child can sit and make. So just because you made it and you put it in the textbook, I have to accept. So when you teach from a book, we have to accept it blindly. I have faith. This is a science book. Accept it. That's all. When it comes to same Bhagavad Gita, that is also a book. No, no, no, it is faith. Faith is common for any acquisition of knowledge, whether biology, botany, chemistry, physics, mathematics, any branch of knowledge if you want to acquire, faith is common.

---
#### faith, authorized source, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kmO-HeLRBHw?t=194
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Faith in an authorized source of knowledge is essential for acquiring knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that it's not blind faith, but faith in an authorized source of knowledge, drawing a parallel between trusting science books from authorized institutions and trusting spiritual texts.

But not blind faith. Faith in an authorized source of that knowledge. Why we have faith in the science books? Because the science books are being taught in an authorized college, by an authorized professor. We are not going to some sabjeevala and learning what is the components of water, what is the chemical composition of water. We are not going to some street vendor who is illiterate and learning from him, right? We are going to an authorized college and authorized professor and authorized textbooks we are studying and we are learning from that. So that faith in an authorized source of knowledge is essential for acquiring knowledge. So that faith in an authorized source of knowledge is essential for acquiring knowledge. Any branch of knowledge without that faith, fundamental faith, nobody can acquire any knowledge. This is the reality.

---
#### spiritual knowledge, authorized scriptures, validation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kmO-HeLRBHw?t=260
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Same principle works even for spiritual knowledge also."
**Summary:** The speaker extends the analogy, stating that for spiritual knowledge, authorized scriptures like Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam are the textbooks, and applying the principles in life is the experiment to validate the knowledge.

So, similarly, in for acquiring spiritual knowledge also, you have to have faith in the authorized source of that spiritual knowledge. So, just like we have textbooks which are prescribed by the university which are called authorized sources of knowledge, material knowledge, but similarly, for acquiring spiritual knowledge, the authorized books are Bhagavad Gita, Srimad Bhagavatam and other Vedic scriptures. So if we acquire knowledge from them, we will be able to, we will, if we have faith in those authorized books of spiritual knowledge, we will be able to acquire spiritual knowledge. And just like in the science books, first you have faith in the college and the teacher and the textbook, go and sit in the classroom, hear the theory, then you go to the lab and conduct the experiment. You will be able to see for yourself how the theory is correct, yes or no? But for you to conduct that experiment also, first you should have faith that yes, college is authorized, textbook is authorized, this knowledge being taught to me is authorized. If you have no faith, hey, this is all bogus, yeah, you will never acquire any knowledge. So with that faith, you first go and learn the theory. Then you go to the lab and do the practical experiments. Then whatever you learned in theory gets validated, our faith in that source of knowledge becomes stronger. This is the phenomenon which is working in science also. Same thing, same principle works even for spiritual knowledge also.

---

**Key Quote:** "In the case of spiritual knowledge, the laboratory is yours."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on applying spiritual principles in one's life as a means of validating the knowledge from scriptures, strengthening faith.

You have faith in the authorized scriptures like Bhagavad Gita, and you study that and apply those principles in your life, you will be able to see the results which are predicted. In the end, you will be able to see the results which are predicted. In the end, you will be able to see the results which are predicted in the Bhagavad Gita and in the Vedic scriptures. And if you do that, so what happens? You are validating, you are applying the principles, practically you are applying them in the laboratory of your life. In the, to study science, the laboratory for you is some classroom within the college or school. In the case of spiritual knowledge, the laboratory is yours. You apply, you experiment. By applying those spiritual principles in your life, you will be able to see the results. You are validating, you are doing studies according to those spiritual principles in your life, and you validate whatever is told in the Vedic scriptures, that way your faith grows. So, when we speak of any branch of knowledge, we need to have faith in that branch of knowledge. Without that, we cannot acquire any system of knowledge. So, when we speak of overthinking or over Ωrthinging, then it is not worth knowing the knowledge at all. **Summary:** The speaker directly addresses the initial question, stating that overthinking stems from a lack of faith in the source of knowledge, and emphasizes the importance of determining the authenticity of the source before accepting its teachings.

Analyzing what it simply means is we are not having sufficient faith in the source of knowledge, so if you have faith in that source of knowledge if you have done a little bit of analysis and determined for yourself that yes i accept that this is a bona fide source of knowledge then you don't after that analyze whether this source what the source is saying is correct or not you accept that whatever is coming from the thoughts is correct so then we have we become free from overthinking and over analyzing but if all our life we keep questioning the source then we will never be able to acquire knowledge same thing you go to college first you determine college has certain registration accreditation it has got a recognition you analyze all those things and then you look at who are the professors what are their qualifications and how many years they have been teaching so you analyze all this and come to a conclusion yes this college is authorized after that you get into the college admit yourself in the college then whatever they teach you have to accept this accept it as school right now after admission three years you are in the college still you are researching is this college authorized or not and then why are in the college via this is called over analysis and working so we have to learn to place faith after an initial assessment of the of the authenticity of the source of our knowledge once we have determined that this source of knowledge is authentic whatever comes from there we have to accept it as then we will become free from overthinking and over analysis so the root cause of overthinking and over analysis is what lack of sufficient faith.

#### faith, spiritual knowledge, disciplic succession
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kmO-HeLRBHw?t=531
**Verse References:** —

**Key Quote:** "Attaining this spiritual knowledge should be obtained from the right source."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating the need to develop faith through determining the authenticity of the source of knowledge, particularly for spiritual knowledge, referencing Krishna's instructions in the Bhagavad Gita and Prabhupada's emphasis on disciplic succession.

So we have to learn to develop faith if we and that development of faith comes from the determination of the authenticity of the source of knowledge so therefore it is important that is why krishna also tells in the bhagavad-gita attaining this spiritual knowledge should be obtained from the right source what is the right source right source is how do you determine what is the right source for spiritual knowledge that has to come in a bona fide and that is why prabhupada has given him towards the end of the introduction of the bhagavad-gita the unbroken chain of discipleship succession stemming from krishna himself krishna gave this knowledge to brahma brahma gave it to narada narada gave to vyasa dev fabre who gave it to madhavacharya like that prop급 is the 32nd in the line of discipleship success like that prabhupada is the 32nd in the line of disciples so the criterion for the criterion for reposing our faith in the source of knowledge is given in the Bhagavad Gita and we use that criterion and we repose our faith in the Bhagavad Gita and then we will be able to take advantage of the knowledge in that.

### AAQ: How to Achieve Success Through Spiritual Practice? | HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### spiritual goals, devotional service, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, devotional life, Bhakti Yoga is such that it is the means as well as the end."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in spiritual life, the long-term and short-term goal is the same: service to Krishna and Vaishnavas, achieved through chanting Hare Krishna and serving Prabhupada's mission, emphasizing that devotional service is both the means and the end.

How can I achieve my long-term and short-term goal through spiritual practice? This is the next question. You see, now it all depends on what is your goal. Is your goal material or is it spiritual? If it is spiritual, how to achieve your long-term and short-term goals? Long-term and short-term goal in spiritual life is only one: service to Krishna and service to Vaishnavas. So, we chant Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. We serve the mission of Prabhupada. That's the short-term as well as long-term goal. Life after life, we want to be engaged in the service of the mission of Srila Prabhupada. Life after life, we want Srila Prabhupada as our Diksha Guru and want to follow his instructions. And we want to chant Hare Krishna. We want to never forget Krishna's holy name. This is the short-term goal. This is the long-term goal. So, devotional life, Bhakti Yoga is such that it is the means as well as the end. The means to perfect yourself spiritually, to achieve spiritual perfection in Bhakti Yoga is to chant Hare Krishna. And after perfection, what is it that you are going to do? What is it that you are going to achieve? You will chant Hare Krishna. That's all. So, devotional service is both the means as well as the end.

#### material goals, duty, Vedic principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=104
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Better than working towards the result is to work for duty."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts spiritual and material goals, arguing that focusing solely on material achievements is a regressive approach according to Vedic scriptures, and that prioritizing duty over results is a more progressive way of life.

Now, if you are talking about material goals, short-term, long-term goals, how to achieve through spiritual practice? Well, you see, this focus on achieving a certain goal, first have a goal in life, work towards it, achieve it. This is a very recent and very, what you say, regressive kind of thinking in the human society. It's not a progressive thinking. Why regressive? Because when we speak of goal, that means we have to have a result in mind. What is it that I want to achieve? So, all your activities become result-oriented. All your decisions in life will be result-oriented. Today's world people say, yes, we have to be result-oriented. Yes, that is the way to me. We have to focus. We have to have a goal. We have to work towards it, hard work, etc. But according to the Vedic scriptures, it is not a progressive way of life. Because if we focus too much on the goal that we want to achieve, we will be slipping from the human form of life into animal form of life and lower species of life. Because human life is not meant to work towards results. Better than working towards the result is to work for duty. That we also know in the material world. I mean, even in today's society, little bit of still, there is some respect for duty. Somebody wants to achieve a certain goal.

---
#### duty, Dronacharya, Vedic education
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=257
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Dronacharya thought, what is my duty in this circumstance? I am a teacher. He is a qualified student seeking knowledge. I cannot deny him. I will teach him."
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the importance of duty over results by narrating the story of Dronacharya, who taught archery to Drishtadyumna, even though he knew Drishtadyumna was destined to kill him, highlighting the Vedic principle of imparting knowledge to qualified students regardless of personal consequences.

Focus is on doing one's duty. The best example of this is Dronacharya. Dronacharya was a teacher. And his duty was to teach any eligible, qualified candidate if he comes. That's the duty of the teacher to teach. And in the Vedic times, teachers would not work for money. They would not say, give me a certain piece, then I will teach you. Today's world, teaching means only qualification required is you have to have money to pay the teacher. You have money to pay the teacher, college, whatever, you will get educated. That's the only thing. No other consideration is there. But in the Vedic times, money was never in the picture. The teacher will never ask for money. Teacher will assess, is the student qualified to receive this knowledge and will he use this knowledge properly. If yes, then the teacher will teach him even if he doesn't get any fees in return. And after receiving the knowledge, the student will not just walk away. Today's world, what others? Go to college, get educated, convocation will be there, go, take a degree, walk out after that, you are not bothered. You will not even remember the name of your teacher. But in the Vedic times, it is not like that. If you have received knowledge, then the student at the end of it, although teacher is not asking for any fees, it is the duty of the disciple to give some Guru Dakshina. Dakshina means it is whatever it is possible for you, you give. It is not a fee. It is a Dakshina. From my side, I am giving according to what level of gratitude I feel and what is possible for me, what is my capacity, I am giving. So it is Guru Dakshina. So the student has to give. Guru Dakshina, he cannot come away empty handed. So the teacher also, whatever possible for you, give. I give like that. So Dronacharya was approached by Drishtadyumna. Who is this Drishtadyumna? Drishtadyumna was the son of Drupada. And Drupada had some fight with Dronacharya. And Drupada had conducted a yajna to beget a son who would kill Drona. And that is how Drishtadyumna was born. So Drishtadyumna was born to kill Dronacharya. And this Drishtadyumna comes to Dronacharya and says, I want to learn the art of archery from you. I want to learn Dhanur Vidya from you. And what does Dronacharya do? Dronacharya says, yes, I will teach him. Dronacharya knows very well that Drishtadyumna is born to kill him. So if Dronacharya were to be result oriented, what he will think? If I teach him, what is the result? I will kill him. So I will not teach him. Correct knowledge. That is the common sense. Anybody in today's world, he will think like that. And you cannot fault him. But Dronacharya did not think like that. Dronacharya thought, what is my duty in this circumstance? I am a teacher. He is a qualified student seeking knowledge. I cannot deny him. I will teach him. So impartially he taught Drishtadyumna also along with the Pandavas and Kauravas.

#### duty, Duryodhana, Pandavas, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So in Vedic civilization, the whole fight of the Kurukshetra happened because Duryodhana was result oriented, Pandavas were duty oriented."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Duryodhana's result-oriented approach with the Pandavas' duty-oriented approach, explaining that the Kurukshetra war was a consequence of Duryodhana's desire for the kingdom, while the Pandavas prioritized their duty as Kshatriyas, even when it meant facing hardship.

So duty. In Vedic civilization, duty was the foremost. If you look at Duryodhana, Duryodhana was result oriented. I want the kingdom. Somehow I have to get the kingdom. Either cheat, fight, do whatever, get the kingdom. Result oriented. Pandavas, duty oriented. We will do our duty. Whatever our duty requires of us, we will. Result is immaterial. So Duryodhana cheated and got the kingdom. Pandavas also could have cheated and kept the kingdom. But they said, no, we will not cheat. We will do our duty as a Kshatriya. We have lost the game of dice. We have won in Vannavasa and Ajnatavasa. We will do it. So they were like that. So in Vedic civilization, the whole fight of the Kurukshetra happened. Why? Because Duryodhana was result oriented. Pandavas were duty oriented. That is why it was a Dharma Yudha. Because Duryodhana was not following Dharma. Pandavas were following Dharma. So the higher than being a result oriented worker is to be a duty oriented worker.

---
#### duty, Varnashrama Dharma, Sanatana Dharma, sacrifice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=535
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So all these duties, if somebody follows, that is higher than simply doing duty according to one's livelihood."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of duty, stating that following duty according to Varnashrama Dharma and Sanatana Dharma is superior to simply fulfilling one's professional obligations, and that duties extend to other living entities, Devatas, Rishis, and include Yajna, Dana, and Tapasya.

And in duty oriented work also, following the duty according to the Varnashama Dharma, Sanatana Dharma given in the Vedic scriptures, that is even better than executing duty according to our understanding in today's world. I have to go to office on time. I have to work 8 hours. I have to do my whatever assigned tasks are there. That is one level of duty. But according to Varnashama Dharma, I have to situate myself in a particular Varna and Ashrama and perform those duties. Those duties are not limited only to the office work. The duties extend not only to one's office or one's vocation, one's livelihood. But also to work. But also to the other living entities, to other human beings, to the Devatas, to the Rishis and Sages, to even the animals in the society like cows and dogs and bulls and horses which from all these animals will take so much service. So there are duties to perform towards them also. And over and above all this, Yajna, Dana and Tapasya. There are duties towards sacrifice for the Devatas and for Vishnu. Then there is Dana, charity. One has to compulsorily give charity. That is one of the duties. If you are earning money, it is not that you have to put it in your bank and you have to enjoy. No. You have to also give Dana. That is also a charity. Charity is also a duty. And Tapasya. Not only that. You also have to undertake certain austerities. Tapasyas. You have to fast on Ekadashi. You have to fast on Janmashtami. You have to follow certain Pratas. So certain Tapasyas are also built in. So all these duties, if somebody follows, that is higher than simply doing duty according to one's livelihood.

---
#### surrender, exclusive devotion, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=662
**Verse References:** BG 18.66
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "Your only duty should be to serve Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the highest form of duty is to give up all other duties and exclusively serve Krishna, referencing Krishna's instruction in the Bhagavad Gita to surrender unto Him alone, emphasizing that the ultimate goal is to serve Krishna and the spiritual master without concern for personal liberation or destination.

Now out of all the people who perform duties, according to the Varnashrama Dharma, those who give up all those duties, and make it their only duties to serve Krishna exclusively, he is even higher. That is why Krishna tells in the end of the Bhagavad Gita, here in chapter 66 verse, sarva dharman parityajya. Give up all other duties. sarva dharman parityajya mam ekam saranam vraja. Surrender unto only me. Your only duty should be to serve Krishna. So the person who makes it the goal of his life and the existence of his life, his duty of his life is only exclusively serving Krishna. Serving Krishna means serving the duty of the spiritual master, Srila Prabhupada. So a person who does this, he is the topmost. Even there also there is no goal. There is no goal that we have to achieve. I have to go back to the garden. I have to achieve liberation. I should go to Vaikuntha. None of those things should be there. My only goal is my duty which is to serve Krishna and my Guru Maharaj, Srila Prabhupada. That's all. Where I go, how many births I will take, whether I get liberated or not, none of my consideration.

#### human faculty, duty, self-restraint, austerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=751
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, duty means you should be ready even if something, I have to do something, I have to do something which I don't like to do, I have to still do. That is called duty."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the unique human capacity for duty, self-restraint, and sacrifice, explaining that duty involves doing what is not pleasing, and that developing this faculty leads to higher levels of spiritual development, culminating in exclusive service to Krishna.

So that is the topmost. Development of the human intellect and human faculty. You can go up till there. Because only human beings have this faculty, you see. To think of duty. Animals have no sense of duty. That is why you don't have dharma for them. That you have to fast on Ekadashi. Can you go and tell your pet dog or pet cat, today is Ekadashi fast. You can forcibly make the cat or dog eat Ekadashi Prasastu. That's a difficult matter. But you know, it's not that the cat or dog is voluntarily following. No, we are voluntarily following. So that faculty to take tapasya, that faculty to sacrifice, that faculty to undertake certain austerities, the faculty to exercise self-restraint, is there only in the human form. So if you use that capability, that faculty in the human being, to do things which are not one's liking. I may like to do something, but I say, no, I will not do that. I will do something which is not very pleasing. If you ask me what I like to do, I like to eat when I feel hungry. But today is Janmasthami, I have to fast. So, although you would like to eat, you are ready to sacrifice that desire to eat and you are ready to fast. That faculty is there in the human being to do something, to give up something which you like to do and to do something which is not very pleasing. This faculty is there in the human being. And that is where that sense of duty springs from. In duty, what is there? Duty, you will do your duty even if it is not palatable, even if it is distressing. So, duty means you should be ready even if something, I have to do something, I have to do something which I don't like to do, I have to still do. That is called duty. So, you develop this faculty in the human being of self-restraint. You develop from the point of you know, doing your duty to doing varnashama dharma duty to rendering exclusive service to Krishna. That is the topmost development of the faculty.

---
#### devotional service, chanting, Prabhupada, grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=895
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "That chanting of Krishna's holy name and rendering service will gradually pull you towards that final destination of your devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that the real goal is to reach a stage where one's only duty is to serve Krishna and Guru, achieved through chanting Hare Krishna, associating with devotees, and rendering practical devotional service, emphasizing that this process is driven by the taste for Krishna and the grace of Krishna and Guru.

So, achieving long term, short term, goals is at the very bottom level. We have to progress far beyond that. We have to go to a certain level of sense of duty and in that also we have to go to duty towards varnashama dharma and finally duty towards guru and Krishna. That is the topmost decision. So, we should instead of thinking of short term, long term material goals, we should be thinking of when will I come to the stage where my only duty will be to serve Krishna and Guru. No other duty in my life. That is the real goal that we have to have. And how to achieve that? Very simple. Chant Hare Krishna, associate with devotees and render whatever practical devotional service you can. That chanting of Krishna's holy name and rendering service will gradually pull you towards that final destination of your devotional service. Where you have no other motivation than to serve the pleasure of Guru and Krishna. So, rendering devotional service to Krishna is very powerful. We don't have to force ourselves. Oh no, I have to chant sixteen rounds. You don't have to do that. It's not a matter of force. We all begin, most of us, we begin by chanting one round, two rounds. Slowly we will make it four rounds. Slowly make it eight rounds. Then we will come to sixteen rounds. Isn't it? So, how does that happen? It's not that somebody is standing behind us with a stick. If you don't chant four rounds today, from today onwards I am going to beat you. Nobody did that. We automatically, we felt a taste for chanting. And we felt yes, I want to chant four rounds from today. Start chanting. So, the taste for Krishna is like that. Once you taste the devotional service, that devotional service will drag you until you come to the final point of your devotional service. So, simple thing. Read Prabhupada's books. Follow Prabhupada's instructions. Chant Hare Krishna. And you will achieve your long-term goal, which is to become a pure devotee of Krishna. That's the only result you can, you can desire. That also, it is dependent on the grace of Krishna and Buddha. Whether they give award as pure devotional service or not. But as far as we are concerned, we want to serve our Guru's instructions and Krishna's instructions. That's a period. Whether we get Krishna Prema or not, whether I go to the spiritual world or not, whether I get liberated or not, these things are not our consideration.

### AAQ: How to Serve Krishna Without Feeling Overwhelmed | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### false ego, humility, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HwUSa66RoYw?t=101
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But if that devotee had not done it that day, today I will not be sitting here."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about overcoming his false ego through the encouragement of another devotee to engage in menial service, highlighting the transformative impact of such experiences.

I am for program. I went, kept that in the corner, slowly started walking towards the door. Again, Prabhuji, Prabhuji! I said, now what? I said, where are you? He said, where are you going? There are so many more mats. I said, what, one mat is not enough? You want me to pick up more mats? Appa, it was such a, I tell you, it was a, you know, for my ego, because all my life I have never done any menial work like that. Lived always like a prince, like a king, you know, there are always people to take care of washing the clothes and washing the dishes and all that. Never did any such thing in my life. So it was really, you know, it was really, it was like somebody is pushing me, very uncomfortable doing that and all that. But if that devotee had not done it that day, today I will not be sitting here. That's the reality. So I'm thankful to him that he broke through my false ego barrier and he pushed me to engage in service because of which today I'm here.

#### devotional service, false ego, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HwUSa66RoYw?t=224
**Verse References:** BG 9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The pain that you have to take in Krishna consciousness is the pain of overcoming our false ego, which always advises us against rendering service to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that false ego prevents us from rendering service to Krishna, causing discomfort and reluctance, and emphasizes the importance of gratitude towards those who push us beyond our comfort zones into devotional service.

So reality is, what is preventing us from rendering service to Krishna? Why are we feeling reluctant? Why are we feeling burdensome? Krishna actually says in the ninth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, susukam kartum abhyayam. Devotional service to Krishna is very joyfully performed. So then if it is joyfully performed, when picking up the mat, why am I feeling so much discomfort, so much embarrassment? Because false ego. Maya doesn't want us to engage in service to Krishna, because if we engage in service, we'll get out of our clutches. So Maya will use false ego and tell us, why you are to do all these things? This is not meant for you. You are supposed to live like a king. What is all this, you know, picking up the mat, wiping the floor? No, no, no, no, no, no, no. These are all burdens. And you are engaging in spiritual life for this. I mean, you are an engineer, you have studied in so much, this one big college, you have got so much rank, this, that, and all that. All that for this? So these are the thoughts that will come to your mind and which will inhibit you, which will discourage you from doing service to Krishna. So at that time, some devotee has to come and push you. Baba, do know. So little by little, little by little, that barrier gets broken, broken, broken, broken. What will happen is eventually we'll become comfortable. We'll go beyond comfortable. We'll start loving to do that service. So this is how we have to be ready to go beyond our comfort zone. Then only we can expect to progress. No pain, no gain. The pain that you have to take in Krishna consciousness is the pain of overcoming our false ego, which always advises us against rendering service to Krishna. So therefore, if somebody is pushing you into services, you should not develop grudge against that devotee. You should be grateful to that devotee. You see my false ego, Maya is so strongly preventing me from doing service, but this devotee so mercifully, is pushing me to engage in service to Krishna. That is the way we should look at it. So it is good that somebody is pushing you out of your comfort zone.

### AAQ: How to end the Cycle of Birth & Death? | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### cycle of birth and death, subtle body, reincarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So same logic, if you extrapolate, at the time of death, this body will cease to be, I will go on to another body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the cycle of birth and death, using the analogy of changing bodies within a single lifetime and the role of the subtle body in carrying the soul to the next life.

After death, an interesting question. After death, the human soul has to take another body. When will this process stop, and what do we do for that? So Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita that within this very life, you can see that we are changing so many bodies. I gave up the body of a baby, got the body of a child, gave up the body of the child, got the body of a youth, gave up the body of the youth, got an old body, got a new body, got an old person's body, and finally when death comes, I will give up this body. So logically speaking, when in this life I have changed so many bodies, but I continue to exist. I didn't cease to exist. The body of the baby finished means I was not finished, I continued, right? So same logic, if you extrapolate, at the time of death, this body will cease to be, I will go on to another body. So when death comes, what happens? We are taken by our subtle body. Mind, intelligence, and false ego transfer us to another body. Actually, that's what has happened to us in this life also. When the body of the baby went away and we got the body of the child, we continue to feel I am the same person. And when we get a youthful body, we continue to feel I am the same person, although the body of the child and the baby is not there. Why? Because the same subtle body is continuing. The same mind, intelligence, and false ego is continuing. That is continuing uninterrupted. So for me, that feeling of I, I am this, doesn't cease. So we don't feel that I am changing the body, because although the gross body is changing, the subtle body is not changing. So the subtle body carries us from the body of the baby to the body of the child to the body of the youth to the body of the old person. Similarly, the subtle body will carry us from this body to the next body. So this cycle of birth and death goes on, correct?

---
#### suffering, birth, death, disease, old age
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So whether it is birth or death, it is very painful."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the painful nature of birth, death, disease, and old age, highlighting the suffering inherent in the material world.

So Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita, jātasya dhruva mṛtyur. Whenever a person is born, 100% guaranteed that that person will die. Yes or no? Everybody accepts this. And Krishna says the reverse is also true. Jātasya dhruva mṛtyur janma mṛtasya ca. When a person dies, it is guaranteed that he is going to take another birth. So we are all caught up in this cycle of repeated birth and death. It's not a pleasurable thing to undergo birth and death. Death is a very, very painful experience. That's why nobody wants to die. Even an animal understands this. So if somebody, if a human being says, what is there? Death, you know, it's not a big thing. I am not afraid of death. That means he is duller than an animal. Even an animal understands death is very painful. That's why you try to cut the throat of an animal, it will shout, it will shriek, it will scream, it will try to run away. It will not give up so easily because even an animal knows death is very painful. So death is a very painful thing. Similarly, birth is also very painful. How are all of us born? We are all born crying, no? None of us are born, ahahaha, you know, laughing, you know, I had a wonderful experience, so joyful to take birth. Nobody takes birth like that, right? Everybody takes birth crying. It's an extremely painful experience. The pain is so great. It's so painful, the process of birth. So whether it is birth or death, it is very painful. And if you say, okay, momentarily, I have to undergo some pain for nine months, 10 months, I'm in the mother's womb, painful, but after I come out, over, no pain. And death, few seconds, it will be over. Okay. So, you know, birth, when I took birth, some pain I underwent, some suffering I underwent, 100 years I lived, happy. And at the end of my life, I was happy. And at the end of 100 years, death came, I had to struggle a little bit, no problem. No, sir, in between birth and death, two more things are there, old age and disease. That is also painful, no? One small fever comes also, it will be so depressing for us. We have to take medication, the body will ache, there will be a headache, eyes will be burning, the nose will be burning. So many problems. We will not be able to digest proper food. Taste will go away. So it's painful. Disease is painful. And old age is also painful. All the joints will ache, there will be no energy in the body, you will not be able to walk, you cannot sit down, you cannot walk properly. So much trouble.

---
#### suffering, threefold miseries, kleshas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=312
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So you see, in the midst of this birth and death, so many miseries we have to undergo."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the threefold miseries (kleshas) - adhibhautika, adhidaivika, and adhyatmika - explaining how they contribute to suffering in the material world, using examples like natural disasters, political issues, and mental distress.

And as if these four things are not enough, there are also three kinds of kleshas, adhibhautika klesha, adhidaivika klesha, and adhyatmika klesha. Adhidaivika klesha, excessive redness, headaches, empty brains, floods. You built a house with great difficulty. You earned a lot of money, you did hard work, built a house, one flood will come, everything is swept away. Gone. Nobody has control over this. Supernatural occurrences. Distress, miseries which come on account of supernatural forces. Floods, earthquakes, extreme heat during summer, extreme cold during winter, all these things will create problems for us. Okay, all the rainy season got over, the weather has become very nice and you know October, November season very, very pleasant weather. So things have become peaceful. No Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Immediately what will happen? There will be Adi Daivika Klesa is not there. Adi Bhavathika Klesa will start. Trump will announce H1B visa, $100,000 you have to pay and he will say 50% tariff I am putting exactly when Diwali is starting. So all your, you know, festival mood is all dampened. Arrey, I did not do anything to Trump. Why is he putting tariff on me? So other living entities will cause problems for you. And you will see when it is extreme heat during summer or extreme cold during winter, what will happen? You will be troubled by winter. The heat and the cold of the winter but there will be no mosquitoes. Weather becomes very good, immediately mosquitoes will start. One problem goes another will come. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Other living entities will come and trouble us for no reason, for no fault of ours. And then there is Adhyatmika Klesa. Everything is fine, but my own mind will give me trouble. I will become depressed for no reason. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. I will become depressed for no reason. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Adi Bhavathika no reason. Mood is not good today. I feel very grumpy today. You cannot help it, isn't it? Everybody around you is happy. You alone are feeling very, you know, very foul mood today. So everybody will start avoiding it. Today's mood is not okay. Don't go near it.

---
#### liberation, Krishna consciousness, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=461
**Verse References:** BG 4.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So an intelligent person will question, how can I get out of this cycle of birth and death and disease, old age and these threefold miseries? What should I do?"
**Summary:** The speaker presents Krishna consciousness as the solution to escape the cycle of birth and death and the threefold miseries, emphasizing the importance of understanding Krishna's transcendental nature and engaging in devotional service.

So our own mind is giving trouble. Adhyatmika plays. So you see, in the midst of this birth and death, so many miseries we have to undergo. So an intelligent person will question, how can I get out of this cycle of birth and death and disease, old age and these threefold miseries? What should I do? So if you want to do that, Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, janma karma chamedivyam evam yojanati tatvatah, tyaktvadeham punarjanma naiti mameti sorjuna. Krishna says that if a soul takes the trouble to understand the position of Krishna, that Krishna, his birth, his activities, his qualities, these are all not material. Krishna's birth is not like my birth. Krishna doesn't have to undergo old age and disease like me. Krishna doesn't have to undergo Adhyatmika klesha, Adibhavitika klesha, Adidaivika klesha. Krishna is beyond all this. Simply to understand that Krishna is transcendent, his activities are transcendental, his birth is transcendental, his every quality is transcendental, simply by understanding this, Krishna says, punarjanma naiti, you don't have to take repeated birth and death in this material world. Then what will happen to us? Mameti, Krishna says, we will go to Krishna, we will go to Krishna loka. Just like we all live on this earth planet, Krishna has his own home, which is called Goloka Vrindavan. We will go there. Now, if we go there, what is the advantage? Whether I go there or here, both places I am existing. So, what happens to the troubles of birth, death, disease, old age and the Adidaivika klesha, Adibhavitika klesha, Adhyatmika klesha, what about all these things? Krishna says that once you go to the spiritual world, that Goloka Vrindavan Vaikuntha, there is no more birth, death, disease, old age. And the three-fold miseries there don't exist in the material, in the spiritual world. So, therefore, if you want to get out of the cycle of birth and death, you don't want another birth within the material world, then what you should do? You should learn about Krishna from the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam and follow the instructions of Krishna. And if you follow the instructions of Krishna, then no more the problems of birth, death, disease and old age. All right. So, the simple solution to this repeated birth and death is engaging in devotional service to Krishna. That's the simple thing.
