#### King Raivatha, Revathi, Time
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=178
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So without being aware of it king raivata had spent so much time when the singing of the gandhar was going on in brahma's lord brahma's assembly on the earth by that time what had happened satya yuga had got over satya yuga itself lasts for more than seventeen lakh years."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of King Raivatha and his daughter Revathi, who travel to Brahmaloka to seek advice on finding a suitable husband, highlighting the vast difference in time scales between Brahmaloka and Earth.

The story of King Raivatha. King Raivatha was the son of King Revatha. And this King Raivatha was ruling over a kingdom in which the main city of his was located on a hill. And because of he being the ruler of that kingdom, that hill or that mountain came to be known as Raivathaka mountain or Raivathaka hill. This Raivatha had a daughter by the name Revaethi. When this Revathi grew up, she was beautiful and well qualified girl. King Raivatha thought that I have to look for a suitable groom for my daughter. Now, this King Raivatha was ruling over his kingdom in the Satya Yuga, Satya Yuga. So this is many millions of years before the current proceedings which we are discussing which happened in the Mahabharata. So this King Raivata thought, now for me to find a suitable husband for my daughter, I have to take the advice of somebody superior. So he thought, let me take the advice of Brahmaji himself. Because Brahmaji is the progenitor of everyone, he is the creator, the secondary creator of this universe. So let me, because he is the oldest personality, let me go and ask him. So King Raivata along with his daughter Revati, he went to Brahmaloka. When he approached Brahmaloka and he came to the assembly of Lord Brahma, he said, I want to meet you. At that time, it so happened that Brahmaji was witnessing a song performance by some Gandharvas. Actually, in the Gandharvaloka, there are five very important or most important chief Gandharvas. One of them is Kumbhuru. And so like that, there are five people of which two of them are haha and who who so it so happened that at that assembly of brahma ji haha and who who were performing some musical glorification of lord vishnu lord krishna in front of brahma ji and brahma ji was witnessing this he was participating in this so when king raivata entered brahma ji asked him to sit down and wait till this performance gets over so king raivata along with his daughter into they both of them sat down and for some time haha and who who were singing the glories of krishna vishnu and once they finished then king raivata came forward to brahma ji paid his obeisance offered him his respects and then he asked brahma ji that i want a suitable husband for my daughter raivati so i have come seeking your advice about who is the most appropriate most suitable greatest person whom i can give my daughter in marriage too so in brahma loka the time span is very large as we have seen twelve hours of brahma ji is equal to forty three lakh twenty thousand into one thousand years of our lives on this earth so without being aware of it king raivata had spent so much time when the singing of the gandhar was going on in brahma's lord brahma's assembly on the earth by that time what had happened satya yuga had got over satya yuga itself lasts for more than seventeen lakh years. So, Satyuga got over, then next was Treta Yuga which lasted for about 12 lakh years, more than 12 lakh years, that also got over and Dwapara Yuga had almost, was almost coming to its close, that means another almost more than 8 lakh years had already passed by. But Raivata was not aware of this because he was sitting in Brahmaloka, the passage of time you cannot make out. So he was not aware of what is going on on the earth. So Brahmaji told Raivata, the most suitable person for your daughter right now is Lord Balarama who is an incarnation of the Supreme Lord Krishna. So you can go and hand over your daughter in marriage to Balaramaji.

---
#### Yugas, Lifespan, Size
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=479
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "According to the Srimad Bhagavatam, Namsayat Aayur Balo Smriti, when time passes by from one Yuga to another, the lifespan of the people reduces."
**Summary:** Raivata returns to Earth and finds everything changed, including the diminished size and lifespan of people across the Yugas, illustrating the effects of time.

So Raivata along with his daughter came back to the earth's free planet and when he came back he saw that the whole scenario had completely changed. All his family members, all his brothers, ministers, kingdom, everything had totally changed. And one more thing what he noticed was the people had become very much diminished in size. According to the Srimad Bhagavatam, Namsayat Aayur Balo Smriti, when time passes by from one Yuga to another, the lifespan of the people reduces. For example, in Satyuga people on an average, the average lifespan of the human beings was 100,000 years. When it came to Treta Yuga, that reduced to 10,000 years. Dwapara Yuga... it reduced to 1000 years. Kali Yuga, it is reduced even further to 100 years or even lesser. So Namsayat Aayu... so Aayu reduces. Bala... the physical strength of the people reduces. So accordingly the size of the people also keeps diminishing. In fact, in the past 30 years, in everybody's eyes, the size of the people has also kept diminishing. In South India, there is one, in Andhra Pradesh, there is one place called Lepakshi. There is a beautiful temple there. It is said that the place got the name Lepakshi, meaning in Telugu it means Lepakshi, means O Pakshi, O bird, please get up. So it is said that this is the place where Jatayu, after attacking Ravana, fell down when he was mortally wounded by Ravana. And when Lord Ramachandra, searching for Sita Maya, he came across Jatayu, he lifted Jatayu in his hands and he said, Lepakshi, please get up, O bird. So that's how this place got its name, Lepakshi. And in that place, there is one large footprint of Sita Devi. It is said that Ravana, after fighting with Jatayu, rested for a little while there and that's how Sita Maya's footprint happened to get embedded in the rock there. That footprint, if you see, is almost maybe four feet or bigger than that. It is that huge it is. So from this itself, we can see the people of Satyuga, how huge their bodies were compared to today's time. So with time, the size of the body also keeps reducing. Today, our feet, if you compare with Sita Devi's footprint, the mark of the footprint that is there in Lepakshi, if we compare with that is our feet hardly not even one foot. Whereas Sita Maya's foot is almost four feet. So with this, we can understand how with time over the different ages, the size of the bodies of the human beings, as well as all other animals, birds, trees. Everything keeps changing.

---
#### Memory, Shruti Dhara, Balarama, Revathi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=668
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Because in Kali Yuga, people cannot, don't have such intense memory, such an excellent memory power, therefore Vedavyasa put down all the Vedas in writing for the first time."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the decline of memory power across the Yugas and how Vedavyasa compiled the Vedas in writing due to this decline, then returns to the story of Raivata offering Revathi to Balarama.

Everything keeps reducing. People's Mrithi Shakti, their ability to remember, keeps reducing. That is why in the olden days, in the Satya Yuga, Dwapara Yuga, Treta Yuga, there were no written books. All the students, they would go to the Gurukula, they would hear once the entire Vedic knowledge from the Guru and they would remember it for life. Such people are called Shruti Dhara. They can, they have excellent memories. Because in Kali Yuga, people cannot, don't have such intense memory power, therefore Vedavyasa put down all the Vedas in writing for the first time. This was 5000 years ago. And today the situation is that whatever has been written down, that also we cannot remember. We need computers, we need mobiles, we need these things to remind us. So because with time, memory power keeps reducing. So when Raivatha along with Revathi came down, he saw the size of the people had reduced very much and he saw so many things have changed and all that. But anyway, taking Brahmaji's advice, he approached Lord Balaram and offered his daughter in hand to Balaram. But Revathi was so tall and Balaram was much shorter than her. How can he marry Revathi? So then Balaramji, what he did, he took his plough and pulled Revathi down and reduced their size to his height and then he married her. So this, this story is there in folklore as well as in some Puranas, some part of the story is also there. This is how Lord Balaramji married Revathi.

**Summary:** The speaker returns to the main narrative, describing Arjuna's arrival in Dwaraka, his meeting with Krishna, and his interactions with Subhadra while disguised as a sanyasi.

Now we come back to our story. Our original narration of what Arjuna was doing now that Balaram and Krishna were there in Dwaraka. Arjuna approached Dwaraka disguised as a sanyasi and he approached Krishna and divulged to him that he was Arjuna and that he had come there in disguise because he in his heart he wanted to marry Subhadra. Now Krishna in his heart also was not keen on Subhadra marrying Duryodhana because Duryodhana's character was not so good and Krishna, when he heard from Arjuna, Krishna was also keen that Arjuna should marry Subhadra. So now what to do? It was the Chaturmasya time. So Krishna said, you stay in Dwaraka, let us see how things develop. So then Arjuna, once it happened, Balaram came to know that there is a travelling man. He said, you stay in Dwaraka. Let us see how things develop. So then Arjuna, once it happened, Balaram came to know that there is a travelling man. He said, you stay in the temple. He said, you stay in the temple. Brilliant! Loving Tendulノ Brother Vapшi. For Indian Sentiment, there is a travelling sanyasi Paribhrajaka who has come to our place. He is staying with Krishna in his palace. So he invited him the sanyasi to his palace. Balaram ji did not know that it was Arjuna, he was in this disguise. So Balaraj came to his. So Arjuna, in the disguise of the sanyasi, came to Balaram s palace and Balaram had made lot of arrangements to receive him, and he had prepared a big feast, Prasadam, for feeding the sanyasi. And then Balaram being very affectionate towards his sister, younger sister Subhadra, he called her to serve the sanyasi, the prasadam, so that she could get the blessings of the sanyasi by serving him. So Subhadra came forward and Subhadra was also very beautiful, very intelligent, but at the same time she was simple at heart. So she came following her elder brother's instructions and she served the various kinds of prasadam to this sanyasi. But while serving the sanyasi, Subhadra noticed that this sanyasi was constantly staring at her from the corner of his eyes. And she could also see that this sanyasi was quite young and his body was also quite well built and he was very beautiful to look at. Although there was beard, moustache. He had long matted hair and all that, but still she could make out his features are very beautiful and he was looking quite bright. So she also got a little attracted to him and she was also smiling. Now when Arjuna saw this, Arjuna understood, okay, Subhadra is definitely, she appreciates me, she will not mind marrying me.

#### Krishna, Arjuna, Subhadra, Abduction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=947
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "That you see, best is that you kidnap Subhadra."
**Summary:** Krishna advises Arjuna to kidnap Subhadra because Balaram is unwilling to allow the marriage and favors Duryodhana.

Now after honoring the prasadam and all that, Arjuna again went back to his residence. And then Arjuna and Krishna consulted Vasudeva and Devaki that Arjuna has come here in the disguise of a sanyasi and he wants to marry Subhadra, but Balaramji is unwilling. So Vasudeva and Devaki said, we are very happy if Subhadra marries you, but then Balaram has already decided that she should be wedded to Duryodhana and we cannot go against him. So now what to do? So then Krishna advised Arjuna. That you see, best is that you kidnap Subhadra. You take her away by force. Because otherwise to convince Balaramji, not possible. Dauji is not going to listen to anyone. He has made up his mind. So better is, you abduct Subhadra. Now how to abduct her? Because Dwaraka is such a fortified place, it is in the middle of the sea, nobody can enter, nobody can go without proper permission. And the notice of all the elders. So Krishna said, you wait for a little while. After a few days, there is some pooja in some temple at a certain location outside Dwaraka, which Subhadra is going to visit. When she is on the way there, you can abduct her.

---
#### Abduction, Balarama's Anger, Yadava Reputation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1032
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Yadava reputation, the reputation of the Yadava dynasty is at stake."
**Summary:** Arjuna abducts Subhadra on her way to the Raivathaka mountain (Girnar), leading to Balaram's intense anger and his determination to retaliate.

So then on the scheduled day, Subhadra set out to visit a temple in the Raivathaka mountain. This is the same Raivathaka mountain over which King Raivatha had ruled over. And this Raivathaka mountain, today it is known as Girnar. So this Girnar, today it is in Gujarat. It is about 200-250 kilometers from Dwaraka. So you can, it is about 3-4 hours journey from Dwaraka to this Girnar or that day it was called as Raivathaka hill. So Subhadra was going on the way to Raivathaka hill. And taking that opportunity, Arjuna removed his disguise. He also came on a chariot and he forcibly took away Subhadra. And Subhadra was also very happy to go with Arjuna. Now after this incident happened, those who were accompanying all the other women who were accompanying Subhadra, they all went back to Dwaraka. And they informed everyone that like this, when we were on the way to Raivathaka along with Subhadra to the temple, Arjuna came and abducted Subhadra. So when Krishna heard this, Vasudeva heard this, Devaki heard this, they were all quite happy. But Balaram's anger came out with full force. His eyes became red, he became angry. He took up his weapons and called all the army and said, Come on, let us go. This Arjuna, how emboldened he has become. How can he become so bold enough to abduct my sister like this? The Yadava reputation, the reputation of the Yadava dynasty is at stake. We will not allow this to happen. Come, we will kill Arjuna and bring back Subhadra. Like that, Balaramji got up and fully lived.

#### Krishna, Balarama, Devotee Relationship, Humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is the transcendental loving dealing between Krishna and his various devotees because Balaramji also at heart, even though he is the elder brother of Krishna and Krishna holds his hands, he falls at his feet, he supplicates in front of him, all these things, Krishna being a younger brother, he respects Balaramji a lot."
**Summary:** Krishna pacifies Balaram by falling at his feet, showcasing the transcendental loving relationship between Krishna and his devotees, where Krishna respects Balaram as an elder brother despite being the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

Now what to do? Balaramji goes and attacks Arjuna, then Arjuna will be finished. There is no way he can survive. So now what to do? Then immediately Krishna, there is no other choice, Krishna fell at the feet of Balaramji. So this is very wonderful because originally Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. He is the fountainhead of all incarnations. And Balaram is his first expansion. So Balaramji, that way he subordinates to Krishna. But then Krishna is falling at the feet of Balaram. This is the transcendental loving dealing between Krishna and his various devotees because Balaramji also at heart, even though he is the elder brother of Krishna and Krishna holds his hands, he falls at his feet, he supplicates in front of him, all these things, Krishna being a younger brother, he respects Balaramji a lot. But Balaramji always thinks in his heart that I am Krishna's servant, Krishna is the supreme person. But externally, he deals with Krishna like his elder brother, so that Krishna can enjoy that relationship, so that he can please Krishna with that relationship of an elder brother dominating over a younger brother.

#### Krishna's Diplomacy, Balarama's Acceptance, Arjuna and Subhadra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna was so favorable, he was so affectionate towards Arjuna that he was ready to go against the wishes of his elder brother Balaram and take all risks to hand over his younger sister, his dear most younger sister Subhadra to Arjuna as his wife."
**Summary:** Krishna uses sweet words and reasoning to calm Balaram, informing him of Subhadra's love for Arjuna, leading to Balaram's acceptance and the eventual marriage of Arjuna and Subhadra.

So Krishna, even though he is the supreme person, immediately fell at Balaram's lotus feet. And then, you know Krishna, Krishna, the way, the kind of sweet words Krishna speaks, nobody can withstand it. And then Krishna started speaking to Balaramji in a very sweet way, praying to him and reasoning with him, giving him various logic. And finally, he informed Balaramji that Subhadra also loves Arjuna and she also wanted to marry him. When Balaramji finally heard this, he cooled down, he completely calmed down. Then he said, okay, whatever has happened has happened. Now Krishna, you only see what has to be done now. So then Krishna immediately, he immediately took Akrura with him. And then he went and met Arjuna and then they brought them back to Dwaraka. And both Arjuna and Subhadra took the blessings of Balaramji. And Balaramji also saw that his sister Subhadra is very happy to be with Arjuna. So he also gave lot of gifts to Arjuna and to Subhadra and they sent them back. And like this, Arjuna brought Subhadra to, back to Indraprastha. And over the course of time, Arjuna and Subhadra, their son was born, who was Abhimanyu. So like this, Arjuna married four wives and he had various children through all of them. And we can understand the great significance of Arjuna's relationship with Krishna. Krishna was so favorable, he was so affectionate towards Arjuna that he was ready to go against the wishes of his elder brother Balaram and take all risks to hand over his younger sister, his dear most younger sister Subhadra to Arjuna as his wife.

---
#### Jarasandha, Asuric Mentality, Kalabhairava
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1336
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "His whole intention was how to expand my empire, how to increase my military power, how to conquer my enemies, how to increase the economic development of my country, how to increase the opulences, the material sense gratification of my citizens and myself."
**Summary:** The speaker shifts focus to Jarasandha in Magadha, describing his Asuric nature, his devotion to Kalabhairava, and his focus on material expansion and military power.

So such are the wonderful loving dealings of Krishna with his devotees. Now while all this was happening, in Magadha, there were further developments under Jarasandha. Jarasandha, as we all know, he was a Asura. He was inimical towards Krishna. Seventeen times he had attacked Mathura. Each time he had got defeated. And he was harboring a great inimity towards Krishna. At the same time, he was a great devotee of Kalabhairava. These Asuras are normally like that. They do not subordinate themselves to Krishna or Vishnu. But they will be great devotees of some Devata or the other. Like Hiranyaka Shibu was a great devotee of Brahmaji. Ravana was a great devotee of Lord Shiva. Like that this Jarasandha was also a great devotee of Kalabhairava. Now Jarasandha, he was, because he was an Asuric king, he was not interested in ruling as per Dharma. His whole intention was how to expand my empire, how to increase my military power, how to conquer my enemies, how to increase the economic development of my country, how to increase the opulences, the material sense gratification of my citizens and myself.

---
#### Dharmic Kings, Asuric Kings, Jarasandha's Conquests
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1420
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Economic development would be taken care of by the Vaisyas and the kings would never, the Vaishnava kings who were there like this, people like Yudhishthira and such other people, they would never look to encroach upon other kingdoms and try to annex them and try to loot them and these kind of things."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Dharmic kings, who prioritize spiritual welfare, with Asuric kings like Jarasandha, who focus on military conquest and material gain, detailing Jarasandha's attacks and imprisonment of 20,000 kings.

This used to be the focus of Jarasandha. Actually, in the Vedic times, a Dharmic king means his primary duty was to give protection to the citizens and to take care of their spiritual welfare, to ensure that all of them are engaged in development, developing devotional service to Krishna or Vishnu. These were the two primary functions of a Kshatriya. Economic development would be taken care of by the Vaisyas and the kings would never, the Vaishnava kings who were there like this, people like Yudhishthira and such other people, they would never look to encroach upon other kingdoms and try to annex them and try to loot them and these kind of things. That was never their goal. But Asurik kings like Jarasandha, Kamsa, they were all involved in this. They would unnecessarily attack other kings, they would loot their kingdom, they would constantly be at loggerhead with others, increasing the might of their army. This used to be their focus. They would never pay attention to the devotional life of the people. So, Jarasandha, what he did, as per his policies, he went and attacked all the surrounding kingdoms. And whoever did not ally with him, those who were not Asuras like him, he went and fought with them. And like this, he started fighting with various different kings and he started imprisoning all of them. And like this, one after another, one after another, he practically imprisoned not one or two or three or ten or even hundred, he imprisoned 20,000 kings. You can imagine the amount of military power. The military conquest Jarasandha must have been undertaking. Such an Asurik demoniac king he was.

---
#### Human Sacrifice, Jarasandha's Evil Plan, Kings' Distress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1WD495yHaWI?t=1529
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "His intention was, Jarasandha wanted to conduct a mass human sacrifice for Kalabhairava."
**Summary:** The speaker reveals Jarasandha's nefarious plan to conduct a mass human sacrifice of the 20,000 imprisoned kings to appease Kalabhairava and gain fearful powers, highlighting the kings' suffering and their desperate search for a way to escape.

Now, behind the imprisoning of these 20,000 kings, it was, Jarasandha's intention was not just some political thing, that let me catch them and imprison them, keep them in the dungeon because they are not allying with me, they are inimical to me. That was not the, just that much was not the intention. That was not the intention of Jarasandha. Jarasandha had a much bigger nefarious plan. His intention was, Jarasandha wanted to conduct a mass human sacrifice for Kalabhairava. And so for that, he was collecting all these kings and imprisoning them. And because his intention was 20,000 kings, he wanted to sacrifice to Kalabhairava. And by this, he wanted to appease him and get different kinds of various fearful powers by which he could terrorize the whole world. That was Jarasandha's larger plan. Such an evil plan it was. So now, all these 20,000 kings who were in the dungeons of Jarasandha, they were highly mistreated. They were tortured. They were kept in inhuman conditions. And they were all suffering like anything. And because of being in such conditions, they had become diseased. Their bodies had become frail. They were not able to think properly. They were not getting proper food. And they were really distressed. So all these 20,000 kings who were in the dungeon, they started, they started discussing amongst themselves how to become free from Jarasandha's grip. How to escape from his clutches. So what did the kings think of? They came up with a plan how they could become free from the clutches of Jarasandha. Actually none of them could stand in front of Jarasandha in warfare. They were not powerful enough. So they had to find somebody who would be powerful enough to defeat Jarasandha and free themselves. So what did the kings do? What plan did they think of to gain their freedom? Please join us in the next session of the Mahabharata to know about this. Thank you. Hare Krishna.

---

### Mahabharata | Part 12 | Drupada and Drona | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

---
#### revenge, warfare, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=87
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Please go and capture Drupada and bring him to me alive."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode and introduces the story of Dronacharya's demand for Gurudakshina, which involved capturing King Drupada due to their past feud.

Hare Krishna and welcome back to this episode of the Mahabharata. We have seen in the last episode how the Pandavas and Kauravas were growing up together in Hastinapura and the enmity which had started manifesting itself between the Kauravas and the Pandava brothers. When the Pandavas and the Kauravas were growing up under the tutelage of Dronacharya, when they had sufficiently grown up, Dronacharya asked the Pandavas and the Kauravas for a Gurudakshina. What was the Gurudakshina that he asked for? Before Dronacharya came to the Kaurava family to teach the children the art of warfare, he had a running feud with the king of Panchala Desha. Who was King Drupada. The eastern parts of Uttar Pradesh comprising Bareilly, Farukabad, Badayu, Kanyakubja or Kannowaj as it is known today, all these areas formed what is known as Panchala. And the capital of Panchala was Kampilya. This Kampilya is today in the place known as Kayamganj near Farooqabad. It is a small town and even today it is known by the name Kampilya. Because of this running feud, Dronacharya as Guru Dakshina, he told the Kauravas and the Pandavas, please go and capture Drupada and bring him to me alive. I have a running enmity with him and I want him to be brought before me, captured. And this is the revenge I want to take against him. The Kaurava brothers went and attacked Panchaladesha, but Drupada was no ordinary king. He was also quite powerful and he managed to repulse the attack by the Kaurava brothers. Headed by Duryodhana. Now, when the Kaurava brothers came back defeated, Arjuna alone went and attacked Panchaladesha and he defeated Drupada and brought him bound alive in front of Dronacharya.

---
#### humiliation, revenge, destiny
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=249
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "For a Kshatriya, the greatest humiliation is to be defeated in a battle and captured alive."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Arjuna's attack on Panchala, Drupada's capture, and Dronacharya's subsequent release of Drupada. Drupada, humiliated, performs a Yajna to beget a son capable of killing Dronacharya, resulting in the birth of Drishtadyumna and Draupadi.

When Arjuna attacked Panchala, he attacked a fort called Ahichatra in Panchala where Drupada was currently residing at that time. This Ahichatra is near Ramnagar in Bareilly of Uttar Pradesh and even today, some of the remains of that fort are still seen. Then Dronacharya, when Drupada was brought before him, he spoke to Drupada and told him, see, you had humiliated me and now you are in front of me bound like an animal and you are at my mercy. But I am a very liberal brahmana, so I don't want to kill you. I just wanted to teach you a lesson not to humiliate other people. And he released Drupada. Drupada went back to Panchala, but then he was very much affected by the humiliation he had undergone. For a Kshatriya, the greatest humiliation is to be defeated in a battle and captured alive. Drupada could not tolerate this and therefore he conducted a Yajna to beget a son who would have the ability and the power to kill Dronacharya because Drupada knew he himself was not powerful enough to fight with Dronacharya and defeat him and therefore he conducted a Yajna specifically to beget a son who would be powerful enough to kill Dronacharya and out of this Yajna were born a daughter and a son. The son was Drishtadyumna and the daughter was Draupadi. And when Draupadi emerged out of that fire sacrifice, there was a divine proclamation that this girl will in the future become the cause of destruction of many adharmic kings.

---
#### education, duty, brahmana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=378
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "One who is able to distinguish spirit from matter is called a Brahmana."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the location of Drupada's Yajna and then discusses Drupada's decision to send Drishtadyumna to Dronacharya for education in warfare, despite knowing Drishtadyumna was destined to kill him. He then explains the qualities of a Brahmana.

This Yajna which Drupada conducted in Kampilya is even today, if you go to the place called Kampilya near Farookabad, there is a pond which has been built there at the spot where this fire sacrifice was conducted. There is also a beautiful temple of Vishnu close by and this town of Kampilya is even today there in Uttar Pradesh. Now, when Drishtadyumna was growing up, Drupada thought, I am going to kill Dronacharya. He has to send him for education under some suitable teacher who can teach him the art of warfare. And when he contemplated upon this, he realized that no one on the earth at that point of time was as capable as Dronacharya in teaching the art of warfare. And so therefore, he sent Drishtadyumna to Dronacharya to get taught in the battle of, in the art of warfare. Drishtadyumna came to Dronacharya and he approached Dronacharya submissively bowed down before him and naturally because he was a junior person, he first introduced himself to Dronacharya. Drishtadyumna introduced himself saying that I am Drishtadyumna, the son of Drupada, born to kill you and I have come here seeking to learn the art of warfare from you. Imagine. Imagine somebody comes to you and tells you that I want to learn something from you so that I can use it to kill you. What will your response be? You will either kick him out or you will think how should I finish him now, somehow this man has come to my place, so good opportunity, let me capture him and finish him off so that he will not harm me anyway in the future. That's the natural response a person will have. But then Dronacharya was no ordinary person. He was a great Brahmana, qualified Brahmana. Not just by birth was a Brahmana but even by his qualities he was a Brahmana. The definition of the word Brahmana is brahman janatiti brahmanah. One who is able to distinguish spirit from matter is called a Brahmana. In other words, we have to understand that we are not the body, we are a spirit soul. As Lord Krishna himself says in the Bhagavad Gita, Dehino smin yatha dehe kaumaram yavanam jara, tatha dehan tara praptit dheera statra namuhyati. This is the definition of the word Brahmana.

---
#### detachment, duty, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=532
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "My duty as a teacher is to teach the knowledge that I have."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining the concept of a Brahmana, emphasizing the understanding of the soul's distinction from the changing material body, referencing Bhagavad Gita. He then explains why Dronacharya accepted Drishtadyumna as a student, focusing on his duty as a teacher and Drishtadyumna's qualifications.

An intelligent person according to Krishna is able to discriminate that I was born as a small baby, I grew up into a young boy, then I become a young man and finally I will become old and I will die one day. So even within this lifetime, I have changed so many bodies. I had the body of a small baby, then that of a young boy or a girl, then that of a young man or a young girl. I may be a young person today. In the future I will become old. So within this lifetime, I am changing so many bodies. Although the bodies are changing, I am still constant. That means I am different from all these bodies. A person who is able to distinguish himself, differentiate himself from the material body which can undergo destruction and who understands by this logic that in this life if I can change so many bodies and continue to exist, that means at the time of death, the body which I currently have at that point of time will get destroyed but I will continue to exist in some other body. A person who is able to understand this is called a brahmana. Now Dronacharya was a qualified brahmana. He very well knew that he was not the body. He was an atma. He was a spirit soul. Therefore when Drishtadyumna came in front of him with this kind of request, Dronacharya thought that what is my duty? My duty as a teacher is to teach the knowledge that I have to any qualified person who comes and seeks it of me. And this person Drishtadyumna is a qualified person. The knowledge which I will give to him, he will not misuse. He will not harm others with it. He will use it to protect the citizens. I can see that. And assessing that Drishtadyumna was a qualified disciple, Dronacharya accepted him under his tutelage and he started teaching him the art of warfare along with the Pandavas and the Kauravas. He did not discriminate against him. This shows the great liberal mind of Dronacharya.

---
#### duty, impartiality, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=658
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "It is better that I do my duty and lose my life in the process of doing my duty."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights Dronacharya's liberal-mindedness through two examples: choosing Arjuna as the greatest archer despite having a son, and teaching Drishtadyumna despite knowing he would be killed by him. He emphasizes Dronacharya's focus on merit and duty over personal considerations.

In fact two things are very indicative of the liberal mindedness of Dronacharya. Although Dronacharya had a son Ashwathama, he chose to make Arjuna the greatest archer in the world. There were many things he taught Arjuna which he did not teach Ashwathama. Why? Because Dronacharya went by merit. He saw that Arjuna had a certain dedication and a certain talent which Ashwathama his own son did not possess. As a beautiful father he taught Ashwathama whatever he had to teach and he gave him a little more facility at certain times also. He was a little more lenient because after all he was his son and nobody should criticize him saying that as a father he did not do anything for his son. Therefore he was a little more lenient with his son. But still when it came to certain very critical and advanced things Dronacharya gave that those kind of advanced knowledge to Arjuna which he did not give even to Ashwathama. This shows how Dronacharya was never partial. He went by merit and he went by what was the most appropriate thing, what duty called upon him to do. The second thing was the case of Drishtadyumna where Dronacharya considered that my duty is to teach Drishtadyumna. The result is I will get killed. Now I can refuse to teach him but still one day or the other this body has to get destroyed. I have to die one day or the other. One day or the other I have to leave this body and take on another body. So if I don't do my duty also I have to give up this body and if I do my duty also I have to give up this body. It is better that I do my duty and give up this body rather than to live with the ignominy that look at Dronacharya, he did not do his duty and to live with that ignominy is worse than death. It is better that I do my duty and lose my life in the process of doing my duty.

---
#### duty, discrimination, misuse of knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=785
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The consideration of Dronacharya was whether this knowledge will be used properly or not."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Dronacharya's acceptance of Drishtadyumna with his refusal to teach Ekalavya. He clarifies that Dronacharya's decision was not based on caste, but on his assessment that Ekalavya would misuse the knowledge, as demonstrated by his cruel act of silencing a dog with arrows.

And so saying Dronacharya very liberally he taught Drishtadyumna also. And during this time it so happened that having heard about the fame of Drishtadyumna, he came to Dronacharya and requested him to teach him the art of archery. But Dronacharya saw that Ekalavya would misuse the knowledge if it was given to him and therefore Dronacharya refused to teach Ekalavya. This is generally a misunderstood phenomenon because people think that Dronacharya refused to teach Ekalavya simply based on the caste in which he was born, which is not true. Because if Dronacharya had such considerations based on the body, he would never have taught Drishtadyumna, the art of warfare. Dronacharya was far far above all these things. The consideration of Dronacharya was whether this knowledge will be used properly or not. And Dronacharya's assessment proved true because later once when the Pandavas were going around in the forest, they came upon a dog whose mouth was filled with arrows and when they saw this, they thought who has done this? And they came upon Ekalavya and Ekalavya, he informed the Pandavas that this dog was barking, so to quieten it, I shot all these arrows into the mouth of the dog. And the Pandavas saw that Ekalavya had a bust of Dronacharya and he was learning the art of archery with still keeping Dronacharya as his guru. The Pandavas went and informed Dronacharya. Dronacharya came there and he saw what Ekalavya was doing and he told Ekalavya that Ekalavya I told you that I am not going to teach you the art of warfare. So why are you keeping my bust and still considering me as a guru and learning the art of archery? This very fact showed that Ekalavya was disobedient to his guru when Dronacharya had refused to teach him. If he had gone to some other teacher and learnt the art of warfare, it would have been different, but he refused to listen to Dronacharya. Still he kept Dronacharya as his guru and learnt. Moreover, what we can see is that Dronacharya's assessment of his future potential of Ekalavya misusing the art of warfare was seen in his silencing the dog by shooting arrows into its mouth. How to understand this? The simple question.

---
#### misuse of knowledge, protection, guru dakshina
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=946
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This assault rifle is not meant for shooting at dogs, it is meant for use in a circumstance where is there is danger to the life of the dog."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of an NSD commando misusing an assault rifle to explain Ekalavya's misuse of archery. He emphasizes that the art of archery is meant for protection, not for harming others. He then explains why Dronacharya asked for Ekalavya's thumb as guru dakshina.

The simple example is like for example there is an NSD commando. In the army the NSD commando are the elite commando forces and they are taught all kinds of how to use all kinds of automatic weapons. Now let us say NSD commando he is proficient in using the latest assault rifle, an automatic rifle and he is he has come home and he is relaxing and the neighbors dog starts barking and it disturbs him a lot. So what he does? He comes out of his house, brings his automatic rifle and pumps hundreds of bullets into the dog. What will be the result of this? The army will strip him of his NSD commandoship. They will remove him, they will suspend him from his job and they will take away the assault rifle. They will say, this assault rifle is not meant for shooting at dogs, it is meant for use in a circumstance where is there is danger to the life of the dog. You cannot use it indiscriminately like this. If the dog was disturbing you, all that you have to do was stick up a stone and throw it at it, no, you do not have to throw a stone at a dog. Even if you make a, even if you feign, as if you are going to throw a stone at the dog, the dog will quieten down or it will run away. This is natural. All of us would have experienced this at some time or the other. So similarly, when he came across a dog barking at him, all that he had to do was take a stone and throw at it. But what did he do? He decided to use that great art of archery against the dog. That poor dog became the object for Ekalavya to show off his art of archery which is not acceptable because the art of archery is a very very high level of knowledge to be utilized only for protection of people, not to harm people, whether any person, whether it is a human being or an animal. In Vedic times both human beings as well as animals were considered praja or the citizens of the country. Animals also had an equal right to be protected by the government unlike today's time. Today's time animals are sent to the slaughterhouses and all protection is given to the human beings. Vedic times was not like that. Even animals were given the right of protection. So this Ekalavya did not display that sense of protecting subordinate people or junior people. A dog is a very subordinate animal. It was worthy of his protection but instead he was shooting arrows into his mouth and he did not even consider now that all the arrows are stuck in the mouth of the dog, the poor dog how will it survive? What will it eat? So you can see from this Ekalavya was prone to misuse of the knowledge of warfare and seeing this therefore Dronacharya did not want him to commit further crimes like this and therefore Dronacharya told him okay you are considering me as a guru, you give me your thumb as guru Dakshina and he took away his thumb so that the chances of his misusing the knowledge of archery would be reduced. Another thing Dronacharya could have easily done was to kill him, but Dronacharya was not so keen to kill somebody. He thought why I have to kill someone. Let me just take away his thumb. That is enough to prevent him from misusing the knowledge he has and this was the reason for Dronacharya to do this.

---
#### impartiality, envy, friendship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=1172
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We can see in the character of Dronacharya there was no question of partiality and he never made decisions based on the consideration of the body."
**Summary:** The speaker refutes the misconceptions about Dronacharya's actions towards Ekalavya, emphasizing his impartiality and focus on the proper use of knowledge. He then transitions to discussing the growing enmity between the Kauravas and Pandavas, fueled by Karna, Shakuni, and Duryodhana's envy.

Unlike the mispropaganda that is spread everywhere today that Dronacharya on the basis of caste, he decided to stop the Ekalavya from becoming a good archer. Or another misconception is that Dronacharya was partial to Arjuna and therefore he did not want Ekalavya to become the topmost archer, which is not true. We can see in the character of Dronacharya there was no question of partiality and he never made decisions based on the consideration of the body. This we have seen earlier itself from the example of Drishtadyumna and Ashwathama. So now, the Pandavas, so this showed the great characteristic and quality of Dronacharya. Now, when the Pandavas and the Kauravas, they had sufficiently grown up, we have also seen how there was some amount of enmity between the Kauravas and the Pandavas because Karna was also almost equal to Arjuna, but Arjuna was still better than Karna in many aspects of archery. Karna also did not get along well with the Pandavas and as we know, foes foe is a friend. Duryodhana struck up a friendship with Karna and Karna became a steadfast friend of Duryodhana and Duryodhana, he was being guided by Gandhari's brother Shakuni, who was not a person of good character. He was a cunning person and he was a wild person. He was always his, Shakuni's eyes were always on the throne, that somehow or the other, my nephew should get the throne and Karna was also not a man of good character and so these two people started counselling Duryodhana and thus, they both started feeding, already Duryodhana was burning with envy towards the Pandavas. Now, these two personalities started feeding that fire of envy in Duryodhana's heart and so Duryodhana's heart started burning. And so Duryodhana's heart turned to a fire of pan-d survives itself becoming more and more envious and inimical towards the Pandavas. Now, Duryodhana, he realized that direct confrontation with the Pandavas would not yield any result because he had seen how, against even Drupada, he had been defeated but Arjuna had single handedly defeated Drupada. Arjuna, one brother, was enough to defeat the entire Kauravas. Now they were five brothers. Look at the strength of Bhima and then there was Yudhishthira, Nakula, Sahadeva. They were also not any less warriors. Therefore Duryodhana understood that direct confrontation would not work out.

---
#### plotting, deception, danger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/F6KbDAQAQrM?t=1348
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The intention of Duryodhana was that shellac is a very inflammable material. He would on some pretext send the Pandavas to stay in this house of shellac in Varanavata and his idea was to set it ablaze."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Duryodhana's plan, hatched with Shakuni, to eliminate the Pandavas by building a house of shellac in Varanavata and setting it on fire. Dhritarashtra also participates in the deception by inviting the Pandavas to stay there.

And so based on the advice of Shakuni, Duryodhana hatched a plan to eliminate the Pandavas. Duryodhana had a house built of shellac in a place called Varanavata. This Varanavata is in current day Haryana and today it is known as Barnava. Even today if you go to this place Barnava, the place where the house of shellac stood, the palace of shellac stood is a big mound of earth and this is the place where the house of shellac was built by Duryodhana with a worker named Purochana. The intention of Duryodhana was that shellac is a very inflammable material. He would on some pretext send the Pandavas to stay in this house of shellac in Varanavata and his idea was to set it ablaze and the Pandavas would die in the inferno and he could announce to the world that the Pandavas have died in a fire accident. That was the plan he hatched with Shakuni and Dhritarashtra and with this intention he created this house of shellac and Dhritarashtra also called the Pandavas and he told them that Duryodhana has built a nice palace for all of you. So for a few days you please go there and have some leisure time, relax there and come back after a few days. Like this Dhritarashtra told the Pandavas. The Pandavas were unaware of the plotting by Duryodhana and so they thought uncle is telling us to go so let us all go and so they along with the Pandavas along with Kunti Devi set out to go to Varanavata. Now, what happened to the Pandavas when they went to Varanavata? Could they escape from the house of Shellac? How did they, how could they escape when they had no knowledge of what was going to happen? Join us in our next session of Mahabharata to learn about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna. Jagat Guru Shri La Pravupada Ki Jai, Netai Gor, Pemanande Hari Hari Bol. Copyright © 2020 Mooji Media Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

#### Mahabharata, game of dice, plot
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=92
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana, Shakuni, Karna, and Dhritarashtra, Dushyasana, all these people combined together to lay out a scheme, a plot, how to trap the Pandavas and take their kingdom by cheating."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, highlighting the beginning of the dice game and the conspiracy hatched by Duryodhana, Shakuni, and others to cheat the Pandavas out of their kingdom.

And welcome to today's episode of the Mahabharata. In the previous episode, we have seen the beginning of one of the key moments in the history of the world, in the history of the Mahabharata, a pivotal point in the whole narration of the Mahabharata epic, the game of die. We saw how Duryodhana and Shakuni, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Karna, and Dhritarashtra, Dushyasana, all these people combined together to lay out a scheme, a plot, how to trap the Pandavas and take their kingdom by cheating. Because the Pandavas were too powerful for direct confrontation. We also saw how Yudhishthiram had a great power over the Mahabharata. He was hard excited and eager to turn that game around. He almost stepped out when Dhritarashtra killed him. Udhishthira Maharaj was unwilling to participate in that game of dice, but he was bound by the instructions of his elder, Dhritarashtra. Even though Yudhishthira tried to wriggle out of the trap set by Duryodhana and Shakuni by refusing to play with Shakuni, Duryodhana, on the plea of playing himself made Shakuni catch the dice on his behalf.

While all this was going on the people who are witnessing were not very happy with the way things were proceeding they could see that the whole game was rigged but then dhritarashtra the emperor was sitting there and he was not saying anything and the king's word is final so people did not know what to do they were all talking amongst themselves like this the whole assembly was then vidura started talking to dhritarashtra and he advised him in various ways that this was unfair and it was not the right thing to do and viva could foresee that this is going to lead to the destruction of the Kaurava dynasty because if by unfair means the pundavas unnecessarily the pandavas are angered then eventually it would lead to war and if it led to war and the pandavas their power was so their power they they were so mighty that the Kauravas would not be able to stand in front of them.

#### injustice, silence, helplessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=540
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Despite Vidura's so many good advice, he would never accept it. He always was under the impression that Vidura, he is, is partial towards the Pandavas and he does not think of my welfare. Although in reality Vidura was talking for his own good."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the outrage at the suggestion of staking Draupadi, Vidura's failed attempts to intervene, and Duryodhana's rejection of wise counsel, ultimately leading to Draupadi being put at stake and lost.

Daughter-in-law of the Kaurava household. How can her honor be put at stake? And now Vidura could not tolerate it. He spoke very heavily against Duryodhana and told Dhritarashtra, please stop this. Don't do this. Now, what happens is when Duryodhana heard this, Duryodhana could not broke it, whatever Vidura was saying, and he started chastising him very severely, reprimanding him very severely. Because it is said that, if advice is given to a foolish person for his own welfare, instead of shanta, instead of it making him peaceful, it will only anger him. An intelligent person, when he receives the advice of a foolish person, he will only anger him. When he receives good advice, he will take it very peacefully and he will dwell on it, he will contemplate on it and try to make use of that advice. But a foolish person will think, this advice is for my detrimental, it is detrimental to my interest. And when good advice is given, he will not accept it. Duryodhana was like that. Despite Vidura's so many good advice, he would never accept it. He always was under the impression that Vidura, he is, is partial towards the Pandavas and he does not think of my welfare. Although in reality Vidura was talking for his own good. Duryodhana very severely reprimanded Vidura and Dhritarashtra was silent. He did not take any action. So Vidura also felt silent because he also became helpless. And so now Draupadi was put at stake. And again the dice was rolled. And like all the previous times, again Yudhishthira Maharaj lost.

#### humiliation, revenge, slavery

**Summary:** The assembly is in uproar after Draupadi is lost. Karna, remembering past insults, orders Draupadi to be brought to the servant's quarters, viewing her and the Pandavas as slaves.

Now everything was gone. Nothing was left. The whole assembly was completely in uproar. Nobody was happy with what was happening, except for three, four people. Dushyasana, Karna, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Dhritarashtra. These were the only people who were really happy. Nobody else was happy. Now Karna started remembering the insult that Draupadi had made upon him in the Swayambhara where in front of everyone she had said that Karna, because of being born in a Sudra family, he could not participate in the Swayambhara. So he immediately told Duryodhana, now Draupadi is a slave. The Pandavas have lost themselves. All the Yudhishthira and Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, all the Pandavas have lost themselves. Now Duryodhana, Karna, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Dhritarashtra, Karna, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, all the Pandavas have lost themselves. They are all our slaves. And even Draupadi has been lost in the game of dice. So bring her, why she should stay in the royal household. Please bring her and let us send her to the quarters, the servant's quarters. Let her stay with all the maidservants. So our servant was sent to bring Draupadi from her inner chambers.

**Summary:** Draupadi, unprepared and concerned about propriety, questions the fairness of her being staked, sending a messenger back to the assembly with a crucial inquiry about the order in which Yudhishthira lost himself and her.

Now Draupadi had not dressed herself yet. Generally when we we are at home, we will be wearing some casual dress, we will not be wearing our formal dress. So Draupadi was in her casual dress, she was not planning to come out, so she was not dressed formally. So she was not in a position to come to the assembly when called for and when she heard from the messenger that like this, in the game of dice, Yudhishthira Maharaj had lost all the brothers. He had lost himself and he had lost Draupadi also and now Draupadi was told to come to the assembly so that she could be sent to the servant's quarters. She sent the messenger back with a question asking whether she had been fairly lost in the bet, whether she had been fairly lost in the game of dice. Did Yudhishthira stake her and lose her and then he lost himself or he first staked himself and then he lost himself. He staked Draupadi later in which case the whole, his losing Draupadi becomes questionable.

#### assault, disregard, shame
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=825
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Dushyasana forcibly entered into the women's quarters...caught hold of Draupadi by her hair, by her tresses and he started dragging her to the assembly."
**Summary:** Duryodhana orders Dushyasana to bring Draupadi. Dushyasana forcibly drags her to the assembly by her hair, disregarding her pleas and violating protocol.

Now when the messenger came back and asked this question, nobody had a proper answer. So Duryodhana told Dushyasana, what is all this, you know asking various people to bring her, you go and bring her. So Dushyasana forcibly entered into the women's quarters. Actually men are not supposed to enter the women's quarters. Enter into the women's section of the palace wherever they are staying. The men's and the women's quarters would be completely different. But he forcibly entered there, caught hold of Draupadi by her hair, by her tresses and he started dragging her to the assembly. Draupadi was, she was objecting, she was saying, I am not dressed properly, I can't come in this state. There are so many elderly people like Bhishma, Drona, Dhritarashtra, all such personalities, all such personalities. They are there. How can I come like this? But he would not listen. He just dragged her forcibly into the assembly and pulled her on the floor of the assembly. And when Bhishma saw this, when Drona saw this, when Vidura saw this, they were beside themselves with tears. Actually it was not an insult of Draupadi, it was an insult of the entire Kaurava dynasty.

#### inaction, silence, degradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=902
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Dhritarashtra was not willing to listen. He was not willing to go against Duryodhana. Now who is going to stop them?"
**Summary:** Dhritarashtra remains unwilling to act against Duryodhana, and Vidura's efforts are futile. The assembly is in turmoil as Draupadi is humiliated.

Duryodhana did not understand this. Dhritarashtra was not willing to listen. He was not willing to go against Duryodhana. Now who is going to stop them? Vidura is trying his best. Dhritarashtra would not listen to Vidura, nor would he listen to Bhishma, nor would he listen to Drona. He would not listen to anyone except Duryodhana. So, everybody, nobody knew what to do. Everybody was beside themselves and the assembly, big furor in the assembly. How can Draupadi be brought here? So many people were objecting. Some people were speaking in favor of Duryodhana. Some people were objecting. And when she was brought like this, then Duryodhana started telling her, now your husbands have lost you. Now you are a slave. You please go to the servants quarters and be with the maid servants. And then Karna told her, told Draupadi, Draupadi, now all your husbands, they have all lost you. They have all become our slaves. Now you pick up somebody else as your husband. These people are no more your slaves. And these people are no more your husbands. Like this, such piercing words were being used. Draupadi was weeping and she was inconsolable. And she looked at Drona, she looked at Bhishma, Vidura, none of them are saying anything. Then she started talking to Dhritarashtra, oh my king, what is this? The honor of the entire Kaurava dynasty is being brought to the, in front of all the public like this? Is this the way to treat Dhritarashtra? Is this the way to treat the women folk of the Kaurava dynasty?

#### dharma, confusion, helplessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=991
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma said, my dear daughter, I am unable to answer you because although Yudhishthira lost himself first and in one sense what you are saying is right, at the same time, no matter in what situation a man is, his wife is still his own."
**Summary:** Draupadi questions Dhritarashtra and Bhishma about the legality of her being staked after Yudhishthira lost himself. Bhishma admits his confusion and inability to provide a clear answer.

Dhritarashtra was silent. Then she asked Dhritarashtra, please tell me, if Yudhishthira Maharaj had lost himself before staking me, then how is it right for him to stake me further and then lose me? Because he has already lost himself. He is no more in a position to stake anybody else. Now she asked Bhishma. Bhishma said, my dear daughter, I am unable to answer you because although Yudhishthira lost himself first and in one sense what you are saying is right, at the same time, no matter in what situation a man is, his wife is still his own. Just because Yudhishthira lost himself in the bed doesn't mean that he lost you also. Even after he became the slave of the Kauravas, of Duryodhana and others, he is still your husband. And so he had the right to stake you. So this is also there. At the same time, this logic is also there that Yudhishthira, having staked himself, had no more right to continue playing. So I am confused. I am not able to give a proper answer.

**Summary:** Vikarna, Duryodhana's brother, bravely objects to the treatment of Draupadi, declaring that she was not fairly lost and should not be made a maidservant.

And so now there was nobody was able to say anything. At that time. At that time, one of Duryodhana's brothers, Vikarna, he stood up and he objected. He said, Draupadi, her honor is being put on the floor like this. I am not for this. He objected very strongly. And he said, according to me, Yudhishthira Maharaj, the one, the moment he had lost himself in the game of dice, the game of dice was over. There was no more question of betting and there was no more question of further staking Draupadi. So Draupadi is not lost. She cannot be brought into the ashram. She cannot be made a maid servant. I oppose this. Vikarna spoke very strongly, although he was a younger brother of Duryodhana.

#### restraint, fury, helplessness

**Key Quote:** "Bhima was beside himself with fury. He could not control himself. But then what to do? He was bound by his elder brother's words."
**Summary:** Dushyasana begins to disrobe Draupadi. Bhima is consumed by fury but is restrained by Yudhishthira's adherence to dharma and their status as slaves.

He was also a wild man. Immediately, he was also waiting for an opportunity. Immediately, he caught hold of Draupadi's sari and started pulling at it. Now Bhima. He was waiting. He was beside himself with fury. He could not control himself. But then what to do? He was bound by his elder brother's words. And his elder brother, because he is so much fixed in virtuosity and dharma that he is not going to go against his word. He had already given his word. He had lost himself and his brothers. So he was not in a position to say anything against what Duryodhana and others were saying because they were now their slaves. So Yudhishthira Maharaj could not say anything. He could not do anything. And Bhima was bound by his words. And Arjuna started pacifying Bhima. Calm down. Calm down. Don't become too angry now. Now is not the right time. Our hands are tied. So like this, Arjuna was constantly pacifying Bhima. And so now, the brothers were not able to do anything. And in the whole assembly, Dushyasana started trying to pacify Bhima.

---
#### divine intervention, surrender, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=1222
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Then she remembered one person who is the refuge of all people who are completely shelterless. That is the Supreme Personality of God and Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** Dushyasana attempts to strip Draupadi. Seeing her husbands unable to help and the assembly silent, Draupadi remembers Lord Krishna and begins to call out to him for help.

She started trying to strip Draupadi by pulling at her saree. And Draupadi, she is trying to protect herself. She is trying to protect her honor. She is holding on with great effort to her, with great strength to her saree. But this man, Dushyasana is such a well-built, powerful guy. And Draupadi is such a delicate woman. She is a delicate princess. What she will fight against this, this bulky, hefty man full of muscles and full of strength. So she tried her best to hold the saree but she was not able to. And she saw that her husbands are not in a position to help her. She saw that the entire assembly, nobody is speaking against this injustice. So what to do now? Then she remembered one person who is the refuge of all people who are completely shelterless. That is the Supreme Personality of God and Lord Krishna. So she started calling out to Krishna. Krishna! Krishna! She started calling out. But then this Dushyasana is not giving up. She is holding on. She is calling out to Krishna but nothing is happening. Then finally she realized, I am holding on to this saree with all my effort. But ultimately I cannot protect myself. In this assembly when such big, big people are there, my husbands are such great heroes, such great warriors, they are not able to protect me. What? I will protect myself. My only shelter is Lord Krishna. So she let go of her saree. She raised her hands and started calling out, O Govinda! O Gopijanavallada! O Hari Murari! Like that she started calling out to Krishna.

---
#### divine grace, surrender, miracle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=1328
**Verse References:** BG 15.15
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The moment she gave up everything else, her dependence on her husbands, her dependence on her own abilities, she gave up everything and totally surrendered to Krishna. Krishna as Paramatma immediately sensed that surrender"
**Summary:** Draupadi, realizing her helplessness, surrenders completely to Krishna. Krishna, as Paramatma, responds to her surrender and miraculously protects her honor by providing an endless supply of fabric, exhausting Dushyasana.

The moment she started calling out to Krishna, Krishna, although you are so far away in Dwaraka, but then Krishna, as he says in the Bhagavad Gita, Sarvasya Chaham Hrithi Sannivishtaha. Krishna is seated in every living entity's heart as Paramatma. So the moment she gave up everything else, her dependence on her husbands, her dependence on her own abilities, she gave up everything and totally surrendered to Krishna. Krishna as Paramatma immediately sensed that surrender and although Krishna was not there, Krishna was seated in Dwaraka. He knew everything of what was happening. And then he remembered his promise to Draupadi for the service that she had rendered to him. And Krishna decided now is the time to repay the debt to Draupadi. And so what happened? After that, Dushyasana pulled and pulled and pulled at her saree. The saree just kept coming and coming and the yards and the yards of cloth is being piled up on the floor. The cloth is piling on. Minute after minute, hour after hour he is pulling. Only the cloth is piling up but Draupadi is not getting disrobed. And pulling, pulling, pulling, finally what happened? Dushyasana lost all his strength and his stamina came to an end and he sat down on the floor perspiring in the midst of all the cloth. He was not able to understand what is happening, from where all this cloth is coming. And the whole assembly erupted in an uproar when they saw this wonderful miracle that was happening. Everybody understood how Krishna had saved the honour of Draupadi.
