### Mahabharata | Part 32 | The aftermath of Game of Dice | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### Dharma, Duty, Injustice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=96
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So what use are such masters who do not protect the honour of their servants? So does that not free the Pandavas from their binding to their words?"
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the events following the game of dice, focusing on the moral obligations of the Pandavas and Kauravas, particularly regarding Draupadi's honor and the concept of duty.

Hare Krishna and welcome to today's episode of the Mahabharata. In the last episode, we have seen that very painful incident of the game of dice, where Draupadi was attempted to be stripped naked by the Kauravas, headed by Karna, Duryodhana, Dushyasana and others. And although Dushyasana tried his best to disrobe Draupadi, but then Draupadi surrendered herself completely to Lord Krishna, and Krishna in turn protected her modesty. And so, Dushyasana pulled and pulled at her Sari with great anticipation, but that anticipation turned to amazement when he saw an endless supply of her Sari covered cloth and that amazement turned into frustration that frustration became despair finally he sat down fatigued unable to disrobe Draupadi now when the incidents had gone this far Dhritarashtra had a change of heart what did Dhritarashtra do that is what we will be discussing today before we come to that we need to understand a little bit about the whole egregious happenings in that game of dice we all have understood how Yudhishtira was compelled to participate in the game of dice not because he was inclined to it but because it was the order of his superior Dhritarashtra whom he considered as good as his father now although Yudhishtira knew that this was a trap laid out to hurt him and his brothers Yudhishtira could not help it he was looking towards Dhritarashtra's intervention to correct the injustice that was happening during the game of dice but Dhritarashtra was silent all through now the question comes that when Draupadi was brought into that assembly and when her honor was being sullied by the Kauravas why did Yudhishtira and the other Pandava brothers not act was it not their duty to protect the honour of their wife? Yes, it was their duty to the extent that their powers authorized them to protect her.

---
#### Duty, Kshatriya Dharma, Leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=322
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Kshatriya is the person who protects his citizens from any kind of danger."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the duties of a Kshatriya, particularly the responsibility to protect citizens, and contrasts this with Duryodhana's failure to uphold his dharma as a king, while highlighting Dhritarashtra's violation of his duty to ensure justice for the Pandavas.

The fact is, that the Pandavas having lost all their kingdom and having been stripped of their Kshatriya position, having become servants of Duryodhana, no more were they, in a position, to be able to provide protection to others. Protecting others, protecting the citizens is the duty of the Kshatriya. Kshata trayate yati kshatriya. The Kshatriya is the person who protects his citizens from any kind of danger. It is the Kshatriya king's responsibility to give protection to all his citizens. Now that the Pandavas were no longer kings, they were mere servants, they were mere ordinary citizens, under the rule of Duryodhana. Now it was Duryodhana's duty as a king to protect the honor of the Pandavas, who are now also his citizens. Now if Duryodhana is not following his duty, if he is deviating, if he is flagrantly flouting the codes of Dharma, according to which he has to protect the honor of his citizens and prevent any kind of danger, then he is a king. Any harm to them, whether physically or economically or in terms of their honor, if Duryodhana, the king himself is flouting these duties, then what can the citizen do? So the Pandavas, in fact it was the duty of Duryodhana to protect Draupadi. He was not keeping up his duty. It was the duty of Dhritarashtra to ensure that there was no injustice to the Pandavas and even he violated his duty. Now because Duryodhana and Dhritarashtra and Karna and Dushyasana, all these people they flouted their dharma, their duty does not mean that the Pandavas also should forget their duty. Actually this was the biggest difference between Yudhishthira and the other Pandava brothers and Duryodhana.

---
#### Dharma, Means vs. Ends, Virtue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "On the one hand, Duryodhana was ready to violate every rule of dharma at the drop of a hat."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Duryodhana's focus on achieving his desires by any means necessary with the Pandavas' commitment to upholding dharma even in the face of great adversity, emphasizing that the Pandavas were deprived of their authority to protect others when they lost their kingdom.

On the one hand, Duryodhana was ready to violate every rule of dharma at the drop of a hat. Why? Because his whole consideration was, his whole focus was the end. What I will get? What I want to get? How do I satisfy my desire? The means did not matter to him. He was ready to achieve his desire by hook or by crook. It did not matter to him. It did not matter to him that he was violating the codes of dharma. It did not matter to him that he was violating the rules of the Vedas. It did not matter to him that he was incurring grievous sin because of which he would have to suffer in the future. None of these things mattered to him. For him, all that mattered was, somehow I have to get Indraprastha in my hand. Somehow I have to insult the Pandavas. Somehow I have to insult Draupadi. This was his whole focus. Now if you look at the Pandavas, the Pandavas were reluctant to violate the codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. Words of virtuousness, even in the face of the greatest calamity. Even when Draupadi the wife's honor was put at stake, even there the Pandavas' consideration was, as per the codes of Dharma. What is our duty now? What are the steps that we can take? Or what are the things we should not be doing? So Uddistira Maharaj, from the Pandava's consideration. If we look at it from there. from that perspective, the duty was resting with Duryodhana and with Dhritarashtra to protect Draupadi's honor because she is also their citizen. They had been deprived of their authority to perform that function of protecting others the moment they were shown of their kingdom and they were converted into ordinary servants.

---
#### Duty, Vedic Culture, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=617
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though a person may be very well capable of defending himself, still in Vedic times the people would consider, is it my duty to defend myself or is it somebody else's duty."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Vishwamitra Muni seeking help from Dasaratha Maharaj to illustrate the Vedic concept of assigned duties within a social structure, where even capable individuals rely on others to fulfill their specific responsibilities.

We have examples of this in the Vedic history. Even though a person may be very well capable of defending himself, still in Vedic times the people would consider, is it my duty to defend myself or is it somebody else's duty. When Vishwamitra Muni was performing tapasya's and yajnas in the forest, there was a demoness who was coming and creating trouble for him, not allowing him to carry out his spiritual duty. Vishwamitra Muni is such a powerful sage. Not only that, before Vishwamitra Muni became the sage, he had been a king, a very powerful valiant king. So, it is not that he was unaware of the techniques of combat, how to fight, where to fight, with an asura, with a demoness. It was not unknown to him. Not only that, as a sage, he had powerful mantras by the dint of his austerities with which he could have easily killed the demoness. But he did not do so. He considered, my duty as a sage, what is my duty? My duty is to engage in spiritual activities. Whose duty is it? To ensure that my spiritual duty is fulfilled. That is his duty. His duties are carried on without any obstruction. It is the duty of the king. And therefore, Vishwamitra Muni went to Dasaratha Maharaj, who was ruling the kingdom at that time and he informed him, like this, there is this demoness who is creating trouble for me. So, you have to please solve my problem.

---
#### Dharma, Duty, Varnasrama Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=836
**Verse References:** BG 3.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There is a big difference between occupation for one's livelihood and one's duty."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vedic social structure with the modern world, emphasizing the difference between one's occupation (vritti) and one's duty (dharma), highlighting the various duties individuals have towards society, family, and even animals.

This is because during those times, in the Vedic tradition and the Sanatana Dharma, the duties of different persons in the social functioning of the society was very well laid out. In today's world, unfortunately, that societal structure has completely collapsed of the Varnasthama Dharma. Now we do not have an understanding of what is duty at all. We think we mistake our vritti, our occupation to be Dharma, to be Karma, which is not correct. There is a big difference between vritti and Dharma. There is a big difference between occupation for one's livelihood and one's duty. we have duties towards all other rest of the society. We have duties towards our parents, we have duties towards our forefathers, we have duties towards the sages, who have given us transcendental knowledge, we have duties towards devatas, who are supplying us all kinds of natural facilities, such as sunlight and air and water etc. For our livelihood. we have duties towards all other domestic, even to domestic, animals like the cow and the dog etc which render us service we have responsibilities we have duties we have obligations towards our other relatives it may be my husband it may be my wife it may be my sister my other relatives my friends whoever helps me in various activities i have obligations towards all of them there are duties prescribed in the vedas for all this so duty is completely different from occupation for one's livelihood one may give up one occupation and take on another occupation but one cannot give up one's duty and as lord krishna himself says in the bhagavad-gita one must not give up one's duty however imperfectly performed to take up the duty of some other person however perfectly they may be able to do it.

#### Dharma, Duty, Virtue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=959
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though you are assigned the duty you are able to do imperfectly it is better than doing some other person's duty which is not assigned to you very perfectly this is the code of dharma"
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the importance of adhering to one's assigned duty, even if performed imperfectly, over taking on another's duty, and contrasts the Pandavas' adherence to dharma with Duryodhana's focus solely on achieving his desired results.

This is against the laws of the scripture i may be able to do my duty say with 50 efficiency but i look at somebody else's assigned duty i can do that much better so let me do that no even though you are you are assigned the duty you are able to do imperfectly it is better than doing some other person's duty which is not assigned to you very perfectly this is the code of dharma and so the pandavas always even in that calamitous situation were considering what is virtuosity dictating to me what should i do and what should not i what should i not do is this my duty to protect draupadi or is it not it is the duty of actually the emperor the king dhritarashtra and duryodhana were responsible for her protection and if they are not performing their duty does not mean that i should violate my duty i should give up my duty this is a very big difference between duryodhana and dhritarashtra and the kauravas when compared with the pandavas duryodhana never had such consideration he never thought what should i do and what should i not do is my activity according to dharma or not he was simply focused on the result somehow i have to get the result the means don't matter the only the end matters whereas for the pandavas the end did not matter no matter how they were insulted whether they lost their kingdom whether they lost the honor of their wife whatever it was they would not deviate from the means the end was immaterial to them.

**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of why Bhima and Arjuna followed Yudhishthira's lead, emphasizing the importance of respecting the eldest brother's guidance in matters of dharma and highlighting Draupadi's understanding and acceptance of the Pandavas' adherence to dharma.

Now the question comes okay yudhisthira gave his word now what was the necessity for dhima and arjuna to come to follow yudhisthira's this one he has given his word why could not dhima or arjuna jump in and then protect draupadi was following the instructions of their brother more important than protecting the honor of draupadi the fact is that draupadi is also the wife of yudhisthira so there is the responsibility equal now as far as the vedic culture was concerned the eldest person had the responsibility of guiding the younger people on the path of dharma the younger people always looked up to the elder people for guidance so everybody knew in that assembly that yudhisthira maharaj was dharma raja he would never deviate from the path of dharma his brothers also knew that and nobody knew the intricacies of dharma better than yudhisthira and so therefore the younger brothers dhima arjuna and others even though they could see such a difference between dharma and yudhisthira injustice being meted out in that assembly they held on to their firm faith in their brother yudhisthira maharaj that he knows the codes of dharma very well and he will tell us what is the real dharmic duty that they had to perform at that point of time so even dhima and arjuna were also very clear in their mind we have to do what is our ordained duty we cannot we cannot flout the the requirements of our duty we cannot violate the laws of dharma so therefore the bandavas are the most are the most pious people they are the most praiseworthy people in fact when the game of dice ended in this kind of fiasco even though the yudhisthira maharaj lost in that game of dice even though draupadi was about to be proved but lord krishna had to step in to save her the fact is even draupadi had no resentment in her heart towards yudhisthira maharaj she had full confidence that whatever yudhisthira maharaj has done was within the codes of dharma he could clearly understand that even dhima and arjuna were bound by the ropes of dharma of virtuosity and so therefore she had no resentment in her heart because she was also equally disposed to follow the course of Dharma rather than look at what was the result that had accrued out of that following of that Dharma. She had no resentment in her heart that she, Yudhisthira Maharaj and Arjuna and Jima, they followed the path of Dharma and they ditched me. There was no such resentment in Draupadi's heart. On the other hand, she was completely proud of her husband that even in that position, they followed the path of Dharma.

#### Greed, Lust, Consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=1260
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That was too much. Dhritarashtra also, Dhritarashtra realized that."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Dhritarashtra's dual personality and his motivations, suggesting that he may not have intended for the situation to escalate to the point of attempting to disrobe Draupadi, while highlighting Duryodhana's greed, lust, and desire to insult the Pandavas.

So finally, Dhritarashtra, when he saw that things had gone too far, maybe Dhritarashtra's whole thing was, as we have seen, his dual personality. He had love for the Pandavas, he had love for his younger brother, as well as he had too much love for Gudiyodhana and the throne. And that love for Duryodhana and the throne overwhelmed his love for the Pandavas. So maybe Dhritarashtra had no intention to insult the Pandavas like that. His intention would have been to just get the kingdom and be happy with that. Although actually even Indraprastha was not his. Hastinapura itself, the throne of Hastinapura which he and Duryodhana were occupying, that did not belong to them. That was actually, you can look at it, because of the magnanimity of the Pandavas. They were able to occupy that throne. Otherwise, the Pandavas were the rightful heirs to that throne, not Duryodhana, not Dhritarashtra. But look at the greed of Duryodhana and Dhritarashtra when the Pandavas, from scratch, they built up their own kingdom. They wanted to go and occupy that. They were committing that. So Dhritarashtra's eye was on the opulence of Indraprastha. But even he didn't want to go and occupy that. He wanted to go and occupy that. He wanted to go and occupy that. He had not expected that it would go to the extent of trying to disrobe Draupadi. That was too much. Dhritarashtra also, Dhritarashtra realized that. But by that time, before he could step in, already the act had happened. Everything was happening very swiftly. You see, now we are looking at that whole event in hindsight. But when they were in that, in that whole assembly, when everything was going on, people sometimes have brain fakes. You know, that's a common terminology we use nowadays. Some people have brain fakes. Sometimes some people, even though they have full understanding and the knowledge to be able to act properly in a situation, sometimes people, out of some brain fade at that point of time, they do something which is contrary to what should be done. That was, similar thing could have happened with Dhritarashtra also. Because of which he did not step in at the right time. But when it had gone to this extent, and when he saw Bhima taking vow after terrible vow, that was the end. That was the end. That shook Dhritarashtra out of his slumber, weak slumber that he was in and he realized things had gone too far and he understood that this was something which he had not bargained for. Whereas, you see, in the case of Duryodhana, he wanted, he coveted their kingdom also and he also wanted to insult the Pandavas and he wanted to satisfy his lusty desire towards Draupadi. He got that opportunity and he tried to make, take full advantage of that. So, Dhritarashtra knew about Duryodhana's love for the throne, but he did not know about his lusty desires towards Draupadi. If he had known earlier, probably he would have taken steps to prevent this. But anyway, things went out of hand and things had gone too far.

#### Conciliation, Consequences, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=1451
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dhritarashtra woke up and then he immediately put a stop to everything."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Dhritarashtra's attempt to rectify the situation by returning everything to the Pandavas and seeking forgiveness, while acknowledging the inevitable consequences of the events that transpired, and concludes with the Pandavas' departure and a cliffhanger about those remaining in the assembly hall.

Dhritarashtra woke up and then he immediately put a stop to everything. And what did Dhritarashtra do? He, in order to pacify the Pandavas, he returned everything that the Pandavas had lost. He returned the kingdom to Yudhishthira Maharaj and then he begged them to forgive Duryodhana and others for whatever had happened and he told them, let us forget and forgive. Although at heart, Dhritarashtra also knew that this is not something which the Pandavas are going to forget, nor is Bhima going to forget as well. This is, this is going to create lot of, this is going to create a big war in the future and all my sons are going to be in great peril. Dhritarashtra knew this, but still he was trying to use conciliatory word to try to, as far as possible to postpone the inevitable. That was Dhritarashtra's mode. And then Dhritarashtra, he said. He was a great man. He was a great man. in front of publicly in front of everyone he announced that Yudhishthira I must he actually he commended the Pandavas he commended Yudhishthira Maharaj for standing by virtuosity by standing by Dharma even in such great distress and having trying to and he used many conciliatory words and tried to console Draupadi also and then finally he he dissolved the assembly and asked everyone to go back to their respective places so the Pandavas accompanied by Draupadi with a very heavy heart they took their belongings mounted on their chariots and started back to Indraprastha their minds completely disturbed and their hearts heavy with the insult that they had faced with the dishonor that they had suffered from the Pandavas and they tried to console Draupadi with the insult that they had suffered from the Pandavas and they tried to console Draupadi however we have to understand that when a person gets insulted on account of some mistake he has done it is a cause for great pain but the Pandavas underwent such great dishonor not for any fault of theirs so there was no shame on their part the shame entirely rested on Duryodhana Karna Dushyasana and all the Dhritarastha and all these people so now when the assembly was dissolved they were all in a state of great pain and they were all in a state of great pain and they resolved everybody left to their respective residences and slowly the hall petered out and it became empty the Pandavas left for Indraprastha Bhishma went back to his quarters Vidura went back to his palace Dhritarastha went back to his room and the entire hall all the people who had assembled slowly they all left the assembly hall gradually the assembly hall became empty except for a few people who are these people and what were they waiting in that hall for join us in our next episode to know about this thank you for joining us today Hare Krishna Jagat Guru Srila Prabhupada Ki Jai Netai Gor Premanande Hari Hari Bol Jai Jai Jai Guru

#### Mahabharata, game of dice, conflict
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=95
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the previous episode we have seen how after the game of dice, good sense prevailed over Dhritarashtra and he returned all those things which had been wrongfully, by way of cheating, had been taken away from the Pandavas by Shakuni and Duryodhana."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, where Dhritarashtra returned the Pandavas' possessions after the dice game, but the Pandavas are still troubled by the insults they endured.

In the previous episode we have seen how after the game of dice, good sense prevailed over Dhritarashtra and he returned all those things which had been wrongfully, by way of cheating, had been taken away from the Pandavas by Shakuni and Duryodhana. After the game of dice, after Dhritarashtra returned everything and he spoke a few conciliatory words to Draupadi and to the Pandavas, he dissolved the assembly and everyone left the assembly hall. After everyone left the assembly hall, a few people remained back and they were looking at each other and who are these people and what were their thoughts. Stay with us today to learn about that. The Pandavas after leaving the assembly, they stepped out of the place and they got onto their chariots and they left Hastinapura. They drove back to Indraprastha. Their hearts were heavy. Their thoughts were filled with all those words of insults and the attempt to disrobe Draupadi. The Pandavas were still fresh in their hearts and the wounds were still festering. The Pandavas, although at that moment, at that point of time, there was no way they could vent out their anger and frustration, carrying these heavy thoughts in their mind, they were going back to Indraprastha. After they had gone out of Hastinapura and they had travelled some distance, they were told that somebody was coming on a horseback trying to catch up with them. Soon the person who was on horseback caught up with the Pandavas and they saw that this person on horseback was one of the servants from the Hastinapura palace. This servant, he came with a message to Yudhishthira Maharaj and to the Pandavas. He said, Yudhishthira Maharaj. Yudhishthira Maharaj. Yudhishthira Maharaj.

#### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=232
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana also felt alike but there was nothing they could do"
**Summary:** The Pandavas are surprised to receive a message from Dhritarashtra. The scene shifts to Duryodhana, Karna, Shakuni, and Dushasana, who are shocked and angry that Dhritarashtra reversed their victory. Dushasana expresses his frustration with Dhritarashtra's decision in multiple languages.

The Pandavas were surprised. They, after all that had happened, they just, just left the assembly and they had set out back to Indraprastha. But suddenly this messenger turned up and this messenger said that he was carrying a message for the Pandavas from Dhritarashtra. What was this message that Dhritarashtra had chosen to send to the Pandavas? Actually what had happened? After the game of dice and the Pandavas were returned their kingdom and whatever they had lost at the stakes and that they had left the hall and all the others who are who are present in the assembly had all left. Dhritarashtra had left, Bhishma had left, Drona had left and all the other people whoever were present in that assembly they had all left. Now after everyone had left Duryodhana, Karna, Shakuni and Dushasana were left and they were looking at each other because for them it was a great shock for all of them they had almost won everything but at the last moment Dhritarashtra's decision had turned the tables so they were still in shock of what had happened. Finally Dushasana spoke up that old man has gone senile we had the Pandavas and their kingdom in our grip and he left it off just like that. Buddha satya gaya hai jo hamare haath mein aaya tha nikal gaya. Moose nivala nikal gaya kai ketna du vaai ketala hai. Anda kelavan yedukuthaan ipdi pannano. A kelavan ayalak endu iti. Wap affah diyauta. Just jangal kaiyila vannadana ellan na sipitu. Kai bantit baayi kukki barili la muduka aapet illa halaput lie. Mandit kai k bandit baayi qaratu upmunche muduka illa halpa puta. So these were the feelings of Dushasana.

#### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, Shakuni, strategy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=374
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All along until now, he had been trying to avoid direct confrontation but to somehow through some cunning means, somehow cheat them of their kingdom and cheat them of their royal authority. These were his plans."
**Summary:** Duryodhana fears the Pandavas will seek war due to the insults they suffered. He has always avoided direct confrontation, preferring to cheat them. Shakuni proposes another dice game to regain what they lost, as they cannot afford a direct battle.

Duryodhana also felt alike but there was nothing they could do what to do. But if the matter had been just that, that the Pandavas had been returned their kingdom, it would not have been much of an issue. But the issue was that the Pandavas had been subjected to such great insults that they were not going to keep quiet. On some pretext or the other, Duryodhana was afraid they would take a war with the Kauravas. Then it would be very difficult because Duryodhana very well knew the prowess of the Pandavas. All along until now, he had been trying to avoid direct confrontation but to somehow through some cunning means, somehow cheat them of their kingdom and cheat them of their royal authority. These were his plans. So all along he had always avoided direct confrontation because Duryodhana knew that if he were to directly face the Pandavas in battle, he would not be able to succeed. Chakuni then spoke up and he told Duryodhana whatever happened, happened. Now you see, we cannot directly engage in a battle with the Pandavas. We cannot afford that. Somehow Hastinapura was not ours. We have got it in our hand. Now if we get into a battle with the Pandavas and lose Hastinapura also in our greed for Indraprastha, then we are left with, we are neither here nor there. So we have to think of something immediately.

#### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, persuasion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Then Chakuni said, I have got a plan by which we can still save the situation."
**Summary:** Chakuni suggests convincing the Pandavas to play another dice game. Duryodhana takes it upon himself to persuade Dhritarashtra, who is initially against it, fearing more conflict. Duryodhana argues that the Pandavas will inevitably attack, so a preemptive move is necessary.

Then Chakuni said, I have got a plan by which we can still save the situation. What is that? Chakuni said, somehow if we convince the Pandavas again for another game of dice, then somehow we can manage to win back whatever we want. Now neither Karna nor Duryodhana nor Dushyasana were in favor of a direct fight with the Pandavas because everybody knew they could not win. What Chakuni was proposing seemed to be the only solution. But then how to do it? How could the Pandavas again be brought back to another game of dice? Duryodhana took the task upon himself and went and met Dhritarashtra. He spoke to his father and he tried various ways to convince Dhritarashtra. Dhritarashtra was like, no, no, why? He was not in favor of it. He said, already one game of dice, so much bad blood has happened between the two of you. One more game of dice, there will be no stopping the, stopping a war between the two of you. So Dhritarashtra was not in favor. Finally Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra, my dear father, Dhritarashtra has gone out of control. Sooner or later the Pandavas will gather some, on some pretext or the other, they will gather an army and attack us. We have to preempt this. So the only preemptive fight that we can do is, you have to call them for another game of dice and we have to ensure that we win back the kingdom.

#### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, strategy, exile
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=580
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The Pandavas are now like snakes who have been trampled upon. Once we trample upon a snake's head, after that you cannot think of just walking away silently."
**Summary:** Duryodhana continues to pressure Dhritarashtra, arguing that the Pandavas are like trampled snakes who will inevitably attack. He proposes another dice game to send them into exile, allowing Duryodhana to consolidate power and make it impossible for them to challenge him upon their return.

Now, if we don't do that, we have to call them for another game of dice. If we don't do that, then what, what are we doing? We are inviting trouble. Because it is, the Pandavas are now, are now like snakes who have been trampled upon. Once we trample upon a snake's head, after that you cannot think of just walking away silently. The snake will follow you and it will attack you and it will try to bite you. The only way is, you have to defend yourself. If you have, you have to take protective measures. Duryodhana, hence, told Dhritarashtra that the only way to take a preemptive action is to prevent a battle from happening, which can only be done if the Pandavas are somehow sent out away from Indraprastha and we have to send them in exile somewhere. And for that, the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice. Not only this, Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra, let, let us engage them in a game of dice and let's take this time. Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra that the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice and let's take this time. And for that, the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice and let's take this time. And if the Pandavas are in exile for a sufficient period of time, Duryodhana said, with all the wealth in Indraprastha and with all the kings, I will get all the kings on my side and then my power will become really strong and the Pandavas, whenever they come back, they will not be able to face us. They will not be able to withstand the power of my army.

#### Mahabharata, Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, advice, family
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "On the other hand, please hand over the kingdom to the Pandavas and banish Duryodhana from the family."
**Summary:** Dhritarashtra finally agrees to another dice game. Gandhari, upon hearing the news, confronts Dhritarashtra, reminding him of Draupadi's suffering and questioning his judgment. She advises him to hand over the kingdom to the Pandavas and banish Duryodhana to prevent further conflict.

Dhritarashtra, although he was hesitant, finally, Duryodhana's persuasive words finally overcame his hesitancy. So finally, Dhritarashtra agreed. And Dhritarashtra summoned the servant and sent him, dispatched him to order Yudhishthira for another game of dice. When Dhritarashtra took this decision and this decision came to be that the message started spreading everywhere that Dhritarashtra has again called for one more game of dice, the news reached Gandhari's ears. She came running to her husband and she said, I heard that you have called for one more game of dice. I have heard what Draupadi had to go through in that assembly. It is unthinkable how Draupadi could have been subjected to such ignominy. And somehow you salvage the situation by returning everything. Now you have gone round the bend. You are again calling for another game of dice. Really, I mean, what do you think of yourself? Do you understand the consequences of your action? This is absolutely, what you are doing is absolutely wrong. On the other hand, Gandhari, the mother of Duryodhana, she advised Dhritarasva. On the other hand, please hand over the kingdom to the Pandavas and banish Duryodhana from the family. You send him away from Hastinapura. Don't keep him here. This was a mother's advice to Dhritarasva. Duryodhana's own mother, she could see the evilness in Duryodhana and the righteousness of the Pandavas and she was fully in favour of the Pandavas and she said better to reject Duryodhana and avoid any sort of ill-will and fighting within the family.

#### Mahabharata, Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, fate, duty

**Content Type:** story

**Summary:** Dhritarashtra dismisses Gandhari's advice, claiming he has already made his decision and will let fate take its course. The messenger reaches the Pandavas with Dhritarashtra's summons. Yudhishthira feels compelled to accept the challenge due to his duty as a Kshatriya and his respect for Dhritarashtra.

But Dhritarasva, what was Dhritarasva's response? He told Gandhari, you see Gandhari, whatever I have to do, I have done. Now, whatever happens. By the will of providence, let it happen. I cannot help it. And he walked off. Now, Gandhari, what could she do? She had tried her best. She went back. And finally, this messenger, despatched by Dhritarasva, had now reached the Pandavas and he was standing in front of Yudhishthira with the message from Dhritarasva. What was the message? The message was, the king is calling you, Yudhishthira Maharaja. The king is calling you, Yudhishthira Maharaja. For another game of dice. Yudhishthira looked at his brothers and he said, I am helpless. It is the duty of a Kshatriya never to say no to a challenge. Over and above this, Dhritarasva is in the position of my father. He is my elder. He is ordering I cannot disobey him. The other brothers in Draupadi, they also understood and fully well they empathized with Yudhishthira Maharaja's position. And they could understand that there was hardly anything else they could do in the face of following the rules of Dharma.

#### dharma, duty, ethics, selflessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=891
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As Kshatriyas, they had higher principles to follow. So, since they were Kshatriyas, since they were rulers, those principles took precedence over their personal welfare."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that there are different levels of dharma, from self-preservation to the welfare of family, society, and the environment. The Pandavas, as Kshatriyas, prioritize their duty and honor over their personal well-being, leading Yudhishthira to accept Dhritarashtra's invitation.

Actually, what we have to understand is, there are rules of Dharma for protecting oneself. And then there are higher rules. There are secondary principles and there are higher principles. At a very individual level, if I am very selfish, I take care of myself. Now, I am not living alone in this society. I am living in the midst of so many relatives, friends, servants, so many people are serving me, so many animals. And there are so many demigods, devatas who are providing me so much facilities and conveniences in nature. So, I am living in the midst of all of these things. So, I have duty towards my own, taking care of my own welfare. Then, I also have to think about higher principles. Welfare of my family. Then, welfare of my friends and relatives. Welfare of the entire humankind. Welfare of other animals. Welfare of this earth. Welfare of this environment. We have responsibility towards all these things. At an individual level, if you see, the Pandavas knew that this game of dice is not going to augur well for them. It will bring about their own downfall. But then, as Kshatriyas, they had to rise above the principles of taking care of one's own welfare. As Kshatriyas, they had higher principles to follow. So, since they were Kshatriyas, since they were rulers, those principles took precedence over their personal welfare. So, Yudhisthira could not refuse Dhritarashtra's order and he came back for the second game of dice.

#### Mahabharata, game of dice, Shakuni, deception, exile
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=999
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If Duryodhana and I lose in this game of dice, then we all Kauravas will leave our kingdom. And we will live in the forest in exile for 12 years."
**Summary:** The second dice game begins with the same assembly. Shakuni proposes the terms: if they lose, the Kauravas will go into exile for 12 years, followed by a year incognito; if they are discovered during that year, they repeat the cycle. The same conditions apply to the Pandavas if they lose, making it seem fair.

Now, when the second game of dice started, again, it was the same assembly. The Dhritarashtra was sitting at the head of the assembly surrounded by Bhishma, Drona, Vidura and opposite Yudhisthira Maharaj to play the game of dice was Duryodhana and on his behalf as usual, it was Shakuni casting the dice. The entire assembly was very unhappy. Everyone was censuring Dhritarashtra that why is he doing this? But then, Dhritarashtra was completely under the control, under the sway of his greedy son and he could not help it. Although he knew at his heart that what he was doing was completely wrong. Now, in the second game of dice, Shakuni spoke to Yudhisthira Maharaj and in the being audible to the entire audience, Shakuni put the conditions of the game. Shakuni informed Yudhisthira Maharaj and the rest of the assembly, whatever we played, we played and somehow we won, you lost, let us forget all those things. Now, it is a new game of dice and here the stakes are much higher. If Duryodhana and I lose in this game of dice, then we all Kauravas will leave our kingdom. And we will live in the forest in exile for 12 years. And in the 13th year, we will live in some city or village or town, incognito, in disguise, such that nobody can recognize us. And if the 13th year we are able to pass successfully without your detecting us, where we are and who we are, then in the 14th year, you will return the kingdom to us. Suppose, in the 13th year of exile, you recognize us, you manage to find us out and you are able to spot us, you are able to see through our disguise and identify us, then we will go back to another 12 years of forest exile and one more year of incognito, living in incognito. And then Shakuni said, if we lose, this is the condition. And if you lose, the same conditions will be applicable to you. Look at Shakuni's cunning nature. He did not immediately say that, if you lose, these are the conditions. He said, if we lose, these are the conditions and if you lose, then same conditions will be applicable to you. Making it appear very fair.

---
#### Mahabharata, game of dice, loss, exile, Duryodhana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=1167
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "I am bound by the instructions of my elder, which is Dhritarashtra, and therefore I unwillingly am accepting these conditions. Let the dice roll."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira, bound by duty, accepts the conditions. Shakuni casts the dice, and Yudhishthira loses again. The Pandavas are now sentenced to 12 years of forest exile and one year incognito. Duryodhana is thrilled, believing he can consolidate his power during their absence.

Now, Yudhisthira Maharaj looked at Dhritarashtra, that what he had to say. Dhritarashtra was silent. Then Yudhisthira Maharaj said, I am bound by the instructions of my elder, which is Dhritarashtra, and therefore I unwillingly am accepting these conditions. Let the dice roll. And then Shakuni cast the dice. And when the dice was cast, again, Yudhisthira Maharaj lost the game of dice and Shakuni won the game of dice. Now, it was decided that the Pandavas will have to go for twelve years in forest exile followed by one year of living incognito. As far as the Kauravas were concerned, they were thrilled. Duryodhana's heart's desire had been fulfilled. Shakuni's plan had worked and the Kauravas were now in control of the kingdom of the Pandavas, Indraprastha. And Duryodhana was gleeful and he was thinking, now with time, by the time the Pandavas have finished their twelve years of exile, I would have consolidated my power, they will not be able to come back and oppose me. And after that one year of living incognito, very tough, I will be able to somehow figure them out. That was Duryodhana's thinking.

#### Mahabharata, Bhima, anger, vows, family
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=1259
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "You are laughing at us now, remember I am going to, in the battle field, I am going to tear open your chest and drink your blood."
**Summary:** The Kauravas heap insults on the Pandavas, fueling Bhima's anger. Bhima reiterates his vows to avenge the insults. The Pandavas prepare for exile, giving up their royal attire. Vidura arranges for Kunti to stay with him and the Pandavas' sons to be sent to Drupada's and Krishna's care.

And Duryodhana and the Kauravas started heaping more insults on the Pandavas. They started making fun of them. And Bhima could not control his fury and he reiterated his vow. He reminded Dushyasana, you are laughing at us now, remember I am going to, in the battle field, I am going to tear open your chest and drink your blood. And Duryodhana, you also remember, very soon we will face each other in battle and that day I will break your thighs and I will see that you bite the dust. So, the second game of dice only increased the fire of anger in Bhima's heart. Not only Bhima's heart, all the Pandavas' heart. Now, the Pandavas got ready. They had to, since they had to live in the forest, they could not go there with all their jewelry and their royal clothes and all that. They had to give it up and take on deer skin and tree bark and remove all their ornaments and carrying just their weapons and this very simple attire, they set out for the forest. Vidura told the Pandavas that this condition of this game of dice was not for Kunti Devi. She is already very elderly and she would not be able to bear the rigors of twelve years in the forest. So, therefore, Vidura requested the Pandavas to entrust Kunti in his care and as long as the Pandavas would not return, Kunti could remain in his house. And as far as the sons of the Pandavas were concerned, they were sent to Drupada, to Draupadi's father's place where they could grow up and they could be trained in the various duties of the Kshatriyas.

#### Krishna, Dwaraka, Shalva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At that time Shalva promised to his friend Shishupala, that, this insult to you, I will take revenge of it."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the reason for Krishna's departure from Indraprastha, which was the attack on Dwaraka by Shalva, a friend of Shishupala, seeking revenge for Rukmini's abduction.

After the Rajasuya Ejna was performed by Yudhishthira Maharaj, we all had seen how Lord Krishna stayed back in Indraprastha for a few days. We have seen how Lord Krishna stayed back in Indraprastha for a few days. After staying there for a few days, Lord Krishna, along with his retinue, he returned back to Dwaraka. Actually, for Krishna to go back to Dwaraka from Indraprastha, there was a catalyst, there was a reason why Krishna had to go back to Dwaraka. Then Krishna was in Indraprastha with the Pandavas. Then Krishna was in Indraprastha with the Pandavas. Then Krishna was in Indraprastha with the Pandavas. Staying in their palace built by Mayadanava, suddenly they got a news. It was not a good news, it was a matter of great concern. And what was this news? A friend of Shishupala by the name Shalva, who was a king, had attacked Dwaraka. Now, why did Shalva attack Dwaraka? Why did Shalva attack Dwaraka? When Krishna kidnapped, abducted Rukmini upon her request from her marriage, which was arranged by her brother against her wishes with Shishupala, Shishupala had brought all of his friends for the wedding. King Shalva was one amongst those who had come to attend the wedding of Shishupala. But then Krishna took away Rukmini. And although King Shalva's kingdom had come to an end even though all of Shishupala's army tried to stop Krishna, Balarama, who had come there to support his younger brother, he very easily defeated all of them and drove them back. At that time Shalva promised to his friend Shishupala, that, this insult to you, I will take revenge of it.

#### Shiva, Benediction, Airship, Warfare

**Summary:** Shalva undertakes severe austerities to please Lord Shiva, who grants him an indestructible airship to attack Dwaraka, leading to a fierce battle.

I will get some benediction by worshiping Lord Shiva. Lord Shiva is in Machhtfatakana, which is one of the most crunches from Pandavas. Lord Shiva is in Machhtinese. Lord Shiva is in Machht hedge. God Chorge has 지금은 taken over K style farms. God Chorge has become Rywal masks. Arjun is giving a cheque for him as well. God Shalka, they have such pretty hats for him. That was for K 장ra by his name, isn't he? By which I will finish all the Yadavas and I will kill Krishna. Of course, who can kill Krishna? Who can touch Krishna? It is not possible. But then there are some foolish people, some atheists who think like that, who think that they are strong enough, powerful enough to attack Krishna and kill him. So, this Shalva, he undertook severe austerity and he meditated upon Lord Shiva. He would every day eat only a few handfuls of ash and he would execute other various austerities like this. And Lord Shiva is Ashutosh. He is pleased very quickly and he could not tolerate. Because Lord Shiva is a great Vaishnava, is a great devotee of Lord Krishna and all devotees of Lord Krishna are naturally very soft hearted and very compassionate. When Lord Shiva saw that this Shalva was eating just a handful of ash every day and subjecting himself to so much torture, Lord Shiva could not tolerate out of compassion and appeared before him and he asked Shalva, what do you want? Why are you taking so much austerity? Shalva told him, I want a ship, an airship, which cannot be destroyed by any human or any animal or by any man-made weapon and I want that this airship should be very fearsome to the Yadavas. Lord Shiva said, okay, is that all you want? Take it, take it. Somehow Lord Shiva wanted to stop his severe austerity. Now this Shalva, now with this airship, he took his whole army and he attacked Dwaraka. There were his foot soldiers and cavalry and all such other portions of his army which attacked Dwaraka on the ground, while Shalva himself, seated on his airship, made an air attack upon Dwaraka simultaneously. And this airship was not like our modern Rafals or any such small aircraft which only two or three people can sit and drive. This airship was practically like a whole city and it was equipped with various kinds of facilities and a mini army could be accommodated within that airship. Not only that, the airship had even the ability of night vision. Even if the entire environment was immersed in darkness, the pilot could still manage to navigate the airship. So with this, with this kind of arrangement, with his air power and with his ground force, Shalva attacked Dwaraka and they started tearing apart all the different multi-storied buildings that had been built there. They destroyed the parks. They started attacking various houses and they created a big bhangama in Dwaraka city. Now at that time, Lord Krishna was in Indraprastha. Now at that time, Lord Krishna was in Indraprastha after the Rajasuya Ejna.

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our whole dynasty, nobody has ever run away from the battlefield, and you have taken away me from the battlefield."
**Summary:** Krishna's sons, led by Pradyumna, defend Dwaraka against Shalva's attack, with Pradyumna initially being incapacitated but later returning to vanquish Shalva's commander, Dhiman.

In Dwaraka at that time, only Krishna's sons were present. Immediately, all the sons of Krishna headed by Pradyumna, they responded to this attack with their own army. And Pradyumna and the others, Pradyumna, Samba and Satyaki, all these people came out onto the battlefield and they gave a very good fight to the army of Shalva. And even though Shalva's army was huge, still the army of the Yadavas headed by Pradyumna was up to the task and they managed to beat them back. Now when this fight was going on in real earnest between these two parties, what happened? One of the commanders of Shalva by the name Dhiman. Dhiman. Dhiman. He suddenly, without warning, attacked Pradyumna because he was the foremost commander of the Yadava army. Dhiman attacked him with his mace and struck him a severe blow on his chest and Pradyumna reeled from the sudden force of that attack and he fell down unconscious on his chariot. The charioteer immediately seeing that Pradyumna had gone unconscious and any moment any of the enemy soldiers can capture him, immediately he drove the chariot away from the battlefield and he took him to a place of safety and then he sprinkled some water on him and then tried to massage his feet and slowly he brought Pradyumna back to consciousness. When Pradyumna recovered, he woke up and he started looking around and he saw, I am not on the battlefield and then he started chastising his chariot driver, what have you done? Our whole dynasty, nobody has ever run away from the battlefield, and you have taken away me from the battlefield. If I had died on the battlefield, at least my name would have been glorified that I died fighting. Now people, everyone will make fun of me. Look at this fellow who was born in the Yadava family, the only guy in the Yadava family who ran away from the battlefield. Not only my enemies will make fun of me, even my relatives will make fun of me. My brothers will make fun of me. My sisters-in-law, they will make all kinds of memes about me. So what will I do? I cannot tolerate this. Then the charioteer replied to Pradyumna, Oh my lord, I was simply doing my duty as a charioteer. When you fell unconscious, I had to take care of your safety and so I brought you to a safe place. This is perfectly according to the codes of warfare. So you will definitely not be considered as a coward who ran away from the battlefield because you are unconscious. You did not run away. I took you away from the battlefield. So please recuperate yourself and let us go back to the battlefield. Pradyumna immediately understood the situation and he was pacified and he drank some water, refreshed himself and he came back to the battlefield. And as soon as he came back to the battlefield, Pradyumna, first of all, he challenged Dhiman and there was an intense fight, battle between the two of them. Finally, Pradyumna overcame Dhiman and he managed to vanquish him and Dhiman fell down dead in the battlefield.

---
#### Krishna, Dwaraka, Shalva, Sudarsana Chakra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=693
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna immediately took his Sudarsana Chakra and beheaded Shalva."
**Summary:** Krishna returns to Dwaraka, engages Shalva in battle, destroys his airship, and ultimately beheads Shalva with the Sudarsana Chakra, leading to the Yadavas' victory.

Now while all this was going on, the news was taken by a messenger from Dwaraka to Lord Krishna in Indraprastha and that is how Krishna decided, Oh, this kind of battle has started. I have to immediately leave. Immediately leave for Dwaraka and immediately Krishna took permission from Yudhishthira Maharaj and he came back to Dwaraka. The moment Krishna reached the presence of Dwaraka, he saw this raging battle going on between his sons and headed by Pradyumna and the army of Shalva and this huge fearsome airship flying in the air. So Krishna immediately spoke to Balaram and told him, You better go inside the city and take care of the elders like Ugrasena and others and I will take care of this Shalva who has attacked us. Don't worry. And so Balarama went inside and then Krishna blew his Khans and then entered the battlefield. The moment the Yadava soldiers saw in the distance the Krishna's flag staff mounted on his chariot, with Garuda, with the symbol of Garuda, the flag of Krishna's chariot carrying the symbol of Garuda, everybody understood, Oh, Krishna has now come. And Krishna's arrow started wreaking havoc in Shalva's army. Now Shalva was no mean guy and his airship was also not ordinary. Sometimes this airship of Shalva would be seen in the sky. Sometimes it will be suddenly seen resting on some mountain. Sometimes it would be seen on the water and sometimes nobody could see it and the airship was moving at such high speed that it had generated a dust storm and everywhere nobody could actually see what was happening. When Krishna saw this, Krishna immediately started releasing his arrows and the power of his arrows was such that the whole airship of Shalva could not move. Shalva could not sustain its illusion that it was creating and Krishna's feather-tipped arrows, they struck Shalva's airship and that airship crashed into the water, into the ocean. But Shalva managed to break free. He managed to jump out of the airship and he landed on the battlefield and then he rushed towards Krishna and Krishna and Shalva engaged in quite a big fight and then Shalva was releasing his own arrows. Krishna was releasing his arrows. Finally, Krishna took his Sudarsana Chakra and beheaded Shalva and the moment this happened, the entire army of Shalva broke up because their leader was no more. Their morale was broken. The entire army's power was broken and they were scattered and thus the Yadavas gained victory over Shalva.

---
#### Dantavakra, Krishna, Viduratha, Avenging
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=885
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna immediately took his club which was called Kaumodaki and with that he gave one blow to Dantavakra. Dantavakra fell down there itself and died on the battlefield."
**Summary:** Following Shalva's death, Dantavakra, Krishna's cousin and friend of Shishupala, arrives to avenge Shalva, but is quickly killed by Krishna, followed by the demise of Dantavakra's brother, Viduratha.

But then it did not end there. Right after that came one more guy, Dantavakra and this Dantavakra, how did he came? He came walking with just one mace in his hand and who was this Dantavakra? Dantavakra was the son of Sutadeva, who was one of the sisters of Vasudeva. So this Dantavakra was another cousin brother of Krishna, just like Shishupala and this, although he was the cousin brother of Krishna, he was a friend of Shishupala and he also was inimical towards Krishna. With great anger, eyes red hot, this Dantavakra came on the battlefield and he said, Oh Krishna, you are my cousin brother. Actually, I am not supposed to fight with you. But then you have killed me. But then you have killed my friend Shalva. Shalva was a very powerful friend of mine. But somehow, little bit inattention here and there, you cheated him and you killed him. But I am going to avenge my friend. You are forcing me to come on the battlefield. I will see to that I will avenge my friend. Like this, Dantavakra came on the battlefield. So this is just like, there is a maddened elephant which is running amok and creating havoc and somebody tries to control the elephant by his words. Hey elephant, calm down, keep quiet. I will do this, I will do that. What your words can do to control a mad elephant? He cannot do anything. It is so powerful. So Dantavakra was thinking, simply by my words, I will subdue, I will control Krishna. But who can control Krishna? Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. So Krishna told Dantavakra, why are you simply talking? Come on, you take up your club and you avenge your friend Shalva. Let us see what you will do. So Dantavakra took his mace and powerfully struck Krishna on his shoulder. What was Krishna's response? Krishna did not bat an eyelid. He did not feel even like a leaf had struck him. Krishna immediately took his club which was called Kaumodaki and with that he gave one blow to Dantavakra. Dantavakra fell down there itself and died on the battlefield. So Shalva was killed and now Dantavakra who came to support him was also killed. But then demons are not one. They have many many supporters. So it didn't end there. Now came his brother Viduratha. Now this Viduratha was another demon like Dantavakra and Shichupala and he also came and attacked Krishna. Krishna simply used his Sudarsana Chakra and he finished Viduratha also and so like this all these demons who were against Krishna were all vanquished.

#### Krishna, Game of Dice, Paramatma, Purpose of Descent
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=1061
**Verse References:** BG 9.4, BG 10.20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna purposely did not intervene in that game of dice because it was Krishna's will that this would become the avenue for Krishna to fulfill the purpose of his descent to this material world, which was to annihilate all the demoniac kings."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while Krishna was engaged in battle, the Pandavas were being cheated in the game of dice, and Krishna, despite being able to intervene, allowed it to happen to fulfill his purpose of annihilating the demoniac kings.

And Dwaraka was again safe and like this Krishna entered the palace of Dwaraka. Actually while all this fight was ensuing between Krishna and Dantavakra and Shalva and Viduratha and all these people, what had happened? The Pandavas had been invited to Hastinapura and they had been cheated of their kingdom in the game of dice because Krishna was engaged in this battle so severely here, he was unable to attend. Otherwise Krishna would have rushed to the rescue of the Pandavas, but still Krishna being the supreme personality of Godhead, he is present as Paramatma in everybody's heart. Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita that Sarvasya Chaham Hrithi Sannivishtha and seated as Paramatma in everyone's heart. Although Krishna was standing there in the battlefield in front of Dwaraka, fighting with Shalva and Dantavakra and all these people, simultaneously Krishna protected the honor of Draupadi in that assembly. So Krishna could have stopped that game of dice, the game of dice also, but then Krishna did not do so because it was Krishna's will that this would become the avenue for Krishna to fulfill the purpose of his descent to this material world, which was to annihilate all the demoniac kings who were simply engaged in economic development and military aggrandizement all over the world. If you may recall that was the original purpose for Krishna's descent and Krishna wanted to assemble all these demoniac kings in one place and finish them all at once rather than going after them one by one, one by one, one by one. So in order to give effect to that purpose of Krishna's, Krishna purposely did not intervene in that game of dice.

---
#### Narada Muni, Devotee, Love for Krishna, Pandavas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=1180
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "Despite all these calamities in their lives, the Pandavas, not once have they complained that, O Krishna, we are your devotees, why is this happening to us? On the other hand, their love for you is only increasing day by day."
**Summary:** Narada Muni visits Krishna and sarcastically remarks on the Pandavas' suffering despite their devotion, highlighting their unwavering love for Krishna even in the face of adversity.

Now Narada Muni came to Dwaraka and he approached Krishna and Narada Muni is a great devotee of Krishna. He is a pure devotee. He is an unalloyed devotee of Krishna. So sometimes devotees of Krishna out of great loving affection joke with Krishna. Narada Muni sarcastically started speaking to Krishna. Krishna, the Pandavas are such great devotees of yours. They know nothing other than you. They have dedicated their life, their wealth, their everything to your lotus feet and you have given them such, such a wonderful reward, such a wonderful boon, benediction you have given them in return. So Krishna said, what is that that I have given? Because of your benediction, Pandavas are now bereft of their kingdom and Draupadi has been insulted in that assembly. She was tried to be disrobed. Dushyasana and Karna have heaped insult after insult upon them and very nicely they are now living in the forest. Narada was indirectly criticizing Krishna that you see, they are such unalloyed devotees, such great devotees of yours and look at the suffering they are having to undergo on account of that. But then, Narada Muni was not really criticizing Krishna like that. Further, what Narada Muni said was more important. Narada Muni said, but then Krishna, one thing I am seeing in the Pandavas. Despite all this, despite their kingdom being taken away by cheating, despite Draupadi being tried to be disrobed in that assembly, despite being insulted by sharp words of Karna and Dushyasana and Duryodhana, despite having lost their kingdom, despite having to live in the forest for twelve years, despite having to live in incognito for one year after that, despite all these things, despite all these calamities in their lives, the Pandavas, not once have they complained that, O Krishna, we are your devotees, why is this happening to us? On the other hand, their love for you is only increasing day by day. Every such incident, calamity that happens in their life, their love for you increases. This is incomprehensible. How this is happening. I cannot understand this. This is inexplicable. Nobody can explain the love that the Pandavas have for you. This is the nature of your devotees. Actually, the love that the Pandavas exhibited for Krishna, that unmotivated, unconditional love of the Pandavas for Krishna, such love that no matter what calamity happened in their life, their love for Krishna could never be questioned, it would never be affected. This is a common characteristic of all devotees of Krishna.

---
#### Devotee, Suffering, Krishna's Protection, Love for Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_a8pqvGd4mE?t=1374
**Verse References:** SB 1.2.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "A pure devotee always, whenever he comes across any difficulty in his life, in his heart he is not suffering any difficulty. He is only experiencing the bliss of Krishna's protection."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the nature of Krishna's devotees, using Prahlad Maharaj as an example, and explains that devotees see difficulties as a result of past karma, but also experience Krishna's protection and mercy, maintaining their love for Him.

You look at Prahlad Maharaj. How much difficulty he had to undergo by his own father's hands, but still, never once did he question, never once did he complain, O Krishna, why is this happening to me? Not once. So the Srimad Bhagavatam says that this is the characteristic of the devotees of Krishna. The devotees, their thinking is, whenever they come across any difficulty in their life is, they think that this difficulty is coming into my life because of my past sinful activities. So, somebody may say that okay, why don't the devotees go and pray to Krishna, please remove my difficulty. No, the devotees don't pray to Krishna to remove their difficulty. Why? Because the devotee thinks that according to the sinful activities that I have done in my past, I should have got a mountain of miseries, but Krishna has reduced me to a small mole hill and he has given. Look at Krishna's mercy. He has reduced my misery to such a small thing that very easily I am able to tolerate it. So, a devotee never asks Krishna to remove his misery. A pure devotee always, whenever he comes across any difficulty in his life, in his heart he is not suffering any difficulty. He is only experiencing the bliss of Krishna's protection. See, this much distress has come in my life. If Krishna was not there in my life, I would have had to suffer much, much more, but Krishna has sent it as only a small, simple problem that very easily can be crossed over. This is how devotees are always wrapped in their love for Krishna. So also the Pandavas. Now, Krishna, when he heard that in his absence so much injustice has happened with the Pandavas and that they are now living in the forest in exile, what did Krishna do? Please join us in our next episode of the Mahabharata to learn about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna.

#### devotion, suffering, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=117
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna immediately rushed to meet the Pandavas."
**Summary:** This segment describes Krishna's reaction to the Pandavas' plight, his immediate journey to meet them in the forest, and Draupadi's lamentation about her humiliation.

Lord Krishna being engaged in a battle with Shalva, Dantavakra, and Viduratha, he could not be present in the game of dice to support the Pandavas. So Krishna, when he got the news in Dvaraka that the game of dice had taken place, the Pandavas had been cheated of their kingdom, and they were now living in forest exile, Krishna immediately rushed to meet the Pandavas. The Pandavas, after losing the game of dice as per the conditions, they left Indraprastha, and Indraprastha was taken over by Duryodhana. The Pandavas retired to the forest. There they had built a small hermitage comprising simple patch cottages and mud flooring. They were living by wearing dresses made of tree bark. They would have to cook whatever jungle vegetables were available, and they were leading a very simple life like this. Krishna, when he came to meet the Pandavas, he was very much sorrowful to see the condition of the Pandavas, who are his great devotees. Draupadi fell at the lotus feet of Krishna and cried piteously, describing to him how she had been dragged by her hair by Dushasana into that assembly and that is rest estándar yadāntari assembly and tried to be disrobed. Of course, it's not that Krishna was unaware of all this.

---
#### divine promise, retribution, fate
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=235
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I promise all of you that all the sons of Dhritarashtra, they will have to meet you in the assembly in battle and in the battlefield, when they engage in combat with you, all their wives will become widows. This is my promise unto you."
**Summary:** Draupadi vents her grief to Krishna, who, in turn, promises retribution against the Kauravas, shortening their lifespan and guaranteeing their defeat in the future battle of Kurukshetra.

However, Draupadi wanted to vent her grief. She wanted to share her sorrow, and Krishna was her beloved Supreme Lord, and therefore she narrated the whole incident to him. And Draupadi explained to Krishna how her hair, which had been so glorified in that Rajasuya Yajna, that by that very hair Dushyasana caught her and dragged her into that assembly. When Krishna heard what all had gone on in that assembly, Krishna's anger rose, and the Supreme Lord Krishna then before the Pandavas, he said, 'I promise all of you that all the sons of Dhritarashtra, they will have to meet you in the assembly in battle and in the battlefield, when they engage in combat with you, all their wives will become widows. This is my promise unto you.' And this was promised by Krishna to the Pandavas. And that decision, by that decision of Krishna, the lifespan of all the Kauravas was shortened. The defeat of Kurukshetra was inevitable, and the defeat of Duryodhana and his brothers was guaranteed by the anger of Krishna upon the Kauravas.
