#### insult, consequences, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=339
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Whenever somebody insults a great personality, a great saint or a great devotee of the Supreme Lord, by that act, the person reduces his lifespan and spoils his reputation and fame."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that insulting great devotees reduces one's lifespan and reputation, highlighting Duryodhana's mistake in humiliating the Pandavas and Draupadi.

It is said in the Vedas that whenever somebody insults a great personality, a great saint or a great devotee of the Supreme Lord, by that act, the person reduces his lifespan and spoils his reputation and fame. Duryodhana had insulted, humiliated the Pandavas for no fault of theirs. He had humiliated Draupadi. All of them were great eternal devotees of the Supreme Lord. Duryodhana thought by doing this, he had achieved his goal of becoming a great saint. He had captured the kingdom of the Pandavas, which was Indraprastha, and he thought he had managed to cheat them. But in reality, what had happened, he had only reduced his lifespan and forever he would be vilified and his reputation was foiled.

---
#### piety, impiety, karma, austerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=405
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Piety and impiety, pious activities and sinful activities, the punya karma and the papa karma, they result in creating future happiness or suffering for us."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts Duryodhana's enjoyment of ill-gotten gains with the Pandavas' austere life in the forest, explaining how pious and sinful actions create future happiness or suffering, using the analogy of bank accounts.

At that point of time, Duryodhana could not understand this. So, while the Pandavas were fighting, Duryodhana could not understand this. But the Pandavas lived in austerity in the forest. Duryodhana enjoyed unrivaled sovereignty over the kingdom of both the Pandavas as well as that ruled over by Hastinapura. And he built up his alliances with various kings and over a period of time, he became very powerful and very opulent. He was enjoying the opulences of the kingdom which rightfully belongs to the Pandavas. The Pandavas, on the other hand, they spent their time in the forest with various rishis, sages, understanding from them the finer aspects of Dharma, engaging in various yajnas and austerities, tapasya, vrata and like this they increased their piety. Whereas Duryodhana by enjoying that kingdom which was not rightfully his, he was reducing his piety. Piety and impiety, pious activities and sinful activities, the punya karma and the papa karma, they result in creating future happiness or suffering for us. If we engage in pious activities, punya karma, then we are creating happiness for us in the future. If we engage in papa karma, sinful activities, we are creating distress for us in the future. So the papa karma and the punya karma are like two bank accounts. They keep building up according to our activity. When we engage in pious activities, our balance in the punya karma account keeps increasing. More balance we have in the Punya Karma, the greater happiness that we ensure for ourselves in the future life. And whenever we enjoy the result of that Punya or suffer the distress of some Papa Karma, the respective accounts of Punya and Papa start getting depleted. Therefore, while Duryodhana was enjoying the kingdom, he was consuming all of his past pious activities, whereas the Pandavas engaged in austerities and tapasya and engaging in various vows, engaged in various vratas and under and conducting various sacrifices, yajnas, they were building up their piety more and more.

---
#### duty, charity, divine grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=579
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One of the prime duties of a Kshatriya is to engage in yajna and dana."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira Maharaj, bound by his Kshatriya duties, desires to perform yajnas and give charity, but faces difficulties due to his exile. Dhaumya Rishi advises him to worship Suryadeva for a benediction.

Now Yudhishthira Maharaj, because he was a very righteous king, he could not deviate from the duties of a Kshatriya. One of the prime duties of a Kshatriya is to engage in yajna and dana. This yajna, for the performance of the yajna, Yudhishthira managed to engage all the rishis who were there, the sages who were there in the forest. But for giving dana, he was no more a king. He was, he did not have access to various kinds of treasures to be able to give out gold and cows and such other things in charity. So at least the minimum that Yudhishthira Maharaj could do was to distribute yajna chishta, prasadam which had been offered in the yajna to Lord Vishnu. However, Yudhishthira Maharaj was facing difficulty even in that, because he was living in the forest and he did not have any sort of resources to be able to cook such good foodstuff and offer in large quantities to the rishis. So when Yudhishthira Maharaj was feeling very despondent because of this, Dhaumya Rishi, who was the Kula Guru, who was the family priest of the Pandavas, he advised Yudhishthira Maharaj to worship Suryadeva, the sun god and obtain a benediction from him by which he could be able to fulfill his desire of being able to distribute large quantities of yajna chishta or Vishnu's prasadam after conducting various sacrifices.

---
#### divine gift, prasadam, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=691
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whatever they would cook and serve from that vessel, the vessel would keep on supplying unlimited quantity of food until Draupadi takes her away."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira pleases Suryadeva and receives the Akshaya Patra, an inexhaustible vessel for providing food. He and Draupadi then use it to feed sages with Krishna prasadam, which frees them from sinful reactions.

Based on Dhaumya's instruction, Yudhishthira Maharaj engaged in tapasya, austerities and he managed to please Suryadeva. The sun god, having understood his desire, gifted him a vessel and he informed Yudhishthira Maharaj that whatever they would cook and serve from that vessel, the vessel would keep on supplying unlimited quantity of food until Draupadi takes her away. So, Yudhishthira Maharaj took her away. And she beckoned Yudhishthira Maharaj and told her, what if I swift away, why are you then? Why be slice up, what if I've Harma, Electoral attack, what happened in your head oretry make a arrives? Today's guest will be Yudhishthira Maharaj. Finally, Suryadeva was happy to meet Yudhishthira Maharaj and was surrounded by an eid festival inside the house in the room where he was staying. In the evening, the sun god, Yudhishthira Maharaj and the sun god, Yudhishthira Maharaj were gathered around... empty. And this vessel is the famous Akshaya Patra. Why is it called Akshaya Patra? Kshaya means to reduce. Akshaya means inexhaustible. So, because this vessel was inexhaustible, it could produce an unlimited quantity of food. Therefore, it was called Akshaya Patra. And now Yudhishthira Maharaj was very happy that now he could feed unlimited number of great exalted personalities, sages in the forest. And by doing this, he could continue his duties as a Kshatriya. So, like this, Yudhishthira Maharaj would conduct various sacrifices, and he would have Draupadi cook various food items to be offered to Lord Vishnu in that fire sacrifice. And he would have Draupadi cook various food items to be offered to Lord Vishnu in that fire sacrifice. And then he would distribute the Prasadam to all the sages. This is, this kind of activity is actually far beyond even Punya Karma, even pious activities. To feed a qualified person, like a Sadhu or a saintly person, is actually a Punya Karma, is a pious activity in itself. But then, Lord Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita that if we cook food and eat it without offering it to Krishna, we are eating verily only sin. On the other hand, if we cook food offered to Krishna and then partake of it, then we are freed from all sinful activities. So, Yudhishthira Maharaj, by feeding Vishnu's prasadam offered to Lord Vishnu in the sacrifices, in the Ejnas, he was distributing Krishna prasadam to all the sages. And so, that prasadam was transcendental and its effect was, whoever would honor that prasadam, they would become free from all the reactions to their past activities.

#### austerity, devotion, preparation

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story

**Key Quote:** "Unlike Duryodhana, who was simply engaged in sense gratification and reducing the accumulations, all that he simply kept consuming without engaging in any pious activities."
**Summary:** The Pandavas spend their time fruitfully in the forest, engaging in pious activities, unlike Duryodhana, who indulges in sense gratification. Arjuna seeks celestial weapons in preparation for the inevitable war.

So, Yudhishthira Maharaj and the Pandavas and Draupadi spent their time very fruitfully within the forest, continuing to engage in their past activities, unlike Duryodhana, who was simply engaged in sense gratification and reducing the accumulations, all that he simply kept consuming without engaging in any pious activities, without engaging in dana, tapasya, ejna, etc., which are actually the duties of a Kshatriya, of a king. Like this, time passed and once the Pandavas happened to reach a place known as Kshatriya. Like this, time passed and once the Pandavas happened to reach a place known as Kshatriya. Like this, time passed and once the Pandavas happened to reach a place known as Gandhamadana Hill. This Gandhamadana Hill is said to be beyond even the Kailasa Parvatha in the Himalayas. The Pandavas had come there because it was considered a very holy place which even the celestials visit. During this time, Arjuna also went seeking favors from various devatas to accumulate various kinds of celestial weapons because as advised by Lord Krishna, Arjuna would have to collect all these things which would be necessary for him to engage in the forthcoming war which all the Pandavas could sense that it was going to be inevitable although they did not desire such a war. The Pandavas continued to live in austerity. As I had mentioned earlier, the Pandavas continued to live in austerity. To pushes out the région, Heidi had to go into the parlour of огay and go to Kseti, etc., I had mentioned wearing a dress made out of tree barks, engaging in various kinds of austerities, tapasyas, vratas, etc., even on Gandhamadana hill.

#### desire, divine flower, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=1021
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is such a beautiful flower, I should offer this to my husband, Yudhisthira Maharaj, so that he can use it in his yajnas."
**Summary:** Draupadi finds a golden lotus with a thousand petals and desires to offer it to Yudhishthira for his yajnas. Bhima, seeing her desire, sets out to find more of these flowers.

Once, while the Pandavas were there in Gandhamadana hill, Draupadi, one day in the morning, she found a gentle breeze blowing, and in the breeze, she found one flower floating towards her, and this flower was no ordinary flower. It was a golden lotus with a thousand petals. Generally, lotus has a few petals. Sahasra, Dala, Kamala, even within this world, to get a lotus, which has a thousand petals, is very, very rare. Now, this was a Sahasra, Dala, Kamala. It was a thousand-petal lotus, and it was also golden. When Draupadi saw this, her mind became very much attracted by this flower, and she thought, 'This is such a beautiful flower, I should offer this to my husband, Yudhisthira Maharaj, so that he can use it in his yajnas.' Thinking like this, she picked up the flower. And then Bhima happened to be there, and she looked at Bhima, and she said, 'How I wish that we had more of these flowers, so that I could offer it to Yudhisthira Maharaj.' So, Bhima looked at Draupadi, and he said, 'My dear lady, if that is your wish, I will carry it out.' And so, what did Bhima do? Bhima looked at the direction in which the flower had come. The flower had come from a breeze which was flowing towards them, from the north-eastern direction. So, Bhima started walking through the forest in that path, in the direction from which the breeze was blowing.

#### strength, obstacles, mystery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nCVYyY97P54?t=1136
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhima very easily, very nonchalantly, he just slapped away lions and tigers and picked up elephants very nonchalantly and threw them away. He was so powerful."
**Summary:** Bhima journeys through the forest, effortlessly overcoming ferocious animals. He encounters a strange animal blocking his path, leading to a cliffhanger about its identity and their interaction.

So, it was a forest. So, naturally, there were many animals. There were lions, there were tigers, elephants, and all such kinds of animals were occupying the forest. So, when Bhima started walking through the forest, he saw that there were many animals. So, when Bhima started walking in the forest, naturally, sometimes some animals would come and attack him. But Bhima very easily, very nonchalantly, he just slapped away lions and tigers and picked up elephants very nonchalantly and threw them away. He was so powerful. And so, like this, Bhima made his way in the direction of where, from where the breeze was blowing. He came across a beautiful lake. And there were many beautiful flowers there. There also Bhima searched, maybe I can find this golden thousand petal lotus here. But he could not find it. So, everywhere he was searching like this and he was going forward. So, finally, after travelling quite a distance, he came to a place where he saw a strange animal lying on his path. What was this strange animal? And what was it doing there? And how did it get there? And how did Bhima deal with it? Because for Bhima, until then, he had come across many ferocious animals in the forest and he had dispatched all of them very easily. So, for Bhima, it was not a big deal. So, he was not afraid upon seeing this somewhat different looking and wonderful animal. So, but then Bhima understood when he looked at this animal that this was no ordinary animal. All the other animals were not ordinary animals. All the other animals were not ordinary animals. Till then, whom he had met were all normal forest animals. But this animal seemed a little different. So, what was this animal? And when Bhima looked at it, he carefully looked at it analyzing, is this really an animal or is it some Rakshasa in disguise waiting to pounce upon me, attack me and when I am a little neglectful, when I am not a little careful, maybe he wants to capture me. So, Bhima looked at it and said, he wants to capture me. Bhima thinking in these ways, he approached, he cautiously he approached the animal. Now who was this animal and what happened when Bhima approached it? Please join us in the next episode to know about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Mokam Karoti Vachalam Pangam Langhayate Girim Yath Kripata Mahamvande Sri Gurum Deena Saranam. Hare Krishna.

#### devotion, divine grace, avatars
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=206
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There was one particular devotee of Lord Ramachandra in Ayodhya. His devotion to Lord Ramachandra was so great that every day he would come to the court, have darshan of Lord Ramachandra and then go back and only then he would have his meals."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story from the time of Lord Ramachandra about a devotee whose intense devotion led the Lord to provide deities for him to worship in His absence.

To begin today's episode, we will take you to the time of Lord Ramachandra who ruled over the earth several millions of years ago during the age known as Treta Yuga. The current age in which we all live in is called Kali Yuga which lasts for 432,000 years out of which 5000 years have passed by. The age previous to this was called Dwapara Yuga which lasted for 864,000 years and the age previous to that was Treta Yuga which lasted for 1,296,000 years. It was during the time of Treta Yuga that Lord Ramachandra ruled over the world from his capital Ayodhya. Ayodhya is very close to the capital of the state of Uttar Pradesh which is Lucknow. Now when Lord Ramachandra was ruling over Ayodhya accompanied by his brothers, the citizens were all great devotees of Lord Ramachandra and Lord Ramachandra also ruled over them exactly like a very loving father would take care of his children. There were many devotees who rendered loving devotional service to Lord Ramachandra during that time. There was one particular devotee of Lord Ramachandra in Ayodhya. His devotion to Lord Ramachandra was so great that every day he would come to the court, have darshan of Lord Ramachandra and then go back and only then he would have his meals. Without having darshan of Lord Ramachandra, one would never have darshan of Lord Ramachandra, this is a very rare fact. And there was a man without having first seen the supreme Lord Ramachandra.

#### deity worship, lineage, divine arrangement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=336
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Lord Ramachandra instructed Lakshmana to bring out a set of deities of Sita and Rama. This deities of Sita and Rama had been worshipped by the sun god and it had been handed over by him to his son, Ikshwaku and like this, generation after generation, he was worshipped by the sun god and he was worshipped by the deities of Sita and Rama."
**Summary:** Lord Ramachandra, moved by the devotee's devotion, instructs Lakshmana to bring forth deities of Sita and Rama that had been worshipped in his dynasty, to give to the devotee.

Once Lord Ramachandra had to go out of Ayodhya for about five, six days on some important work. Now during this time this gentleman because he could not see Lord Ramachandra refused to eat anything. he would not even drink water. So like this, one day, two days, three days, days went by like this but this man would not relent from following his austerity, from following his vow. Lord Ramachandra, after finishing his work, when he returned to Ayodhya, then Lakshmana, his younger brother, introduced this gentleman, this devotee to Lord Ramachandra and he explained to him how this man was so devoted to Lord Ramachandra that while he was not there, he has not even taken a drop of water. So Lord Ramachandra was very much affected by this person's devotion and he thought that there may be some other instance like this where I may have to go out and if this person doesn't see me and he doesn't eat his food, it is not good. So Lord Ramachandra instructed Lakshmana to bring out a set of deities of Sita and Rama. This deities of Sita and Rama had been worshipped by the sun god and it had been handed over by him to his son, Ikshwaku and like this, generation after generation, he was worshipped by the sun god and he was worshipped by the deities of Sita and Rama. During Lord Ramachandra's time, when he was personally present, because of his personal presence, there was no need to worship those deities which had come, handed over from one king to the other in that dynasty in which Lord Ramachandra himself appeared. So when Lord Ramachandra was personally present, he said that there is no need for these deities right now and they were kept locked up in a closet. Lord Ramachandra recalled these deities and he instructed his younger brother, Lakshmana,

to bring those deities and Lord Ramachandra gave it to that devotee and he told him that if for some reason you are not able to see me then you can see these deities of mine which is as good as seeing me personally so then you will not be any seglude about anyone. #### devotion, spiritual happiness, divine love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=526
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What gives pleasure to the devotees is to be able to see the Lord. To be able to constantly chant the holy names of the Lord. To be able to constantly remember the past times of the Supreme Lord and to thus always remain in his association."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that true happiness for devotees lies in seeing the Lord, chanting His names, remembering His pastimes, and remaining in His association, not in material pleasures.

The Supreme Lord takes every care possible to keep his devotees always happy. And when we say the Lord wants to keep his devotees happy, the devotees are not happy by engaging in some activities of sense gratification. They are not happy by having good food to eat, good clothes to wear, having a nice luxurious residence. They are not interested in all these things. What gives pleasure to the devotees is to be able to see the Lord. To be able to constantly chant the holy names of the Lord. To be able to constantly remember the past times of the Supreme Lord and to thus always remain in his association. So this, just like this devotee, he because he was a great devotee of Lord Ramachandra, Lord Ramachandra knew that nothing would satisfy him except to be always in his presence. And so the Lord understanding what would keep him happy.

**Summary:** The narrative returns to Bhima's encounter with the monkey, describing the monkey's appearance and their initial conversation, where the monkey discourages Bhima from continuing his quest.

Gave him his own deities. So now we come back to the forest where Bhima had come across this strange animal. Bhima looked upon this animal with wonder. This animal was looked like an ordinary monkey. But then its whole body had a golden hue and it was faintly effulgent. And this monkey was lying on the ground. As if he was sleeping in the path. So Bhima, because his path was blocked, he spoke to the monkey and he told him, that oh monkey, you are lying in my path, please wake up and give way. This monkey slowly raised its head and looked at Bhima and he said, I am very old, it is not possible for me to get up and all that. Why have you come here? Why are you troubling me like this? This is my place, you seem to be some new person, why do you want to go this way? So Bhima introduced himself. He said, I am Bhima, one of the Pandavas and I am going on this path searching for a golden thousand petaled lotus. So this monkey looked at Bhima and he told him, see that lotus what you are talking about is not available in all these ordinary earthly planets. It is there in the heavenly planet. And this path which you are walking on leads you to the heavenly planet. And so ordinary people like you and all cannot go to the heavenly planet.

#### respect, Paramatma, offense
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=688
**Verse References:** BG 13.23
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Bhima told the monkey, you see, in your heart, Paramatma is there. So therefore, if I cross over your body, I will be crossing over Paramatma, which will be a great offense."
**Summary:** Bhima becomes upset with the monkey's obstruction. The monkey challenges Bhima to step over him, but Bhima refuses, citing the presence of Paramatma in every living being's heart, referencing Bhagavad Gita.

Now Bhima became very upset. He said, what do you think of yourself? Who are you to stop me? If you don't step aside on your own, I will pick you up and throw you aside. Now this monkey, he looked at him and he said, why are you doing this? Why are you troubling me? If you are so insistent that you want to go, you step over me and go, no, why I should get up and all this and you should throw me and all that. You just cross over me and you go. Then Bhima looked at the monkey and he said, you see, I cannot cross over you because as Lord Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, sarvasya caaham hrithi sannyavishtha, the Lord as Paramatma is seated in every living entity's heart. So Bhima knew this. That Krishna is present as Paramatma in every living entity's heart, even in the heart of animals, not just human beings. Even in the heart of the monkey also, Paramatma is present. So Bhima told the monkey, you see, in your heart, Paramatma is there. So therefore, if I cross over your body, I will be crossing over Paramatma, which will be a great offense. Therefore I will not commit that offense of crossing over you, you will have to either get up and give me way. Or I will pick you up and throw you aside. Then Bhima told the monkey, see, if I did not know this, that the Paramatma is there in your heart, I would have jumped across you very easily, just like Hanumanji jumped over the ocean for Lord Ramachandra.

**Summary:** The monkey asks Bhima to move his tail, but Bhima is unable to. Recognizing the monkey's extraordinary power, Bhima apologizes and asks the monkey to reveal his true identity.

Anyway that and all is separate. Now you get up and give way. So then the monkey said. See, it is very difficult for me to move. My tail is lying there. You, it is in one side. You remove my tail and then you walk across. So, then Bhima looked at him and he said, what monkey, you know, simply creating trouble for me. He wants me to pick up his tail and keep it aside. Okay, anyway, let me do it. He went and he used his left hand to pick up the tail of the monkey. Then Bhima is holding the monkey's tail and pulling. It is not moving. Bhima was little surprised. He thought very easily like, you know, monkey's tail, how difficult it is to move. So, then he thought very easily he will be able to move, but this is not moving. So, he was carrying his gada with him. He kept it aside and then he used his right hand also. With both hands, he started pulling. But this tail is not moving. So, Bhima realized, this is not some ordinary monkey and this is not some ordinary tail. So, he started putting his full effort. Because now, it was a matter. It was a matter of prestige for him. And he is using his full energy and power and he is perspiring, but this tail is not moving. Then Bhima stepped aside and he understood that this is not some ordinary monkey because Bhima's power is such that no ordinary person can face him and this ordinary monkey's tail is not able to lift. So, this is definitely some extraordinary being. So, Bhima stepped aside and then he apologized to the monkey. He said, it looks like you seem to be some celestial personality. You are not an ordinary monkey. I am sorry that I spoke to you in an insulting way. Please forgive me and please reveal yourself.

#### deity worship, lineage, devotion

**Key Quote:** "These are the deities of Lord Rama and Sita which were originally worshipped by Ikshwaku himself."
**Summary:** Hanuman hands over deities of Sita and Rama to Bhima, explaining their lineage from Ikshwaku to a devotee of Rama, then to Hanuman himself, and now to Bhima, eventually residing in the Udupi temple.

Then Hanumanji was carrying deities of Sita and Rama tied around his neck. So, he took those deities and he handed it over to Bhima. And he gave it to him and he said, these are the deities of Lord Rama and Sita which were originally worshipped by Ikshwaku himself. And during Lord Ramachandra Ramachandra's personal presence, these deities were not being worshipped, but the Lord handed it over to one devotee in his kingdom who, because that devotee had taken a vow not to eat anything until he would have darshan of the Supreme Lord. This is the same devotee which, who had gotten the deities of Sita and Rama from Lord Ramachandra himself. And when that devotee He, as long as he was alive, he was worshipping those deities of Rama and Sita. But when the time came for him to depart, to give up his body, at that time, he handed over these Sita and Rama deities to Hanumanji. And Hanumanji, from that time, was carrying it in a piece of cloth tied around his neck. And every day, he would ecstatically worship them. And now, Hanumanji handed over the same deities to Bhima. So, Bhima received them with great respect and he took those deities. As long as Bhima was present on this earth, Bhima kept those deities very carefully and he would worship them every day. Of course, it is said that today, those deities were further, after the time of the Pandavas, were handed down further to their descendants. And eventually, it reached the hands of Madhvacharya. And he Kept them in the Udupi temple. And it is said that even today, that Udupi temple, there are the deities of Sita and Rama, who are the same deities who were worshipped by Ikshwaku. And then, handed over by Rama to his devotee, who handed it over to Hanumanji, who handed it over to Bhima. And Bhima handed over to his descendants. And finally, it is now in the temple of Udupi.

#### exile, forest life, pilgrimage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=1175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So, like this, the Pandavas were engaging in various activities during their stay."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the Pandavas' activities during their exile, including their movements between different forests and their construction of hermitages.

So, like this, the Pandavas were engaging in various activities during their stay. they were going back and forth between two forests, the Kamyaka forest and the Dwaitavana forest. Dwaitavana forest is said to be modern day Dev Vandh. Dwaitavana was also known as Dev Vandh and now it came to be known as Dev Vandh which is near Saharanpur in Uttar Pradesh. Kamyakavana is said to be a forest which is close to Thar desert. It is further up northwards and the Dwaitavana is down south from Kamyakavana. So, the Pandavas were largely going between these two. Sometimes they would go to the Himalayas and like this they would keep going to various parts of the forest and they would build a hermitage there, live for some time and like this they were going. The Pandavas were spending their time.

#### anxiety, opulence, conflict
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aSURixov4CE?t=1248
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana on the other hand he was merrily spending his time enjoying life and oblivious of the fact that the forest exile was coming to a close instead of becoming anxious Duryodhana was becoming more and more cocksure of himself because he was building up his alliances and his opulence was increasing his military strength was increasing so he was under the false confidence that his power is increasing while the Pandavas are in exile when they come back who is going to support them so this was Duryodhana's thinking"
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Dhritarashtra's growing anxiety about the Pandavas' return with Duryodhana's increasing opulence and false confidence.

Now time went by and slowly the years passed by. One year, two years, three years, four years like this they entered the twelfth year in forest exile. In Hastinapura, Dhritarashtra was counting the days and as the time came closer for the Pandavas forest exile to get over, Dhritarashtra's blood pressure was going up. He was becoming more and more anxious because he knew the Pandavas were righteous and he was also repenting whatever had happened to the Pandavas. So, he knew that unless the Pandavas were given their rightful kingdom eventually there could be war and if war was there then definitely Duryodhana did not stand a chance. Duryodhana on the other hand he was merrily spending his time enjoying life and oblivious of the fact that the forest exile was coming to a close instead of becoming anxious Duryodhana was becoming more and more cocksure of himself because he was building up his alliances and his opulence was increasing his military strength was increasing so he was under the false confidence that his power is increasing while the Pandavas are in exile when they come back who is going to support them so this was Duryodhana's thinking so when the 12th year came then Karna came to Duryodhana and he said long time over we have been enjoying like this and Duryodhana has become very opulent you become very powerful all these things are there and you know by our by our plans by our scheme we managed to keep the Pandavas very far away so but then you know simply enjoying like this is doesn't full maja is not there we should go and show off to the Pandavas you know let us go and show them what we have got and how we are enjoying so you know a little bit let us finger them so Duryodhana heard this and he said ah very good idea let us do that so Duryodhana thought let me let us you know go and show off let me go up and go to the forest and show off my power and my opulence to the Pandavas

Thank you for watching. Om Adhyana Timran Dhasya Dhyanaanjana Salakaya Chaksharanmeetam Jena Tasmai Sriturave Namah. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamini Tinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Dauravani Pracharine Nirvishe Shashunyavadi Aschatya Deshata Dhinne. Mokam Karoti Vachalam Pangum Langhayate Girim Yad Krupata Maham Vande Sri Gurumdina Taranam. Hare Krishna. Welcome to today's episode of the Mahabharata.

---
#### austerity, piety, contrast
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=93
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In this way the Pandavas were going on accumulating pious credits."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, contrasting the Pandavas' austere life in exile, filled with yajnas and service, with Duryodhana's opulent lifestyle and depletion of pious credits.

In the last episode we have seen how the Pandavas being in the forest exile were engaged in executing various kinds of austerity. They were living in the forest, sometimes shuttling between Dwaitavana and Kamyakavana. They were living in simple thatched cottages with mud flooring. They were wearing dresses made out of tree bark. And they were constantly engaged in conducting yajna, sacrifices to please Lord Vishnu. And having obtained from the sun god, Suryadeva, a magical pot which could produce unlimited amount of food for feeding the guests. With that Draupadi would everyday cook and feed large number of saintly people who would come and attend the yajnas conducted by Yudhishthir Maharaj. In this way the Pandavas were going on accumulating pious credits. On the other hand Duryodhana was merely enjoying all the royal opulence and he was not engaged in conducting any fire sacrifice or yajnas. He was simply enjoying the power and opulence of the royal household and thus he was using up all his previous pious credits. Two contrasting things we were able to see.

---
#### envy, instigation, motivation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So that he knew later when the Pandavas would come to seek their kingdom back, he could very easily rebuff them on the strength of his alliances and his personal military strength."
**Summary:** Duryodhana, instigated by Karna, plans to visit Dwaitavana to flaunt his wealth and power to the exiled Pandavas, fueled by envy and a desire to assert his dominance.

And when the Pandavas entered the twelfth year of their forest exile, Duryodhana based on the instigation by Karna decided to go to Dwaitavana to show off his opulence. How much wealth he had acquired and with all the women folk, with all the resources, with some small army with him. He wanted to go there and show off to the Pandavas. He wanted to go there and show off to the Pandavas because look out how I am enjoying life and how you people are suffering. During these twelve years, Duryodhana had spent time not only in increasing the strength of his army but also in striking of alliances with various friendly kings and he was like this building up his army power. So that he knew later when the Pandavas would come to seek their kingdom back, he could very easily rebuff them on the strength of his alliances and his personal military strength. Now when Duryodhana heard this proposal from Karna that it is good to go and show up to the Pandavas and rub salt on their wounds, it appealed very much to Duryodhana because we all have seen how Duryodhana is very envious by nature and he liked to insult the Pandavas although there was no fault on their side.

---
#### deception, permission, insistence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Dhritarasva when he heard this, immediately he said, why are you going to Dwaitavana? The Pandavas are already there, if you go there and by chance both of you come to meet each other, I don't want another fight between the two of you."
**Summary:** Duryodhana seeks permission from his father, Dhritarasva, to visit Dwaitavana under the guise of inspecting cows, concealing his true intention to provoke the Pandavas. Dhritarasva initially objects, but Shakuni supports Duryodhana, and Dhritarasva relents with a warning.

Now for Duryodhana to go to Dwaitavana, an excuse was needed. So he went to his father Dhritarasva and he told him that the time has come to count, to take count of all the cows. And inspect them for their health and well-being whether the caretakers are taking good care of them or not. So I am planning to go with a small retinue, with a small portion, segment of my army I want to take and all the women folk and my wife and all those people I want to take them and go to the place where the cows are currently which happens to be Dwaitavana. Dhritarasva when he heard this, immediately he said, why are you going to Dwaitavana? The Pandavas are already there, if you go there and by chance both of you come to meet each other, I don't want another fight between the two of you. So why you have to go? Send some important minister or general or somebody like that, why do you have to personally go? But then Duryodhana was adamant, he wanted to go himself although he could not openly express it to Dhritarasva. Then Shakuni also supported Duryodhana and told Dhritarasva, why we will go to Dwaitavana? Why? All the Pandavas, they are already in forest exile, they are practically nobody now, why will we go to them? Forget it, we are going for our other purpose, we will keep away from them. So Dhritarasva finally relented and he said, okay, if you want to go, we are very insistent you go but don't trouble the Pandavas. That was Dhritarasva's viewpoint.

**Summary:** Duryodhana, accompanied by his entourage, travels to Dwaitavana, inspects the cows, and then seeks to set up camp near the Pandavas to provoke them with displays of revelry and opulence.

Now having got Dhritarasva's permission, Duryodhana took off with his small army, few horses, a few foot soldiers and some chariots like that, few means a few thousand, not that small also. And then Duryodhana was accompanied by Karna, Shakuni and Dushasana and his other brothers who all wanted to see the fun, how the Pandavas are going to be, you know, they are going to be insulted and they are going to be humiliated, that was the, they wanted to join in the fun. So they all went. Duryodhana with his retinue, he reached Vaithavana and which is said to be modern day Dev Vand in Uttar Pradesh and having reached there, he inspected all the cows, took count of them, asked about the health, he looked up the health records of all the cows and having considered all these things, having finished all his duties. Then he went. He sent some people to figure out in the Vaithavana where the Pandavas are and he told his people go and set up camp close to where they are in such a way that they can see us and we will have some revelry and some celebration and we will have some dancing, music and all those kind of things and that will rub the Pandavas this one ego, that was Duryodhana's this one.

**Summary:** Duryodhana's army, seeking a campsite near the Pandavas, encounters Gandharvas who forbid them from entering a certain area. The soldiers report back to Duryodhana.

So the few people, some few soldiers from his army, they went seeking out a place where they could set camp and also in such a way that it would be visible for the Pandavas. The Pandavas at that moment, Yudhishthira Maharaj was engaged in a fire sacrifice, a yajna was going on and he was along with a few other Rishis and Sages and Ramanas who were conducting the fire sacrifice, he was involved in that. The Pandavas of course were totally unaware of Duryodhana's impending visit. So this small group of army was going forward, they came across the place near a lake on the bank of the lake where the Pandavas were being seated. So they sought of setting camp there. Now in the meantime unknown to both the Pandavas and to Duryodhana what had happened. Some Gandharvas had come there, to that forest. Now these Gandharvas are celestial beings. They are not limited in their travel like we are if we have to go to some planet some other planet outside the earth planet let us say we want to go to the moon or the mars or something like that there are so many restrictions it's not easy to go there for human beings but the gandharvas are not because they are from the upper planets they are not limited in their travel like that they can very easily travel to whichever planet they want and if they so choose they can remain hidden to the view of the human beings or if they want they can reveal themselves also so in this case the gandharvas they stopped the team which duryodhana had sent for and they told them you cannot come to this part of the forest this belongs to us so you please go back so these small team members they because gandharvas they are celestial beings they went back to duryodhana and conveyed to him that like this we were stopped by the gandharvas and they refused to allow us to set camp there.

#### defeat, cowardice, celestial power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=702
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, a Kshatriya is never supposed to show his back in a battle. A Kshatriya is supposed to fight till the death. But this cowardly act from the side of Karna and Dushasana and others was not surprising because they were used to very boastful and ostentatious claims, although they did not have the real powers like the Pandavas."
**Summary:** Despite Karna and Duryodhana's prowess, the Gandharvas, being celestial beings, overpower Duryodhana's army. Shakuni, Karna, and Dushasana flee the battlefield, leaving Duryodhana to be captured.

Now, although Karna was a powerful archer, Duryodhana himself was a great warrior. Despite all this, the Gandharvas being celestial beings, gradually they drove back Duryodhana's army. Finally, what happened? Shakuni ran away from the battlefield when he saw that his life was in danger. So also, when Karna saw that his arrows were failing and he too was in a very precarious condition, he along with Dushasana and the other brothers of Duryodhana, they all ran away from the battlefield. Actually, a Kshatriya is never supposed to show his back in a battle. A Kshatriya is supposed to fight till the death. But this cowardly act from the side of Karna and Dushasana and others was not surprising because they were used to very boastful and ostentatious claims, although they did not have the real powers like the Pandavas. That is why they were always envious of the Pandavas because the Pandavas actually, even though they were very humble and very nice people, the Kauravas, Karna and Duryodhana and others knew that the Pandavas were always far more powerful than them and far more opulent than them. That was why Duryodhana and Karna and others were always envious of the Pandavas. Be that as it may, the whole Kaurava army was defeated, it was routed, practically almost all their army was destroyed, and Karna, Dushasana and others, Shakuni and others, ran away from the battlefield in a cowardly manner. Now, Duryodhana stood fighting there and finally, Duryodhana was captured by the Dandarvas. So, whatever little bit of Duryodhana's army was left, they ran away.

#### lamentation, seeking help, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=821
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So they consulted amongst themselves and they thought it wise to approach the Pandavas and seek their help because they were close at hand to go back to Hastinapura and to call Bhishma Dev and bring him back and Dronacharya and others was practically next to impossible."
**Summary:** Duryodhana's remaining soldiers, lamenting his capture, decide to seek help from the Pandavas, unaware of Duryodhana's malicious intentions.

The moment Duryodhana was captured, their morale was broken and they ran away from there. But still, they were lamenting that our master has been captured, now what to do? So they consulted amongst themselves and they thought it wise to approach the Pandavas and seek their help because they were close at hand to go back to Hastinapura and to call Bhishma Dev and bring him back and Dronacharya and others was practically next to impossible. Something had to be done right there, right then. So this remaining small group of people, the soldiers went and approached Yudhishthir Maharaj who was in the midst of performing the Ejna and they narrated the whole thing before him. Of course, this group of soldiers, they did not know Duryodhana's evil intention, his malified intention in coming there to actually humiliate the Pandavas. They were thinking he came here to count the cows and all those kind of things and after finishing all that, he wanted to just sit, camp there, relax a little bit and go back to Hastinapura. That was their understanding. The moment Pandavas heard that Duryodhana had come there and he had been captured by the Gandharvas, they immediately understood what was the background of this whole incident. And the moment they heard that Duryodhana had been captured, Bhima uttered, Bhishma uttered these words...

#### release, praise, pacification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=1291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana is still part of our kaurava clan and if you capture him and take him like this it will be a great human humiliation for our dynasty not only that his people have come and taken shelter of us like shatryas it is our duty to provide shelter to them and to rescue duryodhana we cannot refuse our shelter to people who have come and surrendered to us so i request you to please release duryodhana"
**Summary:** Yudhishthira, despite knowing Duryodhana's malicious intent, insists on his release to avoid dishonoring the Kaurava dynasty and to uphold the duty of protecting those who seek shelter. The Gandharva, praising Yudhishthira's virtue, releases Duryodhana.

So then the gandharva smilingly he praised yudhishthira maharaj for his great virtuous qualities and he released duryodhana and the gandharvas and went back to their planet very amicably the gandharvas parted with the pandavas and went back to their planet now yudhishthira maharaj he spoke to duryodhana pacified him with some good words and then he told duryodhana please don't do such kinds of bad things wrong things please don't try to engage in evil acts like this which are actually sinful and which will anyway bring you disrepute why do you want to do these kind of things so you please go home and take care of the kingdom we are we are not in interference in all that you go and take care like that yudhishthira maharaj released the duryodhana.

**Summary:** Duryodhana returns to his camp, deeply humiliated by the Pandavas' rescue. Overwhelmed by despondency, he contemplates suicide, alarming his brothers and Karna, who try to dissuade him.

Now duryodhana went back to his camp with a heavy heart he what he had set out to achieve he had achieved exactly the opposite he had set out to humiliate the pandavas on the other hand he got humiliated by the by the pandavas and the pandavas had to come to rescue him all his army his his brothers karna shakuni none of them could come to his health the pandavas had to come and help him and rescued him what greater humiliation can there be than this so duryodhana was very despondent and came back to his camp by then those people who had run away karna shakuni dushyasana and the others they came back to the camp and they saw oh duryodhana has come back they were all very relieved they were thinking we also are taking it serious we should continue all somehow ran away from the battlefield but Duryodhana kept on fighting we don't know what would have happened to him maybe the Gandharvas had killed him they were thinking like that but when they saw that Duryodhana escaped they were very happy but then they were seeing Duryodhana is very despondent then Duryodhana informed them like this the Gandharvas had captured him and it was the Pandavas who released him and so Duryodhana was so frustrated he was saying that i want to commit suicide i don't want to live anymore so then Dushyasana and others heard this they were alarmed Duryodhana is going to commit suicide then what will happen to all of us the kingdom will go to the Pandavas and we will all be thrown out so they all started speaking to Duryodhana and trying to pacify him trying to comment down even Karna sat with him and said no no why are Duryodhana why are you so despondent when you in action you In a Kshatriya's life, when he fights many battles, some battles he will win, some battles he will lose. So what is the big thing in this? Don't take this so much to heart. You please, you take it in your stride. And these are things which we should face in life, you know, like that. And Shakuni also, Dushyasana also, they all tried to motivate him and bring him back to his normal self.

#### plotting, sage, new plan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aFCwu8RBOmw?t=1478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Being the arrogant person he was, he again started, he started plotting how to humiliate the Pandavas."
**Summary:** Duryodhana, after initial despondency, is persuaded to abandon his suicidal thoughts and returns to Hastinapura. He soon reverts to his arrogant nature and begins plotting new ways to humiliate the Pandavas, spurred by the arrival of a sage in Hastinapura.

After much persuasion, finally Duryodhana relented from his decision to commit suicide. And gradually, with a heavy heart, they all came back to Hastinapura. Now, Duryodhana, although initially he was very much afflicted by this humiliation at the hands of the Pandavas, gradually, after a little while, he forgot the incident and came back to his normal self. And being the arrogant person he was, he again started, he started plotting how to humiliate the Pandavas. So, it so happened that one great sage happened to come to Hastinapura. And when Duryodhana heard that, about this sage's arrival in Hastinapura, immediately a plan started hatching in his mind. So what was the plan that Duryodhana had newly thought up of? And who was the sage? And what was the role he was going to play in Duryodhana's new plot? Please join us in the next episode of the Mahabharata to learn about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna. Jagat Guru Shri La Pravupada Ki Jai. Netai Gur Premanande Hari Hari Bol.

#### Duryodhana's envy, Krishna's supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=95
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In the last episode, we have seen how Duryodhana planned to go and humiliate the Pandavas in Vaithavana, but the tables were turned and he became insulted and returned."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode and introduces Duryodhana's new plot involving a sage, then transitions to discussing Lord Krishna's supreme position as the enjoyer of all sacrifices.

In the last episode, we have seen how Duryodhana planned to go and humiliate the Pandavas in Vaithavana, but the tables were turned and he became insulted and returned. But still, he did not give up his envious attitude towards the Pandavas and started hatching another plot to humiliate the Pandavas. And to assist him in his new plot, he decided to take the help of a sage who had come to Hastinapura. Who was this sage and what was this new scheme of Pandavas is what we will learn today. Before we start that, we have to understand one wonderful quality about Lord Krishna. Lord Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita that Lord Krishna is the He is the supreme Enjoyer of all the fire sacrifices which are mentioned in the Vedas.

#### Yajnas, Demigods, Cosmic Administration
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As Lord Krishna explains in the Bhagavad Gita, when the creation happened at the beginning of the creation, Brahmaji created human beings as well as Devatas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the purpose of Yajnas (fire sacrifices) and the roles of demigods (Devatas) in administering the universe, as described in the Bhagavad Gita.

What are Yajnas? What are these fire sacrifices? As Lord Krishna explains in the Bhagavad Gita, when the creation happened at the beginning of the creation, Brahmaji created human beings as well as Devatas. Devatas are the demigods and the demigods as we all know, they are said to be 33 crore in number. There is Indra, Chandra, Vayu, Durga, Lord Shiva, Ganesha, etc. So many personalities are there. Who are these Devatas or who are these demigods? Each of them is not God. They are all demigods, meaning, they are more powerful than human beings but they are not God himself. They are lower than God but higher than human beings. Therefore, they are called demigods. So, these demigods are interested with various administrative responsibilities in running this universe. And there are duties. Knowledge is one of the most important things in running this universe. Chanta has explained how we could keep up with the existence of these elements in eachevery present form. So this universe is running in a very systematic manner.

**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a government with various departments to explain the roles of demigods and the need for humans to express gratitude through Yajnas.

Of all these demigods now brahmaji when he created all these human beings also brahmaji's intention was the was that these devatas or demigods would administer the universe in such a way that the human beings would be able to lead their lives peacefully they would be able to have a harmonious life and the human beings would also be able to utilize their their human body to understand higher spiritual subject matter and liberate themselves permanently from this material world and go back to garden this is the whole scheme of things now this is just like the government is having many many departments the electricity department the water department the drainage department the roadways department the public works department so many departments of the government's new innumerable departments the government has now what is the intention of the government creating so many departments and having so many employees and bureaucrats and all that the idea is to administer the state or the country in a very proper manner so that the citizens can live peacefully they can execute their activities and they can live harmoniously and they can carry out their they can fulfill all their whatever their desires are this is the intention of the government now for the government to be able to do all this they need money where will they get the money from they tax the citizens the citizens have to pay income tax they have to pay gst they have to pay the electricity bill they have to pay the water bill so without the citizens paying the requisite fees the government cannot function

#### Yajnas, Gratitude, Cosmic Balance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In a similar way for the devatas for the demigods also to administer this universe they need such a government to be able to do all this so that the certain fees."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the government analogy, explaining that demigods need 'fees' in the form of Yajnas to maintain cosmic balance, and that disruptions in Yajna performance lead to natural disasters.

In a similar way for the devatas for the demigods also to administer this universe they need such a government to be able to do all this so that the certain fees. But what fees they need is not our paper currency. That is of no value to them. In fact, in India, rupee has value. The same rupees, if you go to the US and try to buy anything with that, it will not run. So, even within this earth, the currencies don't run from one country to another. So, how can we expect the devatas to take our money? It will not work. But some way, the human beings have to express their gratitude to the devatas, to the demigods for administering this universe so that everyone can peacefully, harmoniously execute their duties. So, how to do that? That also, Brahmaji, when he created this universe, he said at the beginning of creation that I will also create Yajnas. The human beings, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they come, when they conduct Yajnas, that will be the process by which they will pay their fees to the devatas, to the demigods. So, this is the whole arrangement. The human beings, they perform Yajnas, devatas are pleased, they will send ample amount of rain, sunshine and everything will be in proper order. The moment there is any discrepancy in this performance of these Yajnas, there will be natural disturbances. There will be floods, there will be earthquakes, there will be tsunamis, there will be volcanoes, there will be pandemics and such other things will take over.

#### Yajnas, Vishnu's Supremacy, Krishna's Ownership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=474
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, although the Vedas prescribe different Yajnas for different devatas, for Ganesha, there is one type of Yajna, for Lord Shiva, there is another set of Yajnas, for pleasing Lord Surya, the sun god, there are another set of Yajnas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while different Yajnas are performed for different demigods, Vishnu is the chief recipient of all Yajna offerings and the supreme controller of all universes.

So, although the Vedas prescribe different Yajnas for different devatas, for Ganesha, there is one type of Yajna, for Lord Shiva, there is another set of Yajnas, for pleasing Lord Surya, the sun god, there are another set of Yajnas. So, there are so many Yajnas, Pratas, etc. described. So, there are so many Yajnas, Pratas, etc. described. So, there are so many Yajnas, Pratas, etc. described. So, there are so many Yajnas, Pratas, etc. described. So, there are so many Yajnas, Pratas, etc. described for the different devatas. But no matter what Yajna you do for whichever devata it is, whether it is Lord Shiva, Lord Ganesha, Indra, Vayu, Surya, for whomever you do, the Yajna Pati, the chief person in every Yajna has to be Vishnu. Without that, even the devatas will not accept the offerings given in the Yajnas. So, Krishna has declared in the Bhagavad Gita, He is Sarvaloka Maheshwara, You can imagine, all the Edhnyas that we perform, he is the chief recipient of all the Edhnya offerings. Not only that, Sarva Loka Maheshwaram, he is the supreme controller of all the planets of all the universes. We are living in one universe, but ours is not the only universe. There are millions of universes, unlimited number of universes in the material manifestation. The Lord Vishnu or Krishna is the supreme controller of all of them. So Krishna is such a great personality. Everything belongs to him. All the universes belong to him. Everything that we have in this world belongs to him. All the gold, all the diamond, all the riches, all the natural resources, everything belongs to him. The whole planet itself belongs to him. The entire solar system, the Milky Way Galaxy, everything belongs to Krishna.

#### Krishna's Humility, Love and Devotion, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=584
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although he is such a great personality, he is not expecting something big from us."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Krishna's humility and low expectations, explaining that He values love and devotion above material offerings, citing the verse from the Bhagavad Gita about offering a leaf, flower, fruit, or water with love.

So Krishna is such a great person. He is such a great personality. But then Krishna, what is he expecting from all of us? Although he is such a great personality, he is not expecting something big from us. Normally if we have to go, if we have an appointment with a big person, let us say we have an appointment with the president of the country and we are going to meet him. Then how will we go? We will not go empty handed. We will take some gift or some present for him. Now what will we take as a gift or present? I tell you, you are going to this one. You come and ask me that you have got an appointment with the president of India. So what do you think you should take as a gift for the president? So I respond, why don't you take one bottle of mineral water? What will you say? You laugh at me. What are you talking? I am going to meet the president, not my some chaddi those living next door. Can I take a bottle of water and go to him? That's all. You know, the whole meeting will be a fiasco. Okay. So I have to take something valuable, something which will be of some value to him. So this will be your response. Now imagine you have got an appointment with the Supreme Lord Krishna, who is the proprietor of all the universe, the entire planetary system, everything belongs to him. You are going to meet him. So what gift can you take to him? So Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita what he expects from us. Krishna is ready to give his time to all of us, to meet us, to hear us, to hear all our prayers. Everything, Krishna is ready. So but then when we go to Krishna, we cannot go empty handed. We have to take something. So Krishna is telling in the Bhagavad Gita what all we can give him. Krishna is saying patram pushpam phalam toyam yomye bhaktya prayatchati tadaham bhakti upasrutam asnami prayatatmanah. Krishna says. Whatever bhakti upasrutam asnami prayatmanah. Bhakti upasrutam, whatever with love you give me, I am ready to accept. Okay, what should I offer you with love? You offer me a glass of water, a leaf, a fruit or a flower, I am happy with that. You can see Krishna is pleased even if you offer him one glass of water. Even that Krishna is looking forward.

---
#### Love and Affection, Krishna's Children, Duryodhana's Plot
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=757
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So similarly Krishna, we are all Krishna's children."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a president and his grandchild to illustrate Krishna's satisfaction with love and affection, then transitions back to the Mahabharata story and Duryodhana's plot to use Durvasa Muni against the Pandavas.

Krishna does not need water. What is? He is the owner of the whole universe. What is the need for him? Even if we take the most precious diamonds and jewels and go to Krishna, it is nothing, it is like garbage for him because everything belongs to him. What can we offer to him? But still Krishna is saying, what I see is your love and affection. So if you give me even a glass of water with love and affection, I will happily accept it. Just like if the president were to go to his home and his grandchild comes to him and offers him a glass of water with so much love and affection. So the president will feel very very grateful to his grandchild, oh so nice, my dear child you have brought me a glass of water, we will very happily he will drink the water. Actually president does not need water, what is one glass of water for him? But then when he sees his grandchild giving that glass of water with love and affection, he feels highly satisfied. So similarly Krishna, we are all Krishna's children. And Krishna is excited. He is happy. He is expecting our love and affection, nothing more, nothing less. So this is Krishna's nature, he is very humble and very down to earth and he has very little expectations from us. All that he is looking forward from us is our love and affection, that's all. This is Krishna's nature. So coming back to our original narration. Now it so happened that the sage Krishna, he was very happy. The sage who had come to Hastinapura was none other than the powerful Durvasamuni. Now Durvasamuni, as you all know, his nature is that he is very short tempered. Now the moment Duryodhana heard that Durvasamuni has come to Hastinapura, immediately something clicked in his mind. He thought, ah, now if somehow I can make Durvasamuni go to the Pandavas and the Pandavas are not able to take care of him. He thought, ah, now if somehow I can make Durvasamuni go to the Pandavas and the Pandavas are not able to take care of him. Then Durvasamuni will definitely get angry and he will curse them. So my military power cannot match the Pandavas' military power. But definitely a Brahmana's power, his power of his austerity, his tapasya, the Pandavas cannot counter. So this will be a good way to take revenge on the Pandavas.
