#### Duryodhana's Manipulation, Durvasa Muni, Patience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So thinking like this, Duryodhana immediately invited Durvasamuni to the palace and Durvasamuni, when he comes, he comes with all his disciples."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Duryodhana invites Durvasa Muni and his thousands of disciples to the palace, enduring his difficult behavior with patience in order to manipulate him later.

So thinking like this, Duryodhana immediately invited Durvasamuni to the palace and Durvasamuni, when he comes, he comes with all his disciples. And now Durvasamuni's disciples are not one or two or three or five or ten, not even hundred, not even a thousand, ten thousand disciples. So you can imagine, Duryodhana had to make all the arrangements to take care of all of them, take care of their accommodation, their food and whatever other requirements are there. And over and above that, Durvasa himself has to be taken care. So Duryodhana made all the necessary arrangements, made very good preparations. He took care of them. He took care of them. And he personally attended to Durvasamuni. And this Durvasamuni was a very difficult customer. Suddenly in the middle of the night, he will wake up and he will say, you know, I want to offer something to my Deity, so my Salagrama, so you please bring varieties of preparation. And immediately the people will run, they will cook something and then after 45 minutes, one hour, they will bring all their offerings. They will, Durvasamuni will say, no, no, I changed my mind. I don't want to offer anything. Take back everything. You know, like that. He was quite a, quite a difficult customer like that. So and no matter how, how much good take care, how much good care would be taken of him, he would find some fault or the other and he would get angry and he would criticize, he would chastise, all these things would go on. But Duryodhana, although he was such an arrogant person, keeping in mind that he had to take the help of Durvasamuni to, to, to humiliate his archenemy, the Pandavas, he did not do it. Duryodhana swallowed all the problems he had to face in front of Durvasamuni and very humbly he served him. He took good care of him. No complaints, whatever Durvasamuni demanded, everything he would run around and, and try to satisfy him in every possible way. This went on for some days. After a few days, then Durvasamuni was very satisfied with the way Duryodhana had served him. Of course, Duryodhana, Durvasamuni was a very simple sage. Although he was a very simple sage. He was a very simple sage. He had, he was a little short-tempered, but he was very good at heart and he would very easily get pleased also because Durvasamuni is a partial incarnation of Lord Shiva and Lord Shiva also has this same characteristic, he has the same trait. He gets pleased very quickly and he gets displeased also very quickly. So this Durvasamuni was very pleased with Duryodhana's way of hospitality and without any complaints. Without any, without any objections, Duryodhana had served him.

---
#### Manipulation, Blessings, Envy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=1035
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So finally, Durvasamuni was very pleased with him and he told Duryodhana, I am very happy with the way you have served me."
**Summary:** Durvasa Muni, pleased with Duryodhana's service, offers blessings. Duryodhana then subtly manipulates Durvasa to visit the Pandavas, masking his envy with a show of generosity.

So finally, Durvasamuni was very pleased with him and he told Duryodhana, I am very happy with the way you have served me. So I bless you. And like this, he gave his blessings and all that. Then Duryodhana replied to Durvasamuni. He wanted to manipulate Durvasamuni and, but at the same time, he could not openly do it also. Because Durvasamuni, after all, he is a good sage. And why would he get angry? Why would he get angry? Why would he get angry? He was angry on the Pandavas for no reason. There had to be some reason. So Duryodhana told Durvasamuni that Durvasamuni, I am very happy that you have blessed me. I am very fortunate to have received your, the dust of your feet on my kingdom, in my palace and to have received the opportunity to serve you. Now I have a humble request to you. My brothers, the Pandavas are in the forest. I want that you should give your blessings to them also. Give them also an opportunity to serve you. By this way, let them also get the fortune. Why I should alone be the recipient of the fortune of serving you? They should also get the opportunity. Durvasamuni was very happy when he heard this because the Pandavas are dear to everyone.

---
#### Deception, Timing, Duryodhana's Plan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=1175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He said, Durvasamuni, I have heard that Yudhishthir Maharaj is conducting various sacrifices, Ejnas and all that."
**Summary:** Duryodhana further manipulates Durvasa Muni by suggesting the best time to visit the Pandavas, ensuring they will be in a difficult situation to provide hospitality.

In fact, another name for Yudhishthira Maharaj was Ajatashatru, meaning one whose enemy had never been born. In other words, he would, by his actions, by his words and by his deeds, he would never create a situation where another person could become inimical towards him. Of course Duryodhana considered the Pandavas and Yudhishthira Maharaj his own enemies. That was because of his envy, not because Yudhishthira Maharaj had mistreated him at any point of time. So naturally because Yudhishthira Maharaj was such a great personality and he was always fixed in virtue. So all the rishis and sages held in this person to be in their arrangements. The Pandavas and Yudhishthira Maharaj always people in virtue. That was the common point that he held on. So, naturally when a person atmospheric, they thought that Yudhishthira Maharaj would be seen him in high esteem. So, Durvasamuni also held him in high esteem. So, now, when he heard from Duryodhana that the Pandavas are there in Dwaitavana and he can go and give them an opportunity to serve him and he can bless them, Durvasamuni was very happy. He said, oh yes, why not, definitely I will go and visit them. Now, Duryodhana started putting his plan into motion. He said, Durvasamuni, I have heard that Yudhishthir Maharaj is conducting various sacrifices, Ejnas and all that. So, if you go to him during the day time, in the first half of the day, he may be busy in his Ejnas and all that. So, he may not be able to receive you properly and take good care of you. So, after he finishes his Ejna and then he has fed all the people who have come to the Ejna and then all the Pandavas have had their food. Then, you can go to him. So, he will also be free. He can give you exclusive attention and he will be able to take care of you in a very nice manner. So, when Durvasamuni heard this from Duryodhana, he said, okay, what you are saying also makes sense. If I go with my 10,000 disciples, if I barge into his Ejna, it is not good, it is not correct also. So, after they have had their food and sent away all their other guests, then I will go. I can spend some good time personally also with them. So, I will do as you say. It is a good service.

---
#### Predicament, Hospitality, Akshaya Patra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=1234
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Poor Durvasamuni, he was, he had fallen into the trap of Duryodhana unknowingly."
**Summary:** Durvasa Muni unknowingly falls into Duryodhana's trap and arrives at the Pandavas' hermitage after they have finished their meal, creating a seemingly impossible situation for them to provide food for him and his disciples.

So, this is the suggestion you are given. Poor Durvasamuni, he was, he had fallen into the trap of Duryodhana unknowingly. Now, Durvasamuni took all his disciples, 10,000 disciples and he proceeded to Dwaitavana and he reached Dwaitavana. When he reached Dwaitavana, he reached at a time after lunch time when the Pandavas had completely finished all their fire sacrifices, all the guests had been fed, they themselves had been, had taken their food. And now, Durvasamuni, entered the ashrama, the hermitage where the Pandavas were living and he came and knocked on the door. When he knocked on the door, the Pandavas came. They opened the door and they saw, oh, Durvasamuni has come with all his disciples. They immediately offered their obeisances and welcomed him and Durvasamuni said, oh, my dear Pandavas, I come here. I heard that you are here. So, I come to meet all of you. So, I have come a long distance. I am a little hungry. I will go and take my bath. I will go and take my bath. I will go to give my food. I will take my food. I will take my bath and come. After that, I will have food at your place. Now the Pandavas said, okay, okay, Durvasamuni, we will wait for you to come back and Bhima took them and showed them a place close by where there was a water body where they could take bath and freshen up. So, Durvasamuni with all his 10,000 disciples went to the water body and they started taking bath. In the meantime, here what was happening? Yudhishthir Maharaj went back into the Kurukshetra. He went to the Kurukshetra. He was at the Kurukshetra, in the Kurukshetra, he was at the Kurukshetra. He was at the Kurukshetra. Yudhisthira Maharaj went back into the cottage and told Draupadi, Oh, this Durvasamuni has come with his 10,000 disciples. Now we have to feed him. He is hungry. So please bring out all your pots and from your Akshaya Patra, please start serving him. Because the Akshaya Patra can serve unlimited number of meals. Draupadi stood there stunned. She said, I cannot feed him now. Yudhisthira Maharaj asked her, why? What happened? She said, I already have my food. And once I have had my food, the Akshaya Patra will become empty. It is totally empty. Nothing is there here. So can you cook something now for 10,000 people? What? It is not like cooking for one or two people. Even for one or two people, if you have to cook, you need at least half an hour, one hour. And this Durvasamuni will finish all his bath and come back in 10-15 minutes. 10,000 people, where will I cook from? How will I cook? Not possible.

---
#### Dharma, Anxiety, Cliffhanger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aHy5ph1ghOE?t=1378
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Now the Pandavas were in great anxiety."
**Summary:** The Pandavas are in a state of anxiety, facing a seemingly impossible situation, and the episode ends on a cliffhanger, leaving the audience to wonder how they will resolve the crisis.

Now what to do? Now the Pandavas were in great anxiety. Now if they don't take care of Durvasamuni, they were not so much worried about his anger. They were more worried that the principles of Dharma will be broken. They would not be able to take care of a guest who had come to their place seeking food. So it is the duty of a Kshatriya and a Grihastha that whoever comes to their house, they should feed them amply. Even if the owner of the house, even if the host says, he has not eaten, even if he goes hungry, he should still take care of feeding the guest. This is the principle of Dharma. Now the Yudhishthira Maharaj was in great anxiety that now the rules of Dharma will be broken. Now what to do? Draupadi. Now Draupadi is a woman. So she also got into great anxiety. She was not able to tolerate. She started crying. Now what to do? The Pandavas were not able to understand what to do. In the meantime, the Durvasa Muni along with all his 10,000 disciples, they were all taking bath and they had finished their bath and they were about to come out of the water body and dry themselves and wear their cloth. They were on the verge of finishing their bath. So now what could the Pandavas do in this scenario? How could they save themselves from the wrath of Durvasa Muni, which was impending, which it looked like it was a mission impossible to be able to feed. How could the Pandavas save themselves from the wrath of Durvasa Muni with his 10,000 disciples? Now the Pandavas were really stuck. So what could they do now in this situation? Please join us in our next episode to know about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna. Jagatguru Srila Prabhupada Ki Jai. Nithayagur Premanande Hari Hari Bol.

### Mahabharata | Part 39 | Durvasa causes anxiety to the Pandavas | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### Mahabharata, Durvasa Muni, Pandavas, Duryodhana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Durvasamuni's intentions were not bad, but he had been manipulated by Duryodhana, unaware of Duryodhana's nefarious design."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, explaining how Durvasa Muni, manipulated by Duryodhana, arrived at the Pandavas' hermitage with 10,000 disciples, creating a challenging situation for them.

Hare Krishna and a warm welcome to all of you to today's episode of the Mahabharata. In the last episode, we have seen how Durvasamuni had come to the hermitage of the Pandavas with his 10,000 disciples at a time when Draupadi had finished her meal. And Durvasamuni came there at that time specifically because Duryodhana had manipulated him into thinking that that would be the right time to come to the Pandavas, when they would be free enough to receive him and his disciples well. Durvasamuni's intentions were not bad, but he had been manipulated by Duryodhana, unaware of Duryodhana's nefarious design. And when Durvasamuni came, he requested Yudhishthira Maharaj to keep lunch ready for all of them and that they would finish their noon bath and come back. And they went to the place, to a water body nearby where they could take bath. In the meantime, we saw how Draupadi and the Pandavas were all very anxious about what they could do now because to cook for 10,000 people in a matter of few minutes was practically impossible. So what did the Pandavas do? What happened to them? Would Durvasamuni's ire vent itself upon them? This is what we will see today.

#### Krishna, Narada Muni, Dwaraka, Opulence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=179
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Naradamuni thought, where will I find Krishna?"
**Summary:** The speaker begins a story about Narada Muni's visit to Dwaraka, where he observed Krishna's opulence and multiple palaces.

We will begin today's episode with a small incident which happened in Dwaraka. Once Naradamuni, wishing to have darshan of Lord Krishna, because Naradamuni is a great devotee of Lord Krishna, he came to Dwaraka. Now when he came to Dwaraka, he saw that Dwaraka city was very opulent. And he found that there were 16,108 palaces, which Krishna had built for his queens, who also equally were equal in number to the palaces. Naradamuni thought, where will I find Krishna? So he randomly entered into one of the palaces. When he entered into the palace, Krishna was with one of his queens, playing with his children and Krishna very warmly welcomed Naradamuni, asked him about his well-being, etc.

---
#### Yajna, Sinful Reactions, Varnasrama, Grahastha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=246
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every grahastha has to perform yajna to free himself of the sinful reactions which are accruing to him."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Narada Muni's continued observations of Krishna in different palaces, engaged in various activities like fire sacrifices, and explains the importance of yajnas for householders to counteract sinful reactions.

Palace and see what's happening there because Krishna is having 16,118, how is he managing all of them. We know how in this world today for one man is to manage one wife, he is, you know, his whole world will turn upside down. Now imagine not one, Krishna has sixteen thousand one hundred and eight wives to manage. So Narada Muni, being curious to know about this, he entered into another palace. There he saw Krishna was engaged in conducting some fire sacrifice, just like the yajnas are compulsory for every grahastha to be performed in the vedic varnasama system. So because while walking, while drinking water, while breathing, while doing all these activities, we unknowingly commit sinful activities by stepping upon insects like ants, etc., and we cause deaths to them which actually accrue sinful reactions to us. Not only that, in the course of our earning our wealth, there will be some lies that we may have to speak or they may be harmless lies just like you go to a nearby kirana shop to buy your groceries and when you go there you ask him, ask the dhukandar, the owner of the shop, you ask him what is the cost of the dal. He will tell you the dal is 95 rupees per kg. You will say why it is so high then he will say no for you at cost price I will give you 93 rupees. So you will also feel very happy, okay two rupees discount and you will buy it but you also know shopkeeper also knows he cannot give it at cost price if he starts selling his boots at cost price then he will heavily manage his family so to just to give you a mental satisfaction he says for you I'm giving it at cost price so this is a harmless life but nevertheless it is still alive like this. In earning our wealth we sometimes have to partake of sinful means to earn our livelihood. So in this many many ways we keep committing sins inadvertently although we may never intend to commit any sinful act therefore the veda state every grahastha has to perform yajna to free himself of his of the sinful reactions which are accruing to him.

#### Charity, Austerity, Krishna's Activities, Duality

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Narada Muni's visit to Krishna's palaces, where he witnesses Krishna engaged in various activities such as charity, advising his sons, and being personally present in each palace.

Not only he has to do dana and he has to do tapasya meaning he has to engage in fire sacrifice to please lord Vishnu he has to engage in activities of charity and he has to undertake some vrata some austerities. These are compulsory for everyone charity was an obligation it was not a choice. So Krishna when Narada Muni came to this particular palace of Krishna Krishna was engaged in doing some yajna so then Narada Muni left that palace and entered another palace and there he saw Krishna was giving away cows in charity to some qualified brahmanas and he went to another palace there he saw Krishna was engaged in advising his sons about the duality of this material nature and how we have to progress spiritually. Like this Narada Mani went from palace to palace in every palace he saw Krishna was personally present and engaged in some activity or the other. So when Narada Mani saw this he was astounded he was astounded because Krishna was personally present in each of those palaces individually these were not illusory forms they were Krishna himself.

#### Krishna's Supremacy, Omnipresence, Godhead
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** General public
**Key Quote:** "This incident demonstrates the supremacy of Krishna, that Krishna is not an ordinary person."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Krishna's unique ability to be present in multiple places simultaneously, highlighting his supremacy and divine nature.

They were not illusory forms, they were Krishna himself. We limited conditioned souls, we can be present in one location at one time. We cannot be in two places at the same time. But Krishna can be present in multiple places at the same time and still he remains the individual that he is. All of these 16,108 Krishnas who were there in each of those palaces, they were not illusory forms, they were not magical forms, they were actually Krishna himself. So this incident demonstrates the supremacy of Krishna, that Krishna is not an ordinary person. He is the supreme personality of Godhead who is not limited by time and space.

#### Draupadi's Prayer, Krishna's Response, Pandavas' Anxiety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=514
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna, Govinda, what to do? You only have to, you are my only protector. You only have to protect me."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions back to the story of the Pandavas and Durvasa Muni, explaining how Draupadi's desperate prayer to Krishna prompted his immediate appearance at their hermitage.

So Krishna once, he was in one of his palaces with Rukmini Devi. Rukmini Devi is the principal queen of Krishna. So he was there. He was in his personal room and he was resting on his bed along with Rukmini Devi. Suddenly, at that moment, Krishna got up and he rushed out of the room. Why did Krishna rush out of the room? Because what had happened at that moment, the Pandavas who were there in the forest, when they saw that they were in great danger of transgressing Dharma by not being able to feed Durvasamuni who had come to their hermitage seeking some food, Draupadi, not knowing what to do, she started calling out to Krishna. Krishna, Govinda, what to do? You only have to, you are my only protector. You only have to protect me. Now this prayer of Draupadi, she submitted her prayer with so much surrender and dependence on Krishna. That Krishna who was in Dwaraka with Rukmini Devi, he heard Draupadi's prayer and he immediately rushed out of his room and although he was in Dwaraka, Gujarat, and the Pandavas were in somewhere near Uttar Pradesh, but still Krishna, within a few seconds, he appeared at the hermitage of the Pandavas. And then Krishna came and knocked on the door. And then the Pandavas came and they opened the door and they saw, oh, Krishna is here. And they immediately welcomed Krishna because Krishna is their dear friend. So they immediately welcomed him, brought him into the room and now their anxiety doubled because until now there was only one guest to deal with, Durvasamuni and his 10,000 disciples. Now Krishna himself has come. So immediately the Pandavas made him sit comfortably and then Krishna said, Yes, Draupadi, you called me. That's why I came. Then Draupadi narrated to him what all had happened and like this Durvasamuni was waiting for his food and she had already had her food. So the Akshaya Patra which could produce unlimited food was now empty. How could she feed Durvasamuni?

---
#### Devotion, Krishna's Hunger, Love, Offerings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=668
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna being the Supreme Lord is the most lovable object for all living entities."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Krishna's arrival and his request for food, highlighting Draupadi's anxiety and her eventual offering of a small amount of leftover sabji with love and devotion.

Now Krishna said, Okay, we'll think about that later. We'll figure out some solution. I'm very hungry. I came all the way from Dwaraka. I've come a long way. I'm very hungry. Give me something to eat first. You know, in an empty stomach your mind doesn't work. So Krishna said, First you bring me something to eat. Then we'll think of some solution. So Draupadi immediately rushed back to the kitchen and then she started searching. Oh, Krishna is hungry. She forgot about Durvasamuni, 10,000 disciples, all that because Krishna was their Supreme Lord, her most lovable object. Krishna being the Supreme Lord is the most lovable object for all living entities. So Draupadi thought, you know, she went into greater anxiety. Oh, Krishna is hungry. I have to feed him something. Then she searched around. Nothing was there. And she came back to the room with an empty pot. Her eyes full of tears. Krishna, what to do? You're hungry. See, all the pots are empty. There is nothing. She was totally, she was very, very sad and disappointed that she could not feed Krishna. Krishna is hungry and not able to feed him. Then Krishna said, something will be there in the pot you have served. Something may be there. Then Draupadi again looked into the pot very carefully and she found little sabji was sticking in the pot. She wiped the bottom of the pot and took out and said, Krishna, this is all that is there. I am so sorry. This is all that I can offer you. With so much affection, with so much love, Draupadi offered it to Krishna.

---
#### Devotion, Krishna's Satisfaction, Love, Surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=787
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna becomes completely satisfied when his devotee offers him something with love and affection."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that Krishna values devotion and love above material offerings, explaining how Krishna's satisfaction with Draupadi's offering relieved the Pandavas.

And like we saw in the last session, what does Krishna expect from us? Even one glass of water, if it is offered with love, Krishna is ready to offer. On the other hand, if we offer the most valuable thing, the most precious jewel and all the wealth in the world, if we are offered the lotus feet of Krishna without any love for him, Krishna is not interested. So when Draupadi gave with so much love and affection, Krishna immediately took that little sabji which was left over and he tasted it and said, he tasted it and he ate it. So like Krishna said, like we discussed in the last episode also, Krishna becomes completely satisfied when his devotee offers him something with love and affection. So although it was a very small quantity of sabji that was there, because Draupadi offered it with full love and affection and surrender, Krishna ate that and he felt completely satisfied. He is satisfied not by the food, but by the devotion with which it is offered. And because of the pure devotion of Draupadi, Krishna said, I am so satisfied with the food that you have given me. So now the Pandavas were happy that Krishna is satisfied, at least we managed to satisfy Krishna. But then, the Durvasamuni's anxiety came back to them. And this Durvasamuni, how to feed Durvasamuni and his 10,000 disciples, that anxiety again was haunting them.

---
#### Krishna's Plan, Durvasa's Disciples, Fear, Embarrassment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=854
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Calling Durvasamuni, what will we do, call him and do? We have got nothing here."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates Krishna's plan to call Durvasa Muni and the disciples' unexpected change of heart, leading to their reluctance to return for lunch due to fear and potential embarrassment.

So then the Pandavas said, now Krishna, you only have to rescue us, please tell us what to do. Then Krishna told Sahadeva, okay, you go and call Durvasamuni. The Pandavas were looking at each other and said, calling Durvasamuni, what will we do, call him and do? We have got nothing here. So Krishna said, no, don't worry, go and call him, no, let him come, after that we will deal with him, I will tell you what to do. Okay, Sahadeva said, and he went to the edge of the place, where the water body was, he did not want to immediately go and interrupt, all Durvasamuni's disciples, they would all be taking bath, he did not want to interrupt in their privacy, so he stepped a little, a little distance away, and he called out, oh Durvasamuni, we are waiting for you, please come for your lunch, to Yudhishthira Maharaj's hermitage. Now Durvasamuni and his disciples, they got out of the water, they had finished their bath, and they had cleaned themselves, and they were, putting on their clothes, when they heard this call, then they started looking at each other's face, one of the disciples walked up to Durvasamuni, and he told him, Durvasamuni, my stomach is feeling very full, I am not hungry, so if I come with all of you, and all of you are eating, and I don't eat, it looks very bad, so all of you come, I will not come, then one by one, all the other disciples also started joining, that, no, no, no, I also don't want to come, my stomach is also full, my stomach is also full, basically every one of them, their stomach is full, none of them want to go, then Durvasamuni looked at them, and he was saying, I am also, my stomach is also full, I also don't feel like going, now we told Yudhishthir Maharaj, that we all have come, prepare the food, now he is calling us for food, and if we go there, and none of us eat something, nothing, if we don't eat anything, what will be, it will be a great insult to them, and God alone knows what will happen, we will have to face their anger on the earth, and who is going to face Bhima, if he gets angry, so what should we do,

#### Escape, Humiliation, Relief, Krishna's Rescue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=972
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Better we silently go away from here."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Durvasa Muni and his disciples, fearing humiliation and embarrassment, decide to secretly flee the hermitage, leading to the Pandavas' relief and Krishna's successful rescue.

So they were looking at each other, then they all consulted amongst themselves, and they decided now, if we go back to the Pandavas hermitage, we will have to, it will be very embarrassing, we will go there, and we will say, we told you to prepare food, but now we are not interested in taking, it looked very bad, it will be very humiliating, very embarrassing, and this is the forest, all sages are there, news will spread, and people will start making fun of us, so, what to do now, better we silently go away from here, so they immediately picked up all their clothes, and whatever little belongings they had, they were all sages, and engaged in various austerities, so they did not have much belongings, so they picked up whatever they had, silently, they ran away from the place, now Sahadeva is calling, calling, no reply is coming, so he thought, let me see what happened, and he goes and sees there, nothing is there, and he could see that, all the disciples had come there, and they had taken their bath, and all that, and he is looking here and there, and nobody, he could not find even one soul, so Sahadeva went back to the hermitage, and he told all the Pandavas, I went there to the water body, I am searching for Durvasamuni and his disciples, not able to find them anywhere, so then Krishna started smiling, and he said, okay, Durvasamuni must have left, maybe he had some other work, so the Pandavas started talking to him, and said, oh, maybe, Durvasamuni, you know, he keeps changing his decisions very frequently, not very stable in his decisions, suddenly he must have remembered some other work, and he must have gone away, anyway, what a relief for us, we were saved from the great disaster of, of inviting a sage, and not feeding him properly, and so like this, Krishna rescued the Pandavas from the danger of, of Durvasamuni's anger and curse, so,

---
#### Mystery, Hunger, Water, Appetite
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AgmGfl3x1TQ?t=1085
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What exactly caused the Durvasamuni and his disciples to lose their hunger?"
**Summary:** The speaker poses the question of why Durvasa Muni and his disciples lost their hunger after bathing, despite the usual effect of bathing increasing appetite, setting up the topic for the next episode.

Now the interesting point is, that, why did Durvasamuni and the, and his 10,000 disciples, who were actually hungry when they came to the hermitage, after taking their bath in that water body, why did they feel that their stomachs were full, because normally, in our experience, if we are hungry, and if we take a bath, our hunger will increase, our appetite will increase, in fact, if we are not feeling so hungry, also, we take a bath, our appetite will increase, and if you go and take bath in a water body, like a river, or stream, or something like that, fresh water body, your appetite will increase even more, so, the, in the case of Durvasamuni and his disciples, when they went and took bath, they should have, their hunger should have increased, their, the intensity of their hunger should have increased, but we saw, the rewards happened, and it's not that it happened with one or two people, all the 10,000 people, all the 10,000 of them, including Durvasamuni, felt the same thing, so, what could be the reason for this, this is not an ordinary incident, there is something really mysterious behind this, so, what exactly caused the, Durvasamuni and his disciples to lose their hunger, please join us in our next episode, to learn about it, thank you, Hare Krishna, Jagat Guru, Srila Prabhupada ki Jai, Mitai Gor, Premanande Hari, Haribo, Mahavohamari Mahava, Kirvan Dalai

#### abduction, retaliation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=100
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Being captivated by the beauty of Draupadi, he tried to forcibly abduct her while the Pandavas were not there in the forest at that time."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, focusing on Jayadratha's attempt to abduct Draupadi and the Pandavas' absence from the hermitage.

Last time we have seen how the ruler of Sindhu, the Sindhu Raja, Jaidratha, who was also known as Saindava, was the husband of Dushyala, the youngest sister of Mahabharata. He came across, he happened to be going to the kingdom of Shalva for an alliance, for a marriage and along the way he happened to pass through the forest where the Pandavas were staying and being captivated by the beauty of Draupadi, he tried to forcibly abduct her while the Pandavas were not there in the forest at that time. Now what happened to Draupadi? They, when Jaidratha came to their hermitage and he abducted Draupadi, the Pandavas were in the forest. They were not in the hermitage at that point of time. When the Pandavas returned to the hermitage, they found the maidservant of Draupadi, Dhatraika, lying on the floor and weeping inconsolably. The Pandavas quickly approached her and they understood that something was amiss. And when they looked around, they could not find Draupadi also. Then this maidservant, Dhatraika, informed the Pandavas how the king Jaidratha had come there and against her wishes, Draupadi was forcibly abducted by Jaidratha. Dhatraika also informed the Pandavas the direction in which Jaidratha, along with his retinue of the few soldiers, of that small bit of army that he had brought along with him, which direction they had gone into. That was also pointed out by Dhatraika.

---
#### pursuit, confrontation, arrogance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=214
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "These people are my five husbands, the Pandavas, whom even the Devatas cannot face in battle. Get ready to face your death."
**Summary:** The Pandavas, informed by Dhatraika and Dhaumya Rishi, pursue Jaidratha. Draupadi warns Jaidratha of the Pandavas' strength, but he remains arrogant and confident in his army.

Immediately, the Pandavas, they had their chariots and their horses and all that. They immediately set out in pursuit of Jaidratha. While pursuing Jaidratha, after a little while, they came across Dhaumya Rishi. This Dhaumya, he was the family priest of the Pandavas. And he also happened to be near the hermitage of the Pandavas, then Jaidratha had abducted Draupadi. And when Dhaumya saw Jaidratha kidnapping Draupadi, he vehemently protested and he beseeched Jaidratha not to do such an evil thing and to set Draupadi free. But this Jaidratha was an evil person. He did not heed anything of what Dhaumya said. After following Jaidratha for some distance and trying to convince him to release Draupadi, when Dhaumya saw that it was not possible for him, that Jaidratha was not listening to, so he was left behind by Jaidratha and he had gone forward. So this Dhaumya Rishi again related to the Pandavas that what had happened and how he was also a witness to Jaidratha forcibly abducting Draupadi and how he had tried his best to stop Jaidratha but he would not listen to him. And Dhaumya again showed the Pandavas in which direction Jaidratha had gone. So now the Pandavas very quickly pursued the direction in which Jaidratha had gone and very soon they caught up with him. And Jaidratha could see from the distance the Pandavas coming forward. So Jaidratha asked Draupadi that who are these people who are coming? And Draupadi replied that these people are my five husbands, the Pandavas, whom even the Devatas cannot face in battle. Get ready to face your death. So Jaidratha said, after all five of them, I got an army of a few thousand people with me, what they will be able to withstand? Let us see. One hand I will also see. Let us see.

#### battle, cowardice, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=353
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He is the husband of our sister Dushala. Therefore, do not kill him. Do not take his life. Only teach him a lesson. That's good enough."
**Summary:** The Pandavas easily defeat Jaidratha's army. Jaidratha's cowardice is highlighted as he tries to flee. Yudhishthira instructs Bhima and Arjuna to spare Jaidratha's life due to his marriage to their sister.

And Jaidratha was also very arrogant and he thought, you know, I will see what these people can do. And quickly when the Pandavas came, Jaidratha sent forth his army and the army of Jaidratha fell upon the Pandavas. A few thousand soldiers on one side and only the five Pandavas on the other. But the Pandavas were no ordinary people. Each of them was capable of fighting thousands of soldiers single-handedly. And so Yudhishthira Maharaj, he killed a few of the few hundred soldiers. Bhima killed a few other hundred soldiers. Arjuna also took care of a few hundred of them, Nakula also, Sahadeva also. And very quickly, in no time, Jaidratha saw that his whole army was completely vanquished. There was hardly anything left. And whatever little few people were surviving, they all ran away. And now Jaidratha is all alone. So Jaidratha realized, I had underestimated the strength of the Pandavas. So he thought, now, you know, Jaidratha, although he had on the spur of the moment, he had abducted Draupadi thinking that the Pandavas would be no match for him. At heart, actually, he was a very big coward. So when he saw that his whole army was finished, and he was all alone standing in front of these five Pandava brothers, he left Draupadi there. And he quickly took his chariot and started, you know, fleeing from the spot. So the Pandavas, now that they had dispersed the entire army, they quickly caught up with Draupadi. And the Pandavas inquired whether she was all right. And then Bhima and Arjuna told Yudhishthir Maharaj, that Maharaj, you please take Draupadi back safely to the hermitage. Nakula and Sahadeva will accompany you. We both will see to this Jaidratha. So then Yudhishthir Maharaj said, okay, I will take Draupadi with me. And then Yudhishthir Maharaj told Bhima, but please be careful. He is the husband of our sister Dushala. Therefore, do not kill him. Do not take his life. Only teach him a lesson. That's good enough.

#### humiliation, kshatriya dharma, ethics
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=490
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For a Kshatriya to set his eyes upon a married woman was a strict no-no. It was a forbidden thing."
**Summary:** Arjuna and Bhima pursue Jaidratha, who continues to flee. The speaker elaborates on the duties and codes of conduct for a Kshatriya, contrasting Jaidratha's actions with true Kshatriya Dharma.

So Arjuna and Bhima continued in pursuit of Jaidratha. And Yudhishthira, Nakula, Sahadeva along with Draupadi, they returned to the hermitage. Now by the time the Pandavas had got back Draupadi, inquired about her welfare and sent her back along with Yudhishthira, Nakula and Sahadeva to the hermitage, by then, at great speed, Jaidratha had already crossed couple of miles beyond the Pandavas. So Jaidratha had got a head start. Now how to catch up with him? So Arjuna sent forth a few arrows in the direction where Jaidratha had gone and those arrows quickly covered all the couple of miles which Jaidratha had already passed by and they went and killed Jaidratha's horses. So now Jaidratha was left without a chariot, so he started running away on foot. So when he was running on foot, Arjuna and Bhima on their chariots very quickly caught up with Jaidratha. And so they saw Jaidratha is running away. And behind him, Arjuna and Bhima are coming. And Bhima started calling out, Hey Jaidratha, where are you running? Stop! Wait! Stand there and fight with us! Jaidratha was in no mood to turn around. He simply kept running. Actually a Kshatriya is never supposed to run away from battle. And especially when he is challenged, he cannot turn away like that. Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, the character, real character of a real Kshatriya is Yuddhe Chaapalaayanam. In a battlefield, even if he is facing death, the Kshatriya, the true Kshatriya will never turn back. But Jaidratha was a Kshatriya in name only. He was actually a big coward. He was not qualified to be a Kshatriya. Kshatriya, the meaning of the word Kshatriya is one who protects his citizens from any kind of harm. He is called a Kshatriya. It was the duty of the Kshatriya to protect the citizens, bodily also and their honor also. A Kshatriya is always supposed to give special protection. Although generally the Kshatriya is supposed to give protection to the citizens, the Kshatriya has to give special protection to the children, to the women and to the elderly people and to the cows. These four were required to be given special protection by the Kshatriyas. Kshatriyas would sometimes abduct some girls with the intention of marrying them. But such girls would be unmarried girls and the intention in abducting them would be to marry them, not with any other evil intention. For a Kshatriya to set his eyes upon a married woman was a strict no-no. It was a forbidden thing. A Kshatriya could abduct an unmarried girl and marry her. But he could never set his eyes upon a woman he was already married. Draupadi was a well-married woman. She had children of her own. And Jayadratha was knowing this very well. And even though she expressed that she had no interest in Jayadratha, still Jayadratha abducted her. So this is completely against the codes of Kshatriya Dharma.

---
#### aggression, justice, anger, restraint
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=698
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By abducting Draupadi, Jayadratha had become an Atatain, an aggressor. And according to the codes of Dharma, he was liable to be killed."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Jaidratha's actions qualify him as an 'Atatain' (aggressor), making him liable to be killed according to Vedic codes. Bhima, enraged, physically assaults Jaidratha, but Arjuna reminds him of Yudhishthira's order to spare his life.

Now this Jayadratha, actually by trying to abduct the Pandavas wife, he had become an Atatain. In the Vedic terminology, there are kinds of Atatains or aggressors. A person who encroaches upon one's property. A person who administers poison. A person who abducts one's wife. A person who suddenly attacks one with deadly weapons without any prior notice. So all these kind of people are called Atatains or aggressors. And in the Vedas it is said that if somebody is an aggressor, is identified as an Atatain, then the person, the victim who is there, if he kills that Atatain, that aggressor, then there is no sin incurred in that. Even in the modern days we can say, we can see that the law will take a very lenient view if a man kills another man in self-defense. This is somewhat similar to that. But of course the Vedas were very specific and they had delineated the seven kinds of people in killing whom nobody could incur a sin and the king also could not punish such people. By abducting Draupadi, Jayadratha had become an Atatain, an aggressor. And according to the codes of Dharma, he was liable to be killed. So now Arjuna and Bhima, when they saw that Jayadratha is not turning around and he is not standing there for a battle, they pursued him and finally very quickly caught up with him. And when they caught up with him, Jayadratha was in no mood to fight. He was already shaking. All his limbs, his whole body was shivering. But then Bhima was very angry. He had to vent his anger. So he started knocking around Jayadratha like he was some piece of doll. So he started punching him, he started kicking him and after some time this Jayadratha, just by Bhima's use of fists and his knees, Jayadratha practically was almost dead. Then Arjuna quickly reminded Bhima, Bhima, please remember Yudhishthira Maharaj has told us not to kill him. He is after all the husband of our sister Dushyala. She should not become a widow. So please don't kill him. Bhima was reminded by Arjuna and then finally he pulled himself back. Bhima's anger was so much that he would not have stopped himself. But somehow Arjuna managed to remind Bhima and then Bhima also controlled, he restrained himself.

---
#### humiliation, submission, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=862
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We are going to spare your life provided you accept that from today you are a subordinate prince to Yudhishthira Maharaj and that you will always obey his commands."
**Summary:** Bhima shaves Jaidratha's head as a sign of humiliation. Jaidratha is forced to accept subordination to Yudhishthira. He begs for forgiveness and is eventually forgiven by Yudhishthira and Draupadi.

And then now you know Bhima's anger somehow he tried to come out. So what he did, he shaved off the head of Jayadratha except for a few clumps of hair here and there, which is a symbolic way of telling the world that this Kshatriya has been captured and humiliated by another Kshatriya. And then Arjuna and Bhima told him, that we are going to spare your life provided you accept that from today you are a subordinate prince to Yudhishthira Maharaj and that you will always obey his commands. Jayadratha had no other alternative. Either he had to lose his life or he had to agree. Jayadratha being the coward he was, he was in no mood to oppose whatever was being said to him. So he decided to kill him. Jayadratha had no other alternative. Either he had to lose his life or he had to oppose whatever was being said. He readily agreed. Then Bhima and Arjuna caught him, caught Jayadratha, took him back to the hermitage and presented him before Yudhishthira Maharaj and Draupadi. And there Jayadratha fell at the feet of Yudhishthira Maharaj and Draupadi, begged forgiveness from them and accepted to be a subordinate king to the Pandavas and he would always obey their command. Like this he accepted. Yudhishthira Maharaj being the very generous and noble person he was, very quickly he forgave Jayadratha and he advised him, please do not do such things. This does not behove you as a king. As a king you should behave in more noble ways and you should protect the citizens. You should not do such things. Like that in many ways nicely Yudhishthira Maharaj advised Jayadratha on the principles of Dharma and Draupadi also forgave him. Okay, some mistake he did. Anyway, after all he is Dushala's husband so she also forgave him.

#### revenge, austerity, benediction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T3pnrLnbmiM?t=984
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He wanted to take revenge."
**Summary:** Despite his outward submission, Jaidratha harbors resentment and seeks revenge. He decides to perform austerities to please Lord Shiva and obtain a boon to defeat the Pandavas.

And then the Pandavas released Jayadratha. Now Jayadratha slowly trudged back and as he was going back the humiliation he had suffered he was not able to digest it. Although in front of the Pandavas he had accepted that he was not, he would be subordinate to the Pandavas and he would obey their commands, at heart he had other plans. He wanted to take revenge. Now how to take revenge? Jayadratha was a very, he was a weakling in front of the Pandavas. So what to do now? So he thought the only way is that I have to perform some tapasya, some austerities and I have to please Mahadeva, Lord Shiva and get a benediction from him that I will be able to kill the Pandavas. Thinking like this, Jayadratha walked and went all the way to Gangotri. He went to Gangotri which is in Uttarakhand and which is in the Himalayas and which is, it is quite a arduous climb to reach to Gangotri. So he went all the way to Gangotri and there he meditated and did tapasya for a long period of time. And after some time Lord Shiva being pleased with his tapasya appeared before Jayadratha. Lord Shiva is Ashutoshya. He is very quickly pleased by people's austerities and Lord Shiva very quickly gives blessings and benedictions to his devotees. Lord Shiva does not think that his benedictions, whether they are harmful or they are for the good of the people. Whatever the people ask, Lord Shiva very quickly and very generously he gives. That's the nature of Lord Shiva. So Jayadratha by his tapasya he managed to please Lord Shiva and Lord Shiva appeared before Jayadratha. And he asked the Jayadratha that O Jayadratha I am pleased with your tapasya. It looks like you are expecting some boon from me. So please ask what you want to. So then Jayadratha folded his hands before Lord Shiva and he said my request is that please grant me a boon that one day I will be able to kill all the five Pandavas in battle. Now when Jayadratha made this request before Lord Shiva, what was Lord Shiva's response? Please join us in our next episode to know about this. Thank you for joining us today. Hare Krishna Jagat Guru Shri Ila Prabhupada Ki Jai Netai Gor Premanande Hari Hari Bol

### Mahabharata | Part 43 | The lake of death | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa.
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

#### boon, forest, water
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Today we will see what happened further in this incident."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with a recap of Jayadratha's boon from Lord Shiva and transitions into the Pandavas' search for firewood, leading to Nakula being sent to fetch water and not returning.

Arayakrishna, welcome back to all of you to today's episode. In the last episode, we have seen how Jayadratha managed to pacify or please, appease Lord Shiva, and he requested from him a benediction by which he could face all the five Pandavas in battle and vanquish them, which Lord Shiva said is beyond his powers because all the Pandavas were under the direct protection of Lord Krishna, who was the master of Lord Shiva himself. And therefore, Lord Shiva said, the maximum boon that I can grant you is that you can once hold the four Pandavas other than Arjuna at bay only one time in battle. And even that much was enough for Jayadratha and very satisfyingly, he went back to his kingdom awaiting the day when he would get an opportunity to face the Pandavas in battle and defeat them. In the meantime, we have seen how the Pandavas in search of the firewood which was stolen by a deer went into the forest, and even after pursuing the deer for a long period of time, they could not capture the deer and being very much aggrieved, that they could not recover those Arani firewood sticks which were meant for sacrifice by the sage who was living in the forest, they sat down exhausted in one place in the forest and Nakula was sent to fetch some water. But when he did not return after a reasonable amount of time, Yudhisthira Maharaj requested Sahadeva to go. Today we will see what happened further in this incident.

---
#### worry, mystery, disappearance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=211
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But something undesirable had happened."
**Summary:** Sahadeva, Arjuna, and Bhima also disappear after being sent to find the missing Nakula, causing Yudhisthira Maharaj increasing alarm and prompting him to investigate himself.

After Sahadeva went, the remaining three Pandavas, they were waiting and a few minutes elapsed and there was no sign of even Sahadeva. Now Yudhisthira Maharaj started becoming a little worried. Nakula did not come back initially. They were not so worried. They thought maybe he got engrossed in something, forgot to come back or something like that. But when Sahadeva also did not return, Yudhisthira Maharaj was a little worried and he told Arjuna that you please go and see what has happened. Why the two brothers have not returned? Arjuna immediately took his bow just to be well armed in case of any eventuality and then he went in the direction where Nakula and Sahadeva had gone into the forest in search of water. Yudhisthira and Bhima were waiting and after a few minutes elapsed, even Arjuna did not return and there was no sign of any signal from him. Now Yudhisthira Maharaj was getting really worried. He told Bhima that I am getting alarmed. Arjuna is such a mighty warrior. Nobody can stand in front of him in battle. Not even the Gandharvas. Not even the Devatas. And it is surprising that Arjuna has not returned. Better you go and see what has happened. Bhima immediately took up his Ghada and he also pursued in the three brothers who had gone earlier, Nakula, Sahadeva as well as Arjuna. And Yudhisthira Maharaj was waiting. Few minutes went by. Now even Bhima has not returned. Now Yudhisthira Maharaj was really alarmed. What could have gone wrong? It was highly unthinkable that anything, that any enemy could have attacked and captured Bhima and Arjuna. It was next to impossible. But something undesirable had happened. This much Yudhisthira Maharaj could be why.

---
#### lake, death, mystery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=344
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This was a real mystery."
**Summary:** Yudhisthira discovers his brothers dead near a beautiful lake, with no signs of a struggle or injury, deepening the mystery.

Therefore he himself set out on foot in the direction in which the rest of his four brothers had gone. After walking a short while in the forest, Yudhisthira Maharaj came across a clearing. And then he saw a little in the distance a beautiful lake with pristine water and filled with lotuses. And there were so many birds surrounding it. There were swans swimming in it. It was a very scenic picture that he was seeing in front of him. And Yudhisthira Maharaj looked around. He could not find anybody. So he went closer to the lake. And when he approached closer to the lake, he suddenly saw on the ground his four brothers lying still motionless. Yudhisthira Maharaj became very alarmed. And he ran towards the brothers. And he started shaking them, trying to wake them up. All of them were lying motionless with their eyes closed. Practically when Yudhisthira Maharaj tried waking them and tried shaking them and all that, he realized that they were not even breathing. Practically they were dead. Now Yudhisthira Maharaj, then he became very much surprised and astonished that who could have killed his brothers? That too Arjuna and Bhima? Impossible! He looked around and he did not find any signs of any battle, any mud getting, the earth all around being scattered or any such sort of disturbance to the trees or anything like that. Any weapons lying around, arrows lying around here and there. Nothing. He did not find anything. Everything was proper in its place. This made it more surprising. If some enemy had attacked and killed the Pandavas, there should have been some scuffle, some battle. It would have resulted in some destroying some of the foliage. Some weapons would have been lying around. Nothing. Absolutely nothing. And there were no marks of injury on any of the Pandavas. This was a real mystery.

---
#### thirst, warning, questions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=476
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you want to drink the water from this lake, you have to answer my questions."
**Summary:** As Yudhisthira approaches the lake to drink, a disembodied voice warns him not to drink without answering questions, revealing that his brothers died for disobeying this warning.

Yudhisthira Maharaj quickly got up and started looking around. Who could have done this? But he did not find anybody. Now what to do? He was being afflicted by thirst also. So Yudhisthira Maharaj thought, let me drink the water of this lake, refresh myself and then let me investigate what has gone on here. And thinking like this, Yudhisthira Maharaj approached the lake. The moment he was about to take the water and drink, he heard a voice, a disembodied voice. Disembodied because he could not see from where this voice is coming. And this voice, a deep resonant voice, informed Yudhisthira Maharaj. Yudhisthira Maharaj, I am the owner of this lake. Please do not drink the water of this lake without my permission. If you want to drink the water from this lake, you have to answer my questions. If you try to drink the water of this lake without answering my questions, then you will fall dead just like your brothers. I warned all your brothers not to drink the water without answering my questions. But they did not bother. They went ahead and drank the water without answering my questions. And now you look at their face. They have all fallen dead. If you really want to drink the water, please heed my advice, answer my questions. And if you are able to answer my questions correctly, I will grant your permission. Only then you can drink the water. Now Yudhisthira Maharaj looked around and he said, Who are you? How are you able to wield so much power that you are able to kill my brothers? How do I know who you are?

---
#### yaksha, power, questions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Okay, go ahead. Ask your questions. I am ready to answer them."
**Summary:** The voice reveals itself to be a Yaksha, a powerful being, who has taken the form of a bird. Yudhisthira, understanding the Yaksha's power, agrees to answer his questions.

Then Yudhisthira Maharaj saw on the banks of that lake, one bird standing there, a crane or some other bird like that. And the crane and that bird said, I am the person who is the owner of this lake. Yudhisthira Maharaj looked disbelievingly at that bird. And he said, I don't believe you are capable of doing this to my brothers. It's impossible. Please reveal yourself. Who are you? Then the bird replied to him, I am an Yaksha. And as a Yaksha, I have taken this form of the bird. So Yakshas are actually living beings who live on higher planets and who are very, very powerful. They are, normally the Yakshas are followers of Kubera. They are far more powerful than the human beings. Now, Yudhisthira Maharaj, understanding that this is a Yaksha, he realized Yakshas can possess superhuman powers. Maybe the Yaksha has really got some fantastic power by which he is able to kill my brothers. Then Yudhisthira Maharaj said, Okay, go ahead. Ask your questions. I am ready to answer them.

---
#### death, illusion, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=664
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What can be more surprising than this?"
**Summary:** The Yaksha asks Yudhisthira questions, including what is the most surprising thing in the world. Yudhisthira answers that it is the illusion that people will live forever despite seeing death all around them.

Then the Yaksha started asking Yudhisthira Maharaj varieties of questions. Questions on Dharma, questions on virtuosity, righteousness, about the duties of Kshatriyas and all kinds of questions the Yaksha asked Yudhisthira Maharaj. And Yudhisthira Maharaj very satisfactorily answered all of them from the perspective of the Vedas. Finally, the Yaksha told Yudhisthira Maharaj, I am very much satisfied by all your questions. Now, I have got final four questions for you. If you can answer these four also, I will bring back one of your brothers to life. Now, out of these four questions, two questions were very very important. What were these two questions? One question which this Yaksha asked Yudhisthira Maharaj was, Kim Ashtariam, what is the most amazing, surprising thing in this world? To which, Yudhisthira Maharaj replied, What can be more surprising than this? Than the fact that, day after day, living entities are dying, going to the abode of Yamaraj. This is a, this is an established fact. Every day you open the newspaper, there will be an obituary column. And even if that, that column is a very small representation of the number of people who die. Every day, thousands and lakhs of people die. Every day people are dying. That is not a surprising thing. But what is surprising is, Seshaha, Sthavaramicchanti. Seshaha, those who are surviving, those who are alive, Sthavaramicchanti, they are thinking, I will live forever. My grandfather died, my father died, my uncle died, my brothers died, my friends died, but I am not going to die. I will live forever. This is the thinking of people, misguided thinking of the people. Every day people die in accidents, people die out of diseases, people die out of old age, varieties of causes. But still, we, whenever somebody dies, we get shocked. Oh, this person died. I cannot believe it. Fact is, every one of us has to die. But, that is a fact all of us turn a blind eye to. And we always think everybody who is alive will remain alive forever. And I myself will remain alive forever. Which is a great illusion. And hence, Yudhishthir Maharaj said, this is the most surprising thing.

---
#### dharma, scripture, mahajanas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=840
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One has to follow in the footsteps of the Mahajanas."
**Summary:** The Yaksha asks what is the right path to follow. Yudhisthira explains that the true path cannot be ascertained through scriptures, logic, or approaching various sadhus, but by following the Mahajanas.

The other important question asked by the Yaksha was, Kimpanthaha, what is the right path for people to follow? To which, Yudhishthir Maharaj replied, We cannot ascertain what is the real path that one has to follow, what is the real goal of life, what is the real purpose of this human form of life. What is the greatest achievement to be aspired for in this human form of life. We cannot ascertain this simply by studying scriptures. Why? Because, There are many many scriptures and within the scriptures there are various kinds of contradictory conclusions. If you look at the world, there are many many religious scriptures. You have the Quran, you have the Bible, you have the Torah, you have the Vedas, etc. Now, if you look at the Vedas, within the Vedas itself, the different Puranas, the different Itihasas will give you different conclusions. The Upanishads will give you another kind of conclusion. The Vedanta Sutras will give you another kind of conclusion. So, which is the ultimate conclusion that I am supposed to follow? You cannot understand that simply by studying the scriptures. So, if I cannot understand this by scriptures, I will understand it by logic. Tar ko apratishta. By logic, it is not possible to establish what is the ultimate goal of life. Because, today, I am a good logician. I come up with some logic and everybody appreciates it and everybody praises me and everybody says, yes, wonderful logic. Tomorrow, somebody else will come up who is more intelligent than me and he will counter my logic with another logic of his own. So, logic, it can only lead to arguments. It cannot lead to a conclusion. Hence, logic also fails. Then, okay, I will approach some saintly person, some sadhu and I will try to understand from him. Even that does not work. Because, nasavrishir yasya matam na bhinnam. The definition of the word muni or rishi is a person who has some sort of opinion which is different from all the others. Nasavrishir, a person cannot be called a rishi or a muni until and unless, matam na bhinnam, until and unless he has a bhinnamata, he has a different opinion about the conclusion of life from all the other rishis and sages. Therefore, simply by approaching any and every sadhu also, it is not possible to understand what is the real conclusion of the Vedas, of the scriptures. Then, what is the kinpantha, what is the path to follow? How does a person understand? What is the right path to follow? Mahajanoyena gatha sapantaha. One has to follow in the footsteps of the Mahajanas. Who are these Mahajanas? These Mahajanas are numbered twelve.

---
#### sampradaya, guru, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=1102
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we want to understand the real conclusion of the Vedas, one has to be connected to one of these four sampradayas."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the twelve Mahajanas and the four bona fide sampradayas (Brahma, Rudra, Sri, and Kumara), emphasizing that true understanding of the Vedas comes only through connection to one of these lineages.

These twelve Mahajanas are there listed by Yama Raj in the Srimad Bhagavatam. So, these twelve Mahajanas, Yama Raj himself, Brahmaji, Narada Muni, Yasadev, Kapila Muni, and Prahalad Maharaj, Janaka Dev, Bhishma Dev, the four Kumaras, these are all the people who are considered the Mahajanas. These Mahajanas, these twelve Mahajanas, actually know what is the real path of Dharma. Nobody else can ascertain it without following their instructions. In other words, if we follow the path of Dharma in a gurusishya parampara which is stemming from one of these Mahajanas, then we will be able to understand what is the real goal of life. If we approach any gurusishya parampara which is not connected to one of these Mahajanas, then one cannot understand what is the real goal of life. For example, there are four sampradayas which are actually bona fide. One is stemming from Brahmaji himself which is called the Brahma sampradaya. And in this sampradaya comes Madhvacharya as well as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and the Gaudiya Vaishnava sampradaya is also coming from the Brahma sampradaya. Then there is the sampradaya stemming from Lord Shiva which is called the Rudra sampradaya in which Vishnu Swami is the prominent Acharya. Then there is the Sri sampradaya stemming from Lakshmi Devi in which Ramanuja Acharya is the prominent Acharya. Ramanuja Acharya, Yamuna Acharya and many others in that sampradaya. The fourth one is the Kumara sampradaya stemming from the four Kumaras, the Sanath Kumaras who are the four sons of Brahmaji. They are the initiators of another sampradaya. And in this sampradaya, the prominent Acharya is Nimbarka Swami. If we want to understand the real conclusion of the Vedas, one has to be connected to one of these four sampradayas. Otherwise, it is not possible to understand the real conclusion of the Vedas.

---
#### satisfaction, dharma, choice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5qdrfn8-qMY?t=1268
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I am very very satisfied with your answers."
**Summary:** The Yaksha is pleased with Yudhisthira's answers and offers to bring one of his brothers back to life, posing the question of which brother Yudhisthira will choose.

Now why is it like this? Because the Dharmasya Tatvam Nihitam Guhayam although all the Vedas are in black and white, Bhagavad Gita is in black and white, 700 shlokas, very simple Sanskrit, not very complicated, but still, people do not understand that the real conclusion of the Bhagavad Gita is that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead and the duty of every living entity is to surrender to Him as our Lord and Master. This conclusion misses many people, all those people who are not connected to the sampradaya. There are more than 700 editions of the Bhagavad Gita in English alone in the market. But if you read them, you will never get this conclusion that Krishna is the Supreme Person and we are all His eternally, His part and parcel servants. This conclusion you can get only by reading the Bhagavad Gita as it is by His divine grace, Srila Prabhupada. Therefore, Yudhisthira Maharaj said, even though the Vedas may be in black and white, even though a person may be a Sanskrit scholar, simply by reading the Vedas or simply going to any and every sadhu, simply by using one's logic and intelligence, one cannot understand the real conclusion of the Vedic scriptures. The real understanding if you want to get, then you have to approach one of these four Vaishnava sampradayas. Otherwise it is not possible. Now when Yudhisthira Maharaj finally gave this answer, what was the reaction of the Yaksha and what did he do? The Yaksha became very pleased. He was satisfied with Yudhisthira Maharaj's reply. And he told Yudhisthira Maharaj that I am very very satisfied with your answers. All of your answers are fully in accordance with Dharma, fully in accordance with righteousness as enjoined in the Vedic scriptures. And therefore, as per my promise, I will bring back to life any one of your brothers. You choose whom you want to bring back to life. Now whom did Yudhisthira Maharaj choose to bring back to life? Did he choose Bhima or did he choose Arjuna? Because both of them were the most powerful warriors. Very quickly there was going to be the situation where they would have to take back the kingdom for Duryodhana which was not going to be easy. He was not going to give it on his own. Most likely there would be a battle. And if Yudhisthira Maharaj had to win that battle, the support of Bhima and Arjuna was very essential. Without them he could not think of winning the war. So did he choose Bhima or Arjuna over and above the two brothers Nakula and Sahadeva? Furthermore, Nakula and Sahadeva were his step-brothers. Whereas Bhima and Arjuna were his own brothers. Whom did Yudhisthira Maharaj choose? Please join us in the next episode of the Mahabharata to know about this.

#### dharma, benevolence, equanimity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8X-VhBG8mRc?t=101
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One who protects Dharma will in turn be protected."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, focusing on Yudhishthira's choice to revive Nakula and the Yaksha's satisfaction with his adherence to dharma, leading to the revival of all his brothers.

A warm welcome to all of you to today's episode. In the last episode of the Mahabharata, we have seen how Yudhishthira managed to answer all the questions of the Yaksha at the lake of death very satisfactorily. We were told that once Yudhishthira Maharaja had answered all the questions which covered a wide range of topics from dharma to spirituality to material duties etc., after all that, the yaksha informed Yudhishthira Maharaja that he would bring one of his brothers back to life. He would want to bring back to life. The question was whether Yudhishthira would choose Bhima or Arjuna because they were the most powerful warriors and they were the two brothers on whom Yudhishthira Maharaj was entirely dependent upon to win a battle against Duryodhana if it arose, if it was required to get back their kingdom. Without these two, Yudhishthira Maharaj would be really crippled. When the Yaksha asked Yudhishthira Maharaj to choose one of his brothers to bring back to life, Yudhishthira Maharaj responded that please bring back Nakula to life. This was a very surprising thing. The Yaksha was taken aback. And he asked Yudhishthira Maharaj, Yudhishthira, that everyone knows that Bhima is very dear to you and he has the strength of thousands of elephants put together and Arjuna also is a warrior par excellence. Even the Devatas cannot stand in front of him in battle. Instead of choosing one of these two, the Yaksha asked Yudhishthira Maharaj, why have he chosen your step-brother Nakula? What is the reason? In response, Yudhishthira Maharaj replied, dharma yeva hato hanti dharmo rakshati rakshitah tasmad dharmam nathya jami. He said, Yudhishthira Maharaj replied to the Yaksha, that you see, whoever neglects dharma or does not follow the path of the Yaksha, he is not worthy of the Yaksha. Whoever does not follow the path of the Brahma, he will eventually be vanquished. Dharma, hato, hanti. A person who brings about the downfall of Dharma himself, will undergo destruction. On the other hand, dharma, rakshati rakshitah. One who protects Dharma will in turn be protected. Tasmad dharmam nathya jami. Therefore, I will not give up Dharma. I will go and save your life. the path of Dharma. Now, what does the following of Dharma have got to do with bringing back Nakula to life? Yudhishthir Maharaj explained to the Yaksha that his father had two wives Kunti and Madri and Yudhishthir Maharaj said, for me both Kunti and Madri are equal to me, both of them are my mothers and I see both of them equally. I am still alive who am Kunti's son. Now, if only one of my brothers is going to be brought back to life, then let one of the sons of Madri also live and that is why I have chosen Nakula and this is according to the rules of Dharma where kindness is supposed to be practiced by everyone. When Yudhishthir Maharaj replied like this, the Yaksha replied, I am very pleased by your benevolence and by your equanimity, your equal vision towards all your brothers. Therefore, I am bringing back all your brothers back to life and slowly all the four Pandavas who are lying almost dead on the ground next to Yudhishthir Maharaj.

#### divine intervention, boons, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8X-VhBG8mRc?t=379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I just wanted to test you."
**Summary:** The Yaksha reveals himself as Yamaraja, Yudhishthira's father, pleased with his son's adherence to dharma. He offers boons, and Yudhishthira first asks for the return of a Brahmana's Arani sticks.

Them opened their eyes and they got up as if they had just woken up from some deep slumber. And surprisingly enough, they were no longer feeling thirsty and they felt completely refreshed. When Yudhishthira Maharaj saw this, he looked at the Yaksha and he told him, My dear Yaksha, I don't believe you are a normal Yaksha. No normal Yaksha can do what you have done. First of all, to kill my brothers is impossible for any ordinary Yaksha to do. And after killing them, bringing them back to life. This is indeed a real miracle, which only some great Devata can do. So it is hard for me to believe that you are a simple Yaksha. Please, if you feel it appropriate, please kindly reveal yourself. The Yaksha then suddenly transformed into a Dami God, bedecked with a lot of golden ornaments and wearing silk clothes and looking very handsome. The demigodd revealed Himself to be the Yamaradj who is also known as Dharma Raja. And he responded to Yudhishthira that, My dear son, please know that, I am your father Yamaradj. And today, I have been very pleased with you, I just wanted to test you. And that is how I created this whole situation and I am very pleased with the way you have reacted. Therefore, being pleased with you, I want to give you some boon. So please ask me whatever you would like to ask. Immediately Yudhishthira Maharaj replied, O my Lord, if you want to bless me, kindly give us a boon that that Brahmana whose Arani sticks were taken away by the deer, his Yajna may not be stopped and he should be able to continue his Yajna. And then Yamaraja replied to Yudhishthira Maharaj, My dear son, please know that the deer was not an ordinary deer, but it was I who took the form of a deer and took away the Arani sticks to lure you to this place so that I could test all of you. Therefore, I am immediately granting you the boon that you have requested for that the Brahmana's Arani wood, the wood for the fire sacrifice, I will return.
